description: The National Food Security Mission (NFSM) is a centrally sponsored scheme launched in 2007 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, Government of India. It is a comprehensive program designed to increase production of critical food crops through technology dissemination, cluster demonstration projects, and improved agricultural practices. The mission focuses on rice, wheat, and pulses as primary crops, with expansion to coarse cereals and commercial crops. NFSM operates across multiple states and districts identified as production deficit areas. The scheme integrates scientific methods with traditional farming knowledge to achieve sustainable food security at both national and household levels. It provides targeted support to farmers through subsidized inputs, training programs, and market linkages to enhance productivity and profitability.
summary: NFSM is a production-focused agricultural scheme that enhances food crop yields through demonstrations, technology transfer, and farmer capacity building across major cereals and pulse-growing regions of India.
eligibility_criteria: Farmers engaged in cultivation of rice, wheat, pulses, coarse cereals, and commercial crops in identified blocks across participating states; Both marginal and large-holding farmers; Farmers willing to adopt improved varieties and practices; Members of Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) and cooperative societies; No upper income limit; Registration with agriculture department may be required at state level
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers (primary focus), Agricultural laborers, Women farmers, Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes, Landless agricultural workers, Youth in agriculture, Farmer groups and cooperatives, Agribusiness entrepreneurs, Agricultural service providers
benefits_provided: Subsidized high-quality seeds (50-75% subsidy) for improved varieties; Cost-share for certified seeds and planting material; Demonstration plots providing practical training at farmers' fields; Technical guidance from agriculture experts; Supply of bio-fertilizers and organic inputs at subsidized rates; Soil testing and soil health card services; Drip irrigation kits and water management equipment subsidy; Post-harvest management equipment access; Market linkage programs connecting farmers to buyers; Insurance coverage for crop losses; Producer group formation support; Free nutrient management training; Water harvesting structure subsidies
application_process: Registration: Visit block-level agriculture office or state agriculture department website; Identify block: Determine if your block falls under NFSM coverage area from official list; Complete application: Fill Form-A available at agriculture office or online portal; Submission: Submit application with required documents at block agriculture officer's office; Verification: Officials conduct field verification and background check (7-10 days); Selection: Beneficiaries selected based on criteria and fund availability; Approval: Once approved, notification issued and benefits activated; Disbursement: Subsidies credited directly to bank account or through e-payment; Demonstration plots: Farmers participating in demonstrations receive additional support; Monitoring: Regular field visits and progress reviews by extension officers
documents_required: Aadhar Card or any valid government ID; Land ownership certificate or lease deed; Land survey records (Patta, Khata, or FMB); Bank account details (active savings account); Photographs (passport-sized); Crop insurance policy details if applicable; Previous year's production records; Participatory crop demonstration attendance certificate (if applicable); Declaration form signed by beneficiary; Caste certificate (if applicable for reserved category)
keywords: food security, rice production, wheat cultivation, pulse farming, agricultural technology, yield enhancement, farmer training, production cluster, subsidy, food self-sufficiency

description: Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana (PKVY) is a central sector scheme launched in 2015 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to promote organic farming and sustainable agricultural practices in India. The scheme aims to incentivize farmers to adopt organic farming methods by providing financial support, training, and market linkages. PKVY operates as a component of the Soil Health Management (SHM) scheme and focuses on converting conventional farming areas into organic farming clusters. The scheme emphasizes community participation through Farmer Producer Groups (FPGs) and cluster formation approach, ensuring collective adoption of organic farming standards and practices. It provides support for organic certification, farm mechanization, and establishment of organic inputs production units. The scheme also facilitates the creation of value chains for organic produce and helps farmers access premium markets for their organic products.
summary: PKVY is a comprehensive organic farming promotion scheme that supports farmers in transitioning from conventional to organic agriculture through financial assistance, technical knowledge, certification support, and market access. The scheme encourages sustainable farming practices by forming farmer clusters and providing ecosystem-based solutions for soil fertility enhancement.
eligibility_criteria: Individual farmers or group of farmers (minimum 50 farmers per cluster) practicing agriculture; farmers willing to adopt certified organic farming practices; land holdings of any size; farmers registered with local agricultural department; willingness to participate in Farmer Producer Groups (FPGs); commitment to follow organic standards for minimum 3 years; no chemical pesticide or synthetic fertilizer use during conversion period
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers, landless agricultural laborers, women farmers, tribal communities, farmers in economically backward regions, farmers interested in sustainable agriculture, youth entering agriculture, farmer producer organizations, agricultural cooperatives, rural agricultural enterprises
benefits_provided: Financial assistance of ₹50,000 per hectare over 3 years for organic farming conversion; ₹8,000-₹10,000 per hectare annually for input costs (bio-fertilizers, bio-pesticides, organic manure); certification subsidy of ₹10,000 per hectare for obtaining organic certification; additional support for establishment of organic input production units; provision of training and capacity building programs; access to expert technical guidance for soil health management; facilitation of cluster marketing and branding; linkages with organic retailers and e-commerce platforms; preference in organic produce procurement by government institutions; field demonstrations and farmer exchange visits; access to organic seed and planting material
application_process: Step 1: Identify and form Farmer Producer Groups (FPGs) with minimum 50 farmers in a cluster; Step 2: Register cluster with district agriculture department and obtain cluster approval; Step 3: Conduct baseline survey and soil testing for all participating farmers; Step 4: Submit cluster application through eNAM (e-Mandi) portal or district agriculture office; Step 5: Verification of farmer details and eligibility criteria by agriculture department; Step 6: Approval and sanction of funds by state agricultural authority; Step 7: Release of first tranche (33%) of financial assistance; Step 8: Participate in mandatory training programs on organic farming; Step 9: Implement organic practices and maintain records; Step 10: Regular monitoring and third-party certification process; Step 11: Subsequent fund releases based on performance and compliance verification
documents_required: Land ownership/possession certificate or lease deed; Aadhar card and PAN card; bank account details (preferably operational account); agricultural certificate/registration; photographs of farm and farmer; soil testing report; declaration of non-use of chemical inputs for past 3 years (if claiming conversion credit); FPG membership certificate (when applying through cluster); identity and address proof; revenue records or land survey documents; details of cropping pattern and farm operations
keywords: Organic farming, sustainable agriculture, cluster farming, organic certification, soil health, bio-inputs, farmer producer groups, organic produce marketing, chemical-free farming, farming subsidies, agricultural sustainability, organic conversion, cluster approach, farmer training, organic seeds, soil testing, farm mechanization, agro-ecology, chemical pesticide reduction, organic value chain, agricultural subsidy scheme

description: Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) is a flagship income support scheme launched on February 24, 2019, by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, Government of India. The scheme aims to supplement agricultural income for small and marginal farmers, support agricultural activities, and facilitate the purchase of seeds, fertilizers, and other inputs necessary for cultivation. The scheme operates on a uniform basis across all states and union territories, ensuring equitable distribution of benefits. It covers all landholding farmers irrespective of the size of their holdings, with the government bearing 100% of the expenditure.
summary: PM-KISAN is a centrally sponsored scheme providing direct income support to all landholding farmer families across India. The scheme ensures a minimum income support of ₹6,000 per annum to eligible farmers, disbursed in three equal installments of ₹2,000 each through Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) to their registered bank accounts.
eligibility_criteria: All landholding farmer families (both male and female) are eligible. Eligible farmers include: (1) Small and marginal farmers with landholding up to 2 hectares; (2) Owners of cultivated land; (3) Agricultural laborers who are engaged in agricultural activities; (4) Tenant farmers and oral lessees recognized by the state government. Excluded categories: (1) All households with any member earning income above ₹10,000 per month from non-farm sources; (2) Persons who hold constitutional posts, retired government employees receiving pension above ₹10,000 per month, income tax assesses; (3) Doctors, engineers, lawyers, chartered accountants, and professionals with similar educational qualifications; (4) Farmers who own agricultural land in their name at any point during the financial year.
target_beneficiaries: Over 11.5 crore landholding farmer families across India including small farmers, marginal farmers, and agricultural workers. Special focus on economically weaker sections and those engaged in cultivation at subsistence levels. The scheme covers farmers from all states and union territories, with particular emphasis on backward districts and drought-prone regions.
benefits_provided: Direct cash transfer of ₹6,000 per annum in three equal installments: First installment (April-July): ₹2,000, Second installment (August-November): ₹2,000, Third installment (December-March): ₹2,000. Benefits are transferred directly to the farmer's registered bank account through Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT). Additional benefits include: (1) Promotion of agricultural productivity and sustainable farming; (2) Encouragement of adoption of modern agricultural techniques; (3) Supplementary income for meeting essential household needs; (4) Reducing distress migration from rural areas; (5) Promoting financial inclusion through formal banking channels; (6) Support for agricultural input purchases.
application_process: Registration through multiple channels: (1) Online registration via PM-KISAN portal (pmkisan.gov.in) using Aadhar/mobile number; (2) Registration through Common Service Centers (CSCs) across villages; (3) Registration through State Agriculture Department offices and Taluk offices; (4) Self-registration on the PM-KISAN website using registered mobile number and land records. Required documents for registration: Aadhar card, bank account details, land records (Khasra/Jamabandi number from revenue records). For farmers without Aadhar, Aadhar enrollment can be done simultaneously. The application is processed through state governments which verify land ownership records. Farmers receive confirmation SMS after successful registration. Once approved, payment is credited to the bank account within 7-10 days of the benefit period commencement.
documents_required: Aadhar card (mandatory), Bank account passbook or statement showing account number and IFSC code, Proof of land ownership (Khasra, Jamabandi, Title deed, or official revenue record), Mobile number (preferably registered with Aadhar), Identity proof (Voter ID, PAN, Driving License, or Passport), Self-attested copy of above documents for offline registration at CSC or Agriculture office. For tenant farmers: Written agreement or state government recognized tenancy document. For agricultural laborers: Certificate of agricultural work from Gram Panchayat or local administration.
keywords: Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi, PM-KISAN, income support scheme, direct benefit transfer, farmer welfare, agricultural income, small farmers, marginal farmers, Ministry of Agriculture, cash transfer, rural development, financial inclusion, agricultural subsidy, government scheme, farmers benefit, land holding, agricultural workers, DBT portal, Kisan Yojana, minimum income support, sustainable agriculture

description: e-NAM is an online trading platform developed by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare in collaboration with the National Multi-Commodity Exchange (NMCE) and the Small Farmers' Agribusiness Consortium (SFAC). The platform operates as a network of electronic markets linked to traditional mandis, allowing farmers to sell their produce directly to traders and buyers without geographical limitations. It covers commodities including cereals, pulses, oilseeds, spices, fruits, vegetables, and other agricultural products. The system uses transparent pricing mechanisms based on actual supply and demand, real-time information dissemination, and secure digital transactions. As of 2024, e-NAM operates across 1000+ mandis in 35+ states and union territories, handling transactions worth thousands of crores annually.
summary: e-National Agriculture Market (e-NAM) is a pan-India electronic trading platform that connects farmers, traders, and consumers, enabling transparent and efficient agricultural commodity trading across regulated mandis. Launched in 2016, it integrates with existing Agricultural Produce Market Committees (APMCs) to create a unified national market for agricultural products.
eligibility_criteria: Registered farmers, agricultural traders, commission agents, exporters, and buyers with valid trading credentials. Farmers should possess agricultural land or be engaged in farming activities. Registration requires basic documents including Aadhar/PAN card, bank account details, and mandi registration number. Traders and exporters need appropriate licenses and registration certificates from respective APMC authorities. Daily wage laborers engaged in agricultural work are also eligible. No minimum land holding requirement for farmers.
target_beneficiaries: Primary: Small and marginal farmers (58% of beneficiaries), medium farmers, agricultural traders, commission agents. Secondary: Agricultural exporters, processors, wholesalers, retailers, corporate buyers, consumers. Tertiary: Agricultural cooperatives, farmer producer organizations (FPOs), agribusiness enterprises. Geographic focus: Rural agricultural communities in all states and UTs with significant agricultural production.
benefits_provided: Direct market access without intermediaries reducing transaction costs by 15-20%. Transparent price discovery through real-time bidding and competitive trading. Elimination of multiple middlemen layers, increasing farmer income by 5-25% depending on commodity. Reduced agricultural produce market charges and fees. Access to nationwide buyer network, improving demand and price realization. Secure digital payment system with faster fund transfers within 24-48 hours. Quality-based price differentiation encouraging better produce. Real-time market information enabling informed decision-making. Reduced price manipulation and market monopolies. Increased market participation by institutional buyers and exporters.
application_process: Online Registration: (1) Visit official e-NAM portal (enam.gov.in) or use e-NAM mobile application. (2) Click 'New User Registration' and select category (Farmer/Trader/Exporter). (3) Enter basic details: Name, mobile number, email ID, Aadhar number. (4) Complete KYC verification through Aadhar OTP. (5) Fill commodity details (for farmers): type of produce, expected quantity, quality grade. (6) Upload required documents in specified format (PDF/JPG). (7) Bank account verification through micro-deposits (2 small amounts credited within 2-3 days). (8) Receive unique ID and password. Offline Support: Visit nearest e-NAM enabled mandi during business hours (8 AM - 6 PM). Trained e-NAM coordinators provide free registration and training. Verification and Approval: Documents verified within 24-48 hours. Approval notification sent via SMS/email. Account becomes active within 5-7 working days. Post-activation, users can start bidding and trading immediately.
documents_required: For Farmers: (1) Aadhar Card or Voter ID, (2) PAN Card, (3) Bank Account passbook with IFSC code, (4) Mandi registration certificate or proof of land ownership (land deed/revenue receipt), (5) Mobile number linked to Aadhar, (6) Recent photograph (passport size). For Traders/Commission Agents: (1) Business registration certificate, (2) Aadhar Card, (3) PAN Card, (4) Bank Account details, (5) APMC trader registration certificate, (6) Shop/establishment proof, (7) GST registration (if applicable). For Exporters: (1) Import-Export Code (IEC) from DGFT, (2) Business registration, (3) Quality certifications, (4) Phytosanitary certificates (for agricultural products).
keywords: e-NAM, electronic agriculture market, agricultural marketing, mandi, farmer producer, APMC, digital trading, market access, price discovery, agricultural commodities, farmer income, market linkage, transparent pricing, agricultural trade, rural marketing

description: The National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture is a flagship program designed to transform India's agricultural sector towards sustainability and climate resilience. Launched as part of the broader climate change agenda, NMSA focuses on developing and promoting farming practices that reduce greenhouse gas emissions, enhance soil health, and improve water efficiency. The mission operates through multiple sub-schemes including Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY), Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana (PKVY), and Soil Health Management (SHM). NMSA addresses critical challenges such as soil degradation, water scarcity, declining farm productivity, and vulnerability to climate variability. The scheme promotes organic farming, conservation agriculture, precision farming, and integrated nutrient management. It encourages farmers to adopt climate-smart agricultural technologies, crop diversification, and sustainable water harvesting practices. The mission also supports capacity building, knowledge dissemination, and institutional strengthening to enable widespread adoption of sustainable practices across different agro-climatic zones.
summary: National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture (NMSA) is a comprehensive national program launched in 2014 under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to promote climate-resilient and sustainable farming practices across India. The mission integrates climate adaptation and mitigation strategies with agricultural productivity to ensure food security while addressing environmental degradation and climate change impacts on farming communities.
eligibility_criteria: Small and marginal farmers holding up to 5 acres of cultivated land; Tenant farmers and landless agricultural laborers engaged in farming; Farmer producer organizations (FPOs) and agricultural cooperative societies; Women farmers and scheduled caste/scheduled tribe (SC/ST) farming communities; Farmers in drought-prone, flood-prone, and rainfed agricultural areas; Farmers willing to adopt sustainable and climate-smart agricultural practices; Agricultural entrepreneurs and agribusiness entities engaged in sustainable farming; Farmers with no outstanding agricultural loans or debts exceeding a specified threshold
target_beneficiaries: Approximately 5 crore (50 million) small and marginal farmers across India; Women farmers constituting at least 40% of beneficiaries; SC/ST farmers and vulnerable farming communities; Farming communities in climate-vulnerable regions including Himalayan states, coastal areas, and drought-prone regions; Agro-pastoral and livestock-dependent communities; Youth entering agriculture; Farmer collectives and producer groups; Environmental sustainability advocates and agricultural researchers
benefits_provided: Direct financial assistance through grants and subsidies for adopting climate-smart technologies; Soil amendment materials including bio-fertilizers and organic manure at subsidized rates; Micro-irrigation equipment with 40-80% subsidy based on state and farmer category; Certification support for organic farming with financial reimbursement; Free soil health cards providing customized nutrient recommendations; Training programs and capacity building workshops on sustainable practices; Crop and livestock insurance schemes with subsidized premiums; Market linkages and facility to connect with organic product buyers; Weather-based advisory services and crop forecasting information; Demonstration plots in villages to showcase sustainable practices; Support for establishing community seed banks and nurseries; Livelihood diversification opportunities through integrated farming systems
application_process: Register on the official NMSA portal (nmsa.dac.gov.in) or state agriculture department website using Aadhaar and bank account details; Fill out the application form providing personal, land, and farming details; Upload required documents in prescribed formats (PDF/JPG); Visit designated Common Service Center (CSC) or district agriculture office for verification and submission of physical copies; Receive acknowledgment and application reference number via SMS/email; State agriculture department reviews application within 15-20 days; Field verification conducted by district agriculture extension officers to confirm eligibility and land details; Approval notification issued and beneficiary included in treatment list; Financial assistance credited to registered bank account in installments as per implementation schedule; Beneficiary attends mandatory orientation training on selected sustainable practice; Regular monitoring and compliance visits conducted; Annual performance review and renewal of benefits based on adherence to scheme guidelines
documents_required: Land ownership documents (Jamabandi/Pattas/lease agreement for tenant farmers); Government-issued photo identity (Aadhaar Card, PAN Card, or voter ID); Caste certificate (for SC/ST farmers seeking category-specific benefits); Bank account passbook with IFSC code; Mobile number linked to bank account; Village revenue records or land survey documents; Bank loan statement (if applicable); Agricultural credit card or Kisan Credit Card; Farmer registration certificate from state agriculture department; Details of land area and crops grown; Proof of membership in farmer producer organization (if applicable); Soil test report from accredited laboratory (where required)
keywords: sustainable agriculture, climate resilience, climate-smart farming, soil health, conservation agriculture, organic farming, water management, precision agriculture, agro-biodiversity, carbon sequestration, environmental sustainability, farmer welfare, crop productivity, water harvesting, integrated nutrient management, greenhouse gas mitigation, rainfed agriculture, crop insurance, agro-forestry, natural resource management

description: The Soil Health Card Scheme is a comprehensive soil management initiative designed to monitor soil health, provide technical guidance to farmers, and promote balanced nutrient management across Indian agricultural landscapes. Launched with the vision of achieving 'Healthy Soil, Healthy Crops, Healthy Food, Healthy Farmers', the scheme integrates modern soil testing technology with traditional farming wisdom. The soil health card provides detailed information about 12 key soil parameters including nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), potassium (K), pH, electrical conductivity, organic carbon, secondary nutrients (calcium, magnesium, sulfur), and micronutrients (zinc, boron, iron, manganese). The scheme operates through a network of government and government-recognized private soil testing laboratories distributed across all districts. Farmers receive customized fertilizer recommendations based on their specific soil conditions, which significantly reduces input costs while improving crop productivity and sustainability. The scheme also emphasizes capacity building of farmers through training programs on soil conservation, organic matter management, and precision agriculture practices.
summary: The Soil Health Card Scheme (SHC) is a flagship initiative launched in February 2015 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to promote sustainable agriculture through scientific soil testing and management. The scheme provides farmers with personalized soil health cards containing information about soil fertility status and nutrient content, enabling them to make informed decisions about fertilizer application and soil management practices. Over 10 million soil samples have been tested under this scheme, benefiting more than 85 million farmers across India.
eligibility_criteria: Eligibility criteria for the Soil Health Card Scheme include: (1) Indian citizens engaged in agricultural activities, (2) Farmers owning or operating cultivable land (minimum 0.1 hectare), (3) No upper limit on land holding for receiving soil health cards, (4) Both individual farmers and farmer groups/cooperatives are eligible, (5) Tenant farmers and sharecroppers with documentary proof of cultivation rights, (6) Farmers with aadhar or any government-recognized identity proof. Age, education level, and farm size are not restrictive factors. Participation is voluntary, though incentivized through partial cost subsidies.
target_beneficiaries: Primary target beneficiaries include: (1) Smallholder farmers and marginal farmers (67% of beneficiaries), (2) Medium and large farmers seeking precision agriculture practices, (3) Women farmers and farmer groups, (4) Agricultural cooperative societies, (5) Progressive farmers and farmer producer organizations. Geographically, the scheme prioritizes rainfed and semi-arid regions where soil degradation is prevalent. State-wise distribution shows maximum participation from Maharashtra (8.2 million farmers), Uttar Pradesh (7.9 million), Rajasthan (6.5 million), and Madhya Pradesh (5.8 million).
benefits_provided: Key benefits of the Soil Health Card Scheme include: (1) Free or heavily subsidized soil testing (government covers 90-100% cost), (2) Personalized recommendations for optimal fertilizer use based on soil nutrient status, (3) Potential reduction in fertilizer costs by 20-30% through precision application, (4) Improved crop yields by 15-20% through targeted nutrient management, (5) Environmental benefits including reduced fertilizer runoff and groundwater contamination, (6) Long-term soil health improvement and enhanced soil organic matter, (7) Enhanced farm profitability and sustainability, (8) Risk mitigation through data-driven farming decisions, (9) Capacity building and training in modern agricultural practices, (10) Digital access to soil health information through mobile apps and online portals.
application_process: The application and implementation process involves: (1) Registration: Farmers can register at nearest Common Service Centers (CSCs), agricultural extension offices, or online portals of state agriculture departments, (2) Soil Sample Collection: Trained soil sample collectors visit farms and collect samples (500-1000 grams) from multiple spots using standardized protocols, (3) Sample Testing: Samples are processed in government-recognized soil testing laboratories using standardized methods, (4) Analysis & Report: Laboratories analyze samples for 12 parameters and generate detailed test reports, (5) Card Issuance: Soil health cards are issued with recommendations for specific crops, (6) Advisory Dissemination: State agriculture departments distribute cards through village-level meetings and digital platforms, (7) Farmer Capacity Building: Training sessions are conducted to help farmers interpret recommendations, (8) Follow-up Monitoring: State teams monitor implementation and conduct impact assessment. The entire process typically takes 4-6 weeks from sample collection to card issuance.
documents_required: Required documents for obtaining a Soil Health Card include: (1) Aadhar Card or any government-recognized photo identity proof, (2) Land ownership documents such as Patta, Bahi, revenue records, or lease agreement for tenant farmers, (3) Bank account details for subsidy transfer (if applicable), (4) Mobile number for receiving SMS updates and recommendations, (5) Farm location details (GPS coordinates or survey number), (6) For farmer groups/cooperatives: registration certificate and list of member details. Minimal documentation requirement ensures easy accessibility for illiterate or semi-literate farmers.

description: The Kisan Credit Card Scheme is a revolutionary credit initiative that provides farmers with a revolving credit facility in the form of a credit card, enabling them to withdraw credit for various agricultural purposes. Under this scheme, eligible farmers are issued a credit card with a predetermined credit limit based on their landholding, crops grown, and creditworthiness. The scheme aims to bridge the credit gap in the agricultural sector and reduce farmers' dependence on informal credit sources. The credit limit is linked to the farmer's operational land holding and the scale of finance fixed by the NABARD. The scheme covers all direct agricultural activities including crop production, purchase of seeds, fertilizers, pesticides, and hire charges for machinery, as well as allied activities like dairy farming, poultry rearing, and horticulture.
summary: The Kisan Credit Card (KCC) Scheme is a comprehensive credit facility designed to provide timely and adequate credit support to farmers for their agricultural and allied activities. Launched in 1998, it has become one of India's flagship agricultural credit schemes, enabling farmers to access institutional credit at reasonable rates with simplified procedures and flexible repayment terms.
eligibility_criteria:
1. Individual farmers (both owners and tenants) aged between 18-75 years
2. Tenant farmers and share-croppers with lease documents
3. Self-Help Groups (SHGs) and Joint Liability Groups (JLGs) engaged in agriculture
4. Partnership firms and registered societies in agriculture
5. Farmers with valid land ownership or lease documents
6. No minimum or maximum land holding requirement
7. Farmers with good credit history preferred
8. No age bar for tenant farmers
9. Ability to repay the credit
10. Farmers involved in crop production and allied activities
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers, tenant and share-croppers, agricultural laborers taking up farming, women farmers, Scheduled Caste/Scheduled Tribe farmers, farmers engaged in allied agricultural activities (dairy, poultry, fishery, beekeeping), agricultural entrepreneurs, farmers in drought-prone and rain-fed areas, horticulture farmers, and livestock farmers across all states and union territories of India
benefits_provided:
1. Easy access to credit without collateral requirements for loans up to Rs. 1 lakh
2. Simplified application and approval process with minimal documentation
3. Flexible credit limit ranging from Rs. 50,000 to Rs. 3 lakh or more based on landholding
4. Attractive interest rates ranging from 4% to 8% depending on state and lender policies
5. Credit linked to crop cycle and agricultural operations
6. Interest subvention of up to 3% for borrowers repaying on time
7. No recurring processing fees or hidden charges
8. Annual review and increase of credit limit based on repayment record
9. Multi-purpose use for seeds, fertilizers, pesticides, machinery, and equipment
10. Extended credit for post-harvest operations
11. Reduction in dependence on informal credit sources
12. Promotional credit for input purchase at reasonable rates
13. Access to working capital for agricultural operations
14. Coverage under Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) for crop insurance

13. KCC card issued and activated post-documentation

description: PMFBY is an all-inclusive crop insurance scheme designed to provide comprehensive risk coverage to farmers against crop losses. The scheme replaced the previous Modified National Agricultural Insurance Scheme (MNAIS) and the Weather-based Crop Insurance Scheme (WBCIS). Under PMFBY, farmers pay a heavily subsidized premium while the government bears a substantial portion of the premium. The scheme covers food crops (cereals, millets, pulses) and oilseeds at the national level, while cash crops like cotton, sugarcane, tobacco, and turmeric are covered at the state level. The scheme operates on a seasonal basis with two crop seasons: Kharif (monsoon) and Rabi (winter).
summary: Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) is a comprehensive crop insurance scheme launched by the Government of India on February 18, 2016, to provide financial support to farmers against crop losses due to natural calamities, pests, and diseases. This flagship scheme aims to stabilize farmers' income and encourage agricultural investment by reducing the financial burden of crop failures.
eligibility_criteria: All farmers cultivating notified crops in notified areas are eligible. This includes: (1) Loanee farmers (those with crop loans from financial institutions) - mandatory coverage; (2) Non-loanee farmers cultivating notified crops in notified areas - voluntary participation. Farmers must have cultivable land with valid Khasra/Jamabandi or land records proving ownership/tenancy. Tenant farmers and share-croppers with proper documentation are also eligible. However, farmers who have already received compensation for crop damage from any other insurance scheme in the same season for the same crop are not eligible.
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers primarily (who constitute about 86% of farming population), along with medium and large farmers. The scheme particularly targets vulnerable farmer communities, landless agricultural laborers with documented land lease agreements, tenant farmers, and share-croppers. Special focus on farmers in drought-prone, flood-prone, and disaster-vulnerable regions. As of 2024, the scheme covers approximately 45 million farmer accounts annually across 32 states/union territories.
benefits_provided: 1. Premium Subsidy: Farmers pay only 2% premium for Kharif crops, 1.5% for Rabi crops, and 5% for annual commercial/horticulture crops. Government subsidizes remaining 90-98.5% premium. 2. Comprehensive Coverage: Covers crop losses due to non-preventable natural calamities including drought, flood, hail, cyclone, lightning, and unseasonal rainfall. 3. Crop Loss Compensation: Claims range from 50-100% of crop loss depending on the threshold losses. 4. Post-Harvest Loss: Covers losses for 14 days after harvest due to cyclone, rainfall, and hailstorm. 5. Pest and Disease Cover: Protection against specific insurable pests and diseases as defined state-wise. 6. Quick Settlement: Claims processed within 2 months of harvest. 7. Digital Assistance: Online portals and mobile applications for enrollment, premium payment, and claim tracking. 8. Income Support: Direct income protection ensuring farming continues to be economically viable.
application_process: Step 1 - Enrollment: Farmers can enroll through authorized agent banks, cooperative societies, insurance company branches, or online via pmfby.gov.in portal. Registration period typically opens 2 months before each crop season. Step 2 - Document Submission: Submit Aadhar number, bank account details, land ownership/tenancy proof (Khasra/Jamabandi), and photocopy of ID proof. Tenant farmers must provide valid lease agreements. Step 3 - Premium Payment: For loanee farmers, premium is deducted from crop loan disbursement. Non-loanee farmers can pay premium through bank transfer, post offices, or insurance agents. Step 4 - Policy Issuance: Insurance company issues policy within 7 days of enrollment with coverage effective from notified dates (typically mid-June for Kharif, mid-October for Rabi). Step 5 - Loss Assessment: During crop season, farmers report losses to village-level Crop Inspection Committees (CICs) formed by district administration. CICs conduct on-ground surveys and damage assessments. Step 6 - Claims Filing: Claims submitted within 72 hours of loss notification. Supporting documents include harvest/crop cutting certificates, photos, and damage assessment reports. Step 7 - Claim Settlement: Insurance companies process claims within 30-60 days. Payouts credited directly to farmer bank accounts via NEFT/RTGS. Step 8 - Grievance Redressal: Farmers can lodge grievances through toll-free helpline (1800-110-001), district offices, or online portal with 30-day resolution timeline.
documents_required: 1. Identity Proof: Aadhar card, PAN, voter ID, driving license, or passport. 2. Address Proof: Electricity bill, water bill, ration card, or bank statement. 3. Land Documents: Khasra number, Jamabandi, land certificate, or crop history document (Kagazi Kishan/Soil health card). 4. Tenancy/Lease Agreement: For tenant and share-cropper farmers - registered lease deed or affidavit on stamp paper. 5. Bank Account Details: Cancelled cheque or bank passbook copy for claim disbursement. 6. Photo: Recent passport-sized color photograph. 7. Loan Documents: For loanee farmers - crop loan sanction letter or bank approval. 8. Crop Details: List of crops cultivated with sowing dates and area under cultivation. 9. Mobile Number: Active mobile number for SMS updates and communication. 10. Email Address: Optional but recommended for online communication. 11. Caste Certificate: For farmers availing benefits under SC/ST categories (if applicable state-wise). 12. Any Previous Claim Document: If filed claim in past season.

description: Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana - Remunerative Approaches for Agricultural-allied Community's Happiness (RKVY-RAFTAAR) is a flagship centrally sponsored scheme that focuses on agriculture infrastructure development and crop diversification. Launched in 2007 and revamped in 2018 as RKVY-RAFTAAR, it aims to accelerate agricultural growth by promoting sustainable farming practices, developing agricultural infrastructure, and enabling farmers to access modern technologies. The scheme operates through a flexible, participatory, and accountability-based approach, allowing states to prepare their own comprehensive agricultural development plans based on local agro-climatic conditions and comparative advantage.
summary: RKVY-RAFTAAR is a comprehensive agricultural infrastructure development scheme that provides financial assistance for crop production, post-harvest management, agricultural research, and allied sectors. It emphasizes state-specific agricultural development through flexible resource allocation, grassroots participation, and sustainable agricultural practices. The scheme has been instrumental in transforming agricultural practices across India by building critical infrastructure and promoting climate-resilient agriculture.
eligibility_criteria: Agricultural and horticultural farmers, farmer producer organizations (FPOs), self-help groups (SHGs), agricultural cooperatives, state agricultural departments, and recognized research/educational institutions are eligible. Beneficiaries must be residing in rural areas of India and engaged in agricultural activities. Landholding size has no minimum or maximum limit. Priority is given to small and marginal farmers, women farmers, and scheduled caste/tribe communities.
target_beneficiaries: Primary beneficiaries include small and marginal farmers (holding 2 hectares or less), women farmers, farmers from scheduled castes and scheduled tribes, youth farmers, dairy and livestock farmers, horticulture farmers, and fisheries farmers. Secondary beneficiaries include Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs), agricultural societies, village-level producer groups, agricultural research institutions, and state agricultural departments. Estimated beneficiary coverage: 25+ million farmers across 28 states and 8 Union Territories.
benefits_provided: 1. Infrastructure Development: Assistance for construction of farm ponds, drip/sprinkler irrigation systems, agricultural storage facilities, cold storage units, and mechanized equipment centers (cost-sharing: Central Government 60%, State Government 40%). 2. Crop Diversification: Support for shifting from traditional crops to high-value crops including horticulture (fruits, vegetables, spices), floriculture, and medicinal plants. 3. Technology Adoption: Financial support for adoption of advanced farming techniques, precision agriculture, protected cultivation, and organic farming methods. 4. Post-Harvest Management: Assistance for construction of pack-houses, agro-processing units, value addition facilities, and direct marketing infrastructure (APMC bypassing). 5. Capacity Building: Free training programs for farmers on modern farming practices, water management, soil conservation, and sustainable agriculture. 6. Market Linkages: Support for establishing farmers' markets, e-commerce platforms, and supply chain infrastructure. 7. Livelihood Diversification: Aid for livestock integration, dairy farming, beekeeping, and mushroom cultivation. 8. Assistance quantum: Ranging from ₹5,000 to ₹50,000 per farmer depending on project type and category.
application_process: Step 1: Beneficiary Registration - Farmers register through online portal (rkvy.raftaar.nic.in) or visit nearest Common Service Center (CSC) or State Agricultural Department office with required documents. Step 2: Project Submission - Applicant submits detailed project proposal including crop type, area, proposed intervention, budget, and expected outcomes. Step 3: Verification - District Agricultural Officer verifies land ownership, eligibility criteria, and project feasibility through field inspection. Step 4: Approval - District-level selection committee reviews and approves applications based on priority criteria (small-marginal farmers > SC/ST > women > others). Step 5: Fund Disbursement - Approved amount is directly transferred to beneficiary's bank account in installments (typically 50% on approval, 50% on completion and verification). Step 6: Implementation - Beneficiary implements project with technical guidance from department officials. Step 7: Monitoring - Regular inspections conducted; beneficiary must maintain activity and financial records. Step 8: Completion & Verification - Final inspection conducted; completion certificate issued; assets registered with Agriculture Department. Timeline: 3-4 months from registration to fund disbursement. Application deadline: Continuous throughout financial year (subject to fund availability).
documents_required: 1. Aadhar Card and PAN Card. 2. Voter ID or Ration Card for identity verification. 3. Land ownership documents (Pattas, Khata, Land Registry extracts, Lease agreement for tenant farmers). 4. Bank account passbook (first and last page with IFSC code). 5. Caste certificate (for SC/ST beneficiaries seeking reserved category benefits). 6. Gram Panchayat certificate for rural residential status. 7. Applicant's recent passport-sized photographs (2-3 copies). 8. Bank statement or annual income certificate. 9. Project proposal with detailed technical specifications and cost estimates. 10. Beneficiary declaration form (available on scheme portal). 11. Self-declaration that beneficiary is not a defaulter of agricultural loans. 12. Environmental clearance or no-objection certificate (for large-scale projects >25 acres). 13. Compliance certificate from concerned agricultural department. 14. In case of group/FPO application: Registration certificate, bye-laws, list of members with land holdings.

description: PMKSY is a central sector scheme implemented under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare with the primary objective of enhancing irrigated agriculture and optimizing water use efficiency in farming. The scheme focuses on creating sustainable irrigation infrastructure, promoting micro-irrigation technologies, and ensuring 'Har Khet Ko Pani' (water to every field). It aims to reduce dependence on monsoon rains, minimize crop failures, and increase agricultural productivity through improved water availability and management at both field and project levels. The scheme integrates three sub-components: Accelerated Irrigation Benefits Programme (AIBP), Per Drop More Crop (PDMC), and Groundwater Management (GMI), addressing irrigation gaps at different scales.
summary: Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY) is a comprehensive irrigation development scheme launched by the Government of India in 2015 to accelerate agricultural development through enhanced water resource management and efficient irrigation infrastructure across the country.
eligibility_criteria: All states and union territories are eligible to participate in PMKSY. At the beneficiary level: (1) Small and marginal farmers with landholdings up to 2 hectares are prioritized for micro-irrigation support; (2) Farmers having access to water sources or participating in command areas of irrigated projects; (3) Cooperative societies, water user associations, and farmer groups; (4) Individual farmers engaged in agriculture with rights to use agricultural land; (5) Farmers in drought-prone, water-scarce, and sub-watershed areas are given priority; (6) No income ceiling restrictions apply; (7) Farmers must comply with water conservation measures and follow recommended irrigation practices.
target_beneficiaries: The scheme targets approximately 90 million hectares of cultivable land across India. Primary beneficiaries include: (1) Small and marginal farmers (2-5 hectares) - constituting 60-70% of beneficiaries; (2) Rainfed farmers transitioning to assured irrigation; (3) Farmers in water-scarce regions of Rajasthan, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Telangana; (4) Cooperative farming societies and self-help groups; (5) Women farmers in marginalized communities; (6) Tribal and vulnerable farmer communities; (7) Approximately 30-40 lakh farm families receive direct micro-irrigation benefits annually.
benefits_provided:
1. Infrastructure Development: Creation of modern irrigation systems including dams, canals, pipelines, and field channels; rehabilitation of existing irrigation projects; construction of water harvesting structures and check dams.
2. Micro-Irrigation Support: Subsidized drip and sprinkler irrigation systems reducing water usage by 40-60%; targeted for field-level water management; financial assistance up to 55-90% of project cost depending on farmer category.
3. Water Efficiency: Reduction of water consumption by 30-50% through efficient irrigation techniques; increased crop productivity by 30-40% with same water input; reduced operational costs through lower electricity and water expenses.
4. Assured Irrigation: Expansion of irrigation coverage from 54% to 60%+ of cultivable land; reduction in cultivation risks and crop failures; enabling cultivation of higher-value crops.
5. Sustainability: Groundwater recharge through managed aquifer recharge (MAR) structures; rainwater harvesting integration; soil conservation and improved soil health; climate-resilient agriculture practices.
6. Infrastructure Synergy: Integration with Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY), National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture, and River Linking Project; connectivity with renewable energy initiatives for pump electrification.
application_process:
1. Awareness and Registration: Department of Agriculture holds camps and awareness programs in villages; beneficiaries obtain application forms from local agriculture department or block-level centers.
2. Eligibility Verification: Submission of application with proof of land ownership/cultivator rights (Khasra/Adangal, revenue records), Aadhar card, bank details, and income certificate if required.
3. Assessment and Prioritization: District administration conducts field survey and assessment of landholding and water availability; prioritization based on land category (small/marginal), drought-prone status, and location in command area.
4. Documentation: Submission of technical drawings for micro-irrigation (for PDMC); cost estimates and project proposals; proof of water source (well, canal, or borewell); NOC from local water authority if applicable.
5. Approval and Sanction: Block or district-level committee reviews and approves applications; sanction of subsidy amount; issuance of formal approval letter with scheme terms and conditions.
6. Implementation: Selection of HDPE pipe/drip/sprinkler system vendor from approved list; on-site installation by technical teams; quality assurance and inspection before final approval.
7. Payment and Verification: Beneficiary makes initial contribution (10-45% depending on category); remaining subsidy released post-installation verification; final inspection and certification by agriculture officials.
documents_required:
1. Identity and Land Documents: Aadhar card or voter ID; PAN card (optional); Bhu-naksha or land survey map (latest); Khasra or Adangal showing land ownership/tenancy.
2. Land Proof: Revenue records demonstrating cultivator rights; lease deed if tenant farmer; revenue receipt (Lekh Bahi) from past 3 years; NOC from landlord if not owner.
3. Financial Documents: Bank passbook with IFSC code; filled-in bank mandate form; recent bank statements (3-6 months); proof of income if claiming income-based subsidy.
4. Technical Documents: Borewell completion certificate (if applicable); water availability certificate from district irrigation department; technical specification sheet for irrigation system; site plan showing water source and field layout.
5. Additional Records: Agricultural certification or farmer registration documents; caste certificate (if applicable); recent photographs of the applicant and the field; proof of past participation in agriculture (previous crop records).

description: The PM Kisan Maandhan Yojana is a voluntary and contributory social security scheme designed specifically for marginal and small farmers across India. Launched on September 24, 2019, under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, the scheme aims to provide financial security to farming families during their post-retirement years. The scheme operates on a subscription model where farmers contribute a specific amount towards their pension fund based on their age of entry. The Government of India matches the contribution equally. Upon reaching 60 years of age, beneficiaries receive a guaranteed monthly pension of ₹3,000. The scheme is implemented through Life Insurance Corporation (LIC) and the National Pension System (NPS) infrastructure.
summary: PM Kisan Maandhan Yojana (PM-KMY) is a Government of India social security scheme launched in 2019 that provides old age pension security to marginal and small farmers. The scheme ensures a guaranteed monthly pension of ₹3,000 to farmers aged 60 years and above, along with family support provisions.
eligibility_criteria:
1. Must be an Indian citizen
2. Age between 18-40 years at the time of enrollment
3. Should be a marginal or small farmer (owning landholding up to 2 hectares)
4. Regular contributor to the scheme
5. Not a beneficiary of any other social security scheme like Pradhan Mantri Shram Yogi Maan-dhan, Pradhan Mantri Laghu Vyapari Maan-dhan, or any other government pension scheme
6. Should be a member of PM-KISAN scheme for regular financial support
target_beneficiaries:
1. Marginal and small farmers with landholding up to 2 hectares
2. Farmers aged 18-40 years willing to contribute towards the scheme
3. Farming families in rural India seeking old age pension security
4. Approximately 2.25 crore marginal and small farmers across the country
5. Small and marginal farmers not covered under government pension schemes
6. Rural agricultural workers and farmers in vulnerable economic conditions
benefits_provided:
1. Guaranteed monthly pension of ₹3,000 per month after attaining 60 years of age
2. Spouse pension of ₹1,500 per month after the death of the primary beneficiary
3. Government contribution matching the farmer's contribution
4. Portable and transferable across states
5. Low premium contributions starting from ₹55 to ₹200 per month depending on age
6. Easy enrollment through Common Service Centers (CSCs)
7. Digital beneficiary identity through Aadhaar linking
8. Support from LIC-managed infrastructure ensuring transparency and credibility
9. No medical examination required for enrollment
10. Flexible contribution schedule with automatic debit facility
application_process:
1. Visit the nearest Common Service Center (CSC) authorized to enroll PM-KMY beneficiaries
2. Alternatively, visit the official website: pmkisan-vyapari.gov.in or the dedicated PM-KMY portal
3. Download and complete the enrollment form with accurate details
4. Provide all required documents as listed
5. Complete Aadhaar verification and biometric authentication
6. Submit bank details for automatic monthly contribution deduction
7. Pay the first month's contribution at the CSC
8. Receive enrollment confirmation slip with registration number
9. Download digital certificate after successful verification
10. Keep contact information updated for beneficiary notifications
11. For online enrollment, complete eKYC verification through Aadhaar OTP
12. Maintain documentation and confirmation receipts for future reference

2. Agricultural Land Record/Mutation Certificate
3. Farmer Identification Number from SECC or Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN)
4. Bank Account details with IFSC code (Aadhaar-seeded bank account preferred)

description: The Agriculture Infrastructure Fund is a comprehensive credit-linked subsidy scheme designed to support the creation of world-class post-harvest management infrastructure and community farming assets in India. Launched on May 13, 2020, during the COVID-19 pandemic as part of the Atmanirbhar Bharat Abhiyaan, the scheme addresses the critical gap in agricultural infrastructure, particularly in post-harvest management. The scheme has two main components: Component A focuses on post-harvest management infrastructure created by farmers and agri-startups, while Component B supports aggregators and farmer producer organizations in developing community farming assets. The scheme offers loans at concessional rates with government subsidy on interest, enabling farmers and entrepreneurs to invest in modern warehousing facilities, cold storage chains, processing units, and agricultural logistics infrastructure. The AIF recognizes that post-harvest losses in India remain substantial, with estimates ranging from 10-25% for various commodities, making robust infrastructure critical for value addition and farmer welfare.
summary: The Agriculture Infrastructure Fund (AIF) is a centrally sponsored scheme launched in 2020 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to provide affordable credit to agricultural entrepreneurs for creating post-harvest management infrastructure and community farming assets. With a total corpus of ₹1 lakh crore, the scheme aims to strengthen agricultural supply chains and reduce post-harvest losses through modern infrastructure development.
eligibility_criteria: Farmers of any gender (both landholding and tenant farmers), agricultural entrepreneurs, farmer producer organizations (FPOs), self-help groups (SHGs), cooperatives, and agri-startups registered on the Startup India portal are eligible for loans under Component A. Aggregators, bulk handlers, warehousing companies, and FPOs are eligible under Component B. Maximum loan eligibility: ₹10 crore. Minimum loan amount: ₹5 lakh. Age requirement: Applicants should be above 18 years. Credit rating requirement: Minimum CIBIL score of 685 is preferred but not mandatory. The loan can be obtained through scheduled banks and state-owned FIs designated under the scheme.
target_beneficiaries: Primary beneficiaries include small and marginal farmers (holding up to 5 acres), agricultural entrepreneurs interested in value addition, farmer producer organizations with 100+ member farmers, self-help groups engaged in agricultural activities, and agri-startups developing post-harvest solutions. Secondary beneficiaries include agricultural laborers gaining employment in warehouses and processing units, wholesalers and retailers benefiting from improved supply chains, exporters accessing quality-assured produce, consumers gaining access to better-quality agricultural products, state governments developing agricultural infrastructure, and rural entrepreneurs establishing logistics networks. The scheme particularly targets farmers growing perishable commodities (fruits, vegetables, flowers, spices, turmeric, ginger) and specialty crops requiring temperature-controlled storage.
benefits_provided: Farmers benefit from affordable credit at concessional interest rates with government subsidy covering up to 3% of loan amount. Entrepreneurs gain access to term loans for 10-12 years with flexible repayment schedules. Post-harvest losses are significantly reduced through proper storage and processing infrastructure, increasing profitability by 20-35%. Farmers achieve better market linkages and value realization through aggregation platforms. Cold chain infrastructure enables storage of perishable commodities for extended periods, facilitating better price realization. Small and marginal farmers gain access to modern infrastructure through community models and FPOs. Women farmers receive special consideration with slightly lower interest rates and simplified documentation. The scheme supports climate-resilient agriculture by enabling proper storage of climate-suitable crops. Agri-startups receive institutional support and easy credit access to scale operations. Regional agricultural development is promoted through distributed infrastructure across Tier-II and Tier-III cities.
application_process: Step 1: Verify eligibility based on farmer category, project location, and AIF guidelines. Step 2: Approach the designated nodal bank (NABARD, cooperative banks, or scheduled commercial banks notified for the state). Step 3: Submit completed application form along with required documents. Step 4: Provide detailed project report including technical specifications, cost estimates, and market feasibility. Step 5: Bank conducts site inspection and verifies project location and land documents. Step 6: Credit appraisal committee assesses application, loan amount, and repayment capacity. Step 7: Upon approval, loan is sanctioned by the bank with subsidy confirmation from state nodal agency. Step 8: Execute loan agreement and mortgage/pledge documents as per bank's security requirements. Step 9: Post-sanction inspection conducted by bank representatives before fund disbursement. Step 10: First installment disbursed after project commencement verification. Step 11: Subsequent disbursements based on project milestones and completion. Step 12: Repayment begins 18 months after final disbursement (moratorium period), with loans typically repayable over 10-12 years.
documents_required: Aadhar Card and PAN Card (for identity and tax verification). Voter ID or Passport (optional secondary identification). Land ownership documents: Patta extract, revenue records, or land registration certificate (for landholding farmers). Lease agreement or tenant certificate (for tenant farmers). For FPOs: Registration certificate from FASAL (Farmer Producer Organization Database), memorandum of association, board resolution. For SHGs: NRLM registration certificate, DARPAN registration, bank account details. Business plan detailing project description, financial projections, market analysis, and technical specifications. Project cost estimation and quotation from equipment suppliers. Bank account passbook (first and last pages) demonstrating regular transactions. 3 years of income tax returns (if applicable) or Form No. 16/16A. GST registration certificate (for registered businesses). Collateral documents: property valuations, insurance policies, hypothecation deed. CIBIL report for credit assessment. Proof of subsidy eligibility certification from state nodal agency. Photograph (4x6 cm, colored, recent).

scheme_name: Pradhan Mantri Annadata Aay Sanrakshan Abhiyan
description: PM-AASHA is an integrated market support mechanism designed to safeguard the interests of farmers by ensuring they receive fair remuneration for their agricultural produce. The scheme addresses the challenge of farmers receiving prices lower than the declared Minimum Support Price (MSP) by implementing direct interventions through government agencies for procurement of surplus crops. It operates across multiple states and focuses on oil seeds, pulses, and cereals, providing a safety net when market prices fall below MSP. The scheme integrates with existing agricultural markets and e-commerce platforms to create transparent, efficient price discovery mechanisms and reduce market distortions.
summary: PM-AASHA (Pradhan Mantri Annadata Aay Sanrakshan Abhiyan) is a comprehensive price support scheme launched in 2018 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to ensure remunerative prices for agricultural produce and protect farmers' incomes. The scheme operates through three pillars: Price Support Scheme (PSS), Amended Agricultural Produce Market Committee (APMC) order, and e-NAM platform integration to provide minimum support price (MSP) guarantees and market linkages.
eligibility_criteria: All registered farmers engaged in cultivation of notified crops (pulses, oilseeds, and cereals); Small and marginal farmers get priority in procurement operations; Farmers cultivating MSP-notified crops in designated intervention areas during specified procurement seasons; Individual farm holdings and farmer producer organizations (FPOs); Farmers with valid agricultural land records and Aadhar authentication; No restriction on farm size, but special consideration for SC/ST/women farmers; Farmers in non-MSP procurement states eligible through decentralized procurement centers.
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers (primary focus, approximately 70% of beneficiaries); Landless agricultural laborers engaged in notified crop production; Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) and agricultural cooperatives; Women farmers engaged in cultivation of oil seeds and pulses; Farmers in APMC-regulated markets across 22+ states; Vulnerable farming communities dependent on crop sales for livelihood; Approximately 1.2 million farmers directly benefited annually; Indirect beneficiaries including agricultural laborers and rural communities.
benefits_provided: Guaranteed Minimum Support Price (MSP) for notified crops when market prices fall below declared levels; Direct payment to farmers at the point of sale without intermediaries; Income protection mechanism ensuring predictable revenue streams; Market price stabilization preventing agricultural distress; Reduced post-harvest losses through government procurement facilities; Access to government godowns and storage facilities at subsidized rates; Transparent pricing through e-NAM integration; Enhanced bargaining power through collective marketing approaches; Value addition opportunities through government-supported processing units; Insurance coverage against price volatility; Reduced debt burden through timely government payments; Market information systems providing real-time price data.
application_process: Registration through nearest agricultural office or APMC authorized center with valid identity proof and land documents; Enrollment on e-NAM platform for online listing of produce; Crop declaration at designated procurement centers during harvest season (specific windows vary by crop and state); Quality assessment and weighment at government-approved collection centers; Direct bank transfer of MSP amount to farmer's registered account within 48 hours of delivery; For FPOs and cooperatives: Group registration at district agriculture department with membership documentation; Online application portal available at agriculture ministry website; SMS-based notifications for procurement schedules; Mobile app-based entry of produce details for real-time tracking; No application fees required; Grievance redressal through dedicated toll-free helpline (1800-AASHA-00).
documents_required: Aadhar card or identity proof; Land ownership certificates or lease deed for cultivated land; Produce quality certificates from recognized testing laboratories; Bank account details with IFSC code (preferably Jan Dhan or Kissan Credit Card linked); Crop declaration form filled with details of cultivated area and expected production; FPO membership certificate (if applying through organization); Sale deed or cultivated area documentation through Bhavantar scheme; Mobile number registered with bank account; Warehouse receipt or storage certificate (if using storage facilities); Gram panchayat certificate confirming cultivation; Voter ID or PAN card as supplementary identity; Soil health card details (recommended); Crop insurance documents (if claiming under PM-FASAL).

description: The Sub-Mission on Agricultural Mechanization (SMAM) is a comprehensive government initiative designed to boost the adoption of agricultural machinery and mechanized farming practices across India. Launched under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare in 2014, SMAM addresses the critical need for farm mechanization in a country where over 50% of agricultural operations are still performed manually. The scheme operates through a multi-pronged approach including direct subsidy to farmers, custom hiring centers, cluster demonstrations, and capacity building of agricultural engineers. It focuses on mechanizing critical farm operations such as land preparation, sowing, weeding, harvesting, and threshing, thereby reducing labor dependency, improving operational efficiency, and enhancing farm income. The scheme is implemented across all states and union territories with state-specific action plans tailored to regional agricultural needs and farming patterns.
summary: Sub-Mission on Agricultural Mechanization (SMAM) is a centrally sponsored scheme launched in 2014 to promote modern agricultural machinery and equipment adoption among farmers. The scheme aims to increase farm mechanization levels, reduce drudgery, enhance productivity, and ensure sustainable agriculture across India by providing subsidies and promoting hire services for agricultural equipment.
eligibility_criteria: Farmers (individual or groups including SHGs, cooperatives, and FPOs) with valid land holdings and active in agriculture; SC/ST farmers and women farmers given preference in subsidy allocation; Small and Marginal Farmers (SMF) eligible for higher subsidy rates; Landless agricultural laborers in some states; Agricultural entrepreneurs and custom hiring center operators; Beneficiaries must not have availed subsidy for same equipment in past 5 years; Income criteria: generally below ₹1.5 lakh annual income in most states (state-specific variations apply)
target_beneficiaries: Small and Marginal Farmers (primary focus): Approximately 12-15 million farmers; Women Farmers and SHGs: At least 40% of subsidies allocated; SC/ST Farmers: Reserved allocation of 15-20% of benefits; Agricultural Laborers: In select states with mechanization needs; Custom Hiring Center Operators: For procurement of equipment; Youth Agricultural Entrepreneurs: Promoting mechanization-based agribusiness; Tenant/Leasehold Farmers: In states with specific provisions
benefits_provided: Direct subsidy support: 40-50% subsidy for SMF and general farmers, up to 70% for SC/ST categories; Reduced operational costs: 30-40% reduction in per hectare farming costs through mechanization; Enhanced productivity: 20-35% increase in crop yields through timely operations; Labor cost reduction: 40-60% reduction in labor expenses for mechanized operations; Time efficiency: Faster completion of critical farm operations; Custom Hiring Centers: Access to expensive machinery without ownership burden; Gender equality: Women farmers get priority and additional incentive; Improved work conditions: Reduced drudgery and physical strain on farmers; Technology adoption: Access to modern, environment-friendly equipment; Value addition: Equipment for post-harvest processing increases farmer income
application_process: Online Registration: Farmers register on State Agriculture Department portal or SMAM dedicated website (agrimachinery.gov.in) using Aadhar/mobile number; Document Submission: Upload required documents (land records, identity proof, income certificate, bank details); Equipment Selection: Choose equipment from approved list or state-specific list; Vendor Linkage: Portal displays authorized dealers/vendors for equipment; Subsidy Amount Approval: Government calculates eligible subsidy based on equipment cost and farmer category; Payment Process: Two-tier payment - farmer pays their share, government transfers subsidy directly to vendor; Delivery & Registration: Equipment delivered, serial number registered in SMAM database; Post-delivery Support: Follow-up for equipment usage and feedback; Grievance Redressal: Online complaint resolution within 30 days; Timeline: Entire process typically takes 30-45 days from application to equipment delivery
documents_required: Aadhar Card or valid identity proof (Voter ID, PAN, Driving License); Land ownership document (Patta/Lease deed/Land certificate); Caste certificate (for SC/ST category applicants); Income certificate from designated revenue officer; Bank account details (with passbook copy); Photograph (recent, 4x6 cm); Residence proof (electricity bill, water bill, or voter ID); Application form duly filled (available on SMAM portal); Equipment quotation from authorized dealer; Declaration of non-receipt of subsidy for same equipment in past 5 years; Gram Panchayat verification certificate (in some states); Mobile number for OTP verification during online registration

scheme_name: Mission for Integrated Development of Horticulture
description: MIDH is a comprehensive mission aimed at enhancing horticulture production and productivity across India through integrated development of the sector. Launched in April 2014, it consolidates three earlier schemes: National Horticulture Mission (NHM), National Bamboo Mission (NBM), and Coconut Development Board (CDB). The mission focuses on increasing horticultural production from 280 million tonnes to over 400 million tonnes by promoting sustainable farming practices, improving supply chain infrastructure, and generating employment in rural areas. MIDH operates on a co-investment model between the Central Government, State Governments, and beneficiaries. The scheme covers the entire value chain from production to processing and marketing, with special emphasis on addressing regional and crop-specific needs. It promotes modern techniques like drip irrigation, precision farming, organic cultivation, and protected cultivation to enhance productivity while maintaining sustainability.
summary: Mission for Integrated Development of Horticulture (MIDH) is a centrally sponsored scheme launched in 2014 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to promote holistic growth of the horticulture sector in India. The mission integrates development of fruits, vegetables, spices, flowers, and plantation crops through production, productivity enhancement, and value-chain development.
eligibility_criteria: Eligible beneficiaries include: (1) Individual farmers and groups of farmers engaged in horticulture; (2) Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) and agricultural cooperative societies; (3) Self-Help Groups (SHGs) and Women's Groups; (4) Land-holding farmers (both marginal and small farmers preferred); (5) Non-Resident Indians (NRIs) interested in horticultural ventures; (6) Entrepreneurs and agribusiness companies; (7) Youth and women farmers with interest in horticulture; (8) Scheduled Castes (SCs) and Scheduled Tribes (STs) with preference in scheme implementation. Minimum land requirement varies by crop and state (typically 0.25 hectares for vegetables, 1 hectare for fruit crops). No upper limit on land holding for financial assistance under most sub-schemes.
target_beneficiaries: The scheme targets approximately 10-15 million horticulture farmers across India. Priority beneficiaries include: (1) Marginal and small farmers in rural areas; (2) Women farmers and women-headed households; (3) Youth seeking self-employment in horticulture; (4) Farmers in aspirational districts; (5) Tribal and underprivileged communities; (6) Farmers in hill and North-Eastern states; (7) Organic farmers and those practicing sustainable agriculture; (8) Farmer clusters and community-based organizations. The scheme has special focus on creating employment for approximately 50 lakh people in horticulture and allied activities.
benefits_provided: MIDH provides comprehensive benefits including: (1) Capital subsidy ranging from 25-75% of project cost depending on farmer category and location (higher subsidy for women, SC/ST, and marginal farmers); (2) Financial assistance for land preparation, planting material, and infrastructure development; (3) Support for greenhouse/polyhouse construction (40-70% subsidy); (4) Drip and sprinkler irrigation systems (50-90% subsidy); (5) Processing and value-addition infrastructure support; (6) Cold storage and post-harvest management facility assistance; (7) Marketing infrastructure development including farmer markets and pack-houses; (8) Training and skill development programs conducted free of charge; (9) Quality planting material supply at subsidized rates; (10) Technical guidance and extension services; (11) Organic certification support and integrated pest management promotion; (12) Livelihood support and income generation opportunities; (13) Reduced transportation and storage losses; (14) Access to premium markets and quality improvement incentives.
application_process: The MIDH application process involves the following steps: (1) Registration: Prospective beneficiaries register themselves on the state horticulture department portal or visit the nearest District Horticulture Office; (2) Scheme Selection: Choose appropriate sub-scheme based on crop type and available support; (3) Project Proposal Submission: Prepare and submit detailed project proposal with technical drawings, cost estimates, and implementation timeline (typically 10-20 pages); (4) Documentation: Submit all required documents in original and photocopied formats with self-attestation; (5) Verification: District Horticulture Officer or designated committee verifies land ownership, applicant eligibility, and project feasibility; (6) Site Inspection: Official inspection of proposed project location to assess soil, water, and infrastructure; (7) Approval: Scrutiny committee reviews applications and approves based on selection criteria; (8) Sanction Order: Applicant receives sanction letter with subsidy amount and disbursement schedule; (9) Fund Release: First installment (40-50%) released after project initiation and asset creation; (10) Implementation: Farmer executes project with technical support; (11) Monitoring: Quarterly monitoring and evaluation of project progress; (12) Final Verification: Completion inspection and documentation; (13) Final Payment: Remaining subsidy released after verification (30-60 days post-completion). Timeline: Typically 4-6 months from application to first fund release.
documents_required: Essential documents for MIDH application include: (1) Valid identification proof (Aadhar Card, Voter ID, Driving License, PAN); (2) Address proof (Ration Card, Utility Bill, Lease Agreement); (3) Land ownership documents (Land ownership certificate, Patta, Jamabandi, Land survey number); (4) Passport-sized photographs (2-4 copies); (5) Bank account details and passbook copy (first page); (6) Land measurement certificate from Gram Panchayat or Revenue Officer; (7) Soil testing report (for some schemes); (8) Project proposal with cost estimates and technical specifications; (9) Proof of farmer registration (if applicable); (10) Certificate from State Agriculture Department (for marginalized groups if seeking category-specific benefits); (11) Water availability certificate; (12) No Objection Certificate (NOC) from concerned authority if land is mortgaged or leased; (13) Proof of membership in FPO/cooperative (if applying as group); (14) Declaration form on plain paper mentioning farming experience.
