scheme_name: Production Linked Incentive for Food Processing
description: The PLI-FP scheme is a flagship initiative of the Ministry of Food Processing Industries designed to enhance India's food processing capabilities and global competitiveness. Launched on November 10, 2021, the scheme provides production-linked incentives to food processing companies to encourage increased manufacturing, job creation, and export of processed food items. The scheme covers major food processing categories including processed fruits and vegetables, marine products, meat products, milling products, organic products, bakery products, cocoa and chocolate products, and spices. With a total allocation of ₹10,900 crore, the scheme operates for a period of 5 years (2021-2026). It aims to create additional production capacity, enhance India's share in global food trade, and develop a robust domestic food processing ecosystem.
summary: The Production Linked Incentive for Food Processing (PLI-FP) scheme is a government initiative launched in 2021 to boost domestic production and exports of processed food products in India. It offers financial incentives based on incremental sales and manufacturing output, aiming to attract investments in food processing infrastructure and create a globally competitive food processing sector.
eligibility_criteria: Eligible applicants include: (1) New food processing units with minimum investment of ₹25 crore in manufacturing and machinery; (2) Existing food processing units with capex of ₹10 crore; (3) Entrepreneurs engaged in production of processed food items in identified categories; (4) Companies registered under Companies Act, 2013 or as proprietorships/partnerships; (5) Units must be engaged in manufacturing of processed foods (not just trading or distribution); (6) Units should have valid GST registration and compliant tax filing; (7) Units must achieve minimum production thresholds - ₹100 crore in FY2022-23 for new units, with incremental targets in subsequent years; (8) No promoter group involved in food processing business for past 5 years (for new units); (9) Priority given to food processing units in Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities; (10) MSME units with modified criteria are also eligible.
target_beneficiaries: Primary beneficiaries include: (1) Food processing entrepreneurs and manufacturers in organized sector; (2) Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) engaged in food processing; (3) Startups in food processing domain; (4) Food processing clusters and special economic zones; (5) Export-oriented food processing units; (6) Rural and agricultural communities through employment generation; (7) Agricultural producers benefiting from increased demand; (8) Exporters of processed food products; (9) Unorganized sector workers transitioning to formal employment; (10) Consumers through improved quality and variety of processed foods. Approximately 45-50 lakh additional jobs are targeted to be created over the scheme period.
benefits_provided: The scheme provides multiple benefits to eligible applicants: (1) Incentives on incremental sales of 4-10% depending on the product category and unit type (new or existing); (2) Support for capital expenditure with up to ₹25 crore investment for new units; (3) Market linkage support for exports and domestic distribution; (4) Technology upgradation assistance through Ministry schemes; (5) Infrastructure development through food processing parks and clusters; (6) Reduced compliance burden with simplified application process; (7) Working capital support through credit guarantee schemes; (8) Training and skill development programs for workforce; (9) R&D support for product innovation and process improvement; (10) Priority sector lending benefits from banks; (11) Tax incentives and depreciation benefits under existing government schemes; (12) Potential for cluster development with ancillary units; (13) Export promotion support through trade delegations and international forums; (14) Raw material security through linkages with agricultural producers.
application_process: The application process involves the following steps: (1) Pre-application registration on official portal (mofpi.gov.in) with basic company details and contact information; (2) Detailed application submission - Applicants fill Form A with complete project details, financial projections, and technical specifications; (3) Document compilation - Prepare and upload all required documents in prescribed format (PDF, not exceeding 25MB each); (4) Online portal submission - Submit application through registered MOFPI portal with digital signature; (5) Initial verification - Ministry conducts preliminary check for document completeness and eligibility (7-10 days); (6) Request for additional information (RFI) if required - Applicants have 15 days to respond; (7) Technical evaluation - Appointed technical committee reviews DPR, capex plan, market feasibility (20-25 days); (8) Financial viability assessment - Finance team evaluates balance sheets, funding plan, repayment capacity; (9) State-level consultation - MOFPI liaisons with State Food Processing Ministry for on-ground verification; (10) Final committee review - Inter-ministerial committee approves/rejects application; (11) Approval notification - Ministry issues formal approval letter with scheme registration number; (12) MOU signing - Successful applicant signs Memorandum of Understanding with MOFPI; (13) Baseline production documentation - Unit establishes baseline production during first year; (14) Incentive claim procedure - Units submit quarterly claims with sales invoices, production records, and bank statements; (15) Verification and audit - Third-party auditors verify claims; (16) Incentive disbursement - Approved incentives credited directly to bank account (30-45 days). Total processing time from application to approval: 60-90 days.
documents_required: Applicants need to submit the following documents: (1) Completed application form (Form A) as prescribed by Ministry of Food Processing Industries; (2) Corporate identity/Business registration documents - Certificate of Incorporation, GST registration, PAN, Aadhaar; (3) Proof of location and land/property ownership or lease deed for minimum 5-year term; (4) Detailed project report (DPR) including technical specifications, machinery list, and production capacity; (5) Financial statements - Last 3 years audited financial statements and balance sheets; (6) Bank statements and proof of financial capability to meet investment requirements; (7) Product details and quality certifications - BIS, HACCP, ISO standards if applicable; (8) Environmental clearance/approval from State Pollution Control Board; (9) Food license (FSSAI) or certificate of registration; (10) List of major equipment and machinery with technical specifications and cost breakup; (11) Detailed capex plan with timeline and payment schedule; (12) Personnel details - Promoters, directors, key management with brief CVs; (13) Market study and demand assessment; (14) Export plans or domestic distribution strategy; (15) Affidavit regarding promoter group's prior involvement in food processing (for new units); (16) Certificate of no existing dues with statutory authorities; (17) Partnership deed or Memorandum of Association for joint ventures; (18) Environmental impact assessment for mega plants.

description: Atal Bhujal Yojana (ABY) is a centrally-sponsored scheme launched in 2019 by the Ministry of Jal Shakti in collaboration with the World Bank. This scheme aims to promote sustainable groundwater management through a participatory approach at the community level across 78 blocks in 7 states of India. The scheme focuses on addressing groundwater depletion, declining water tables, and water quality issues by implementing integrated water resource management techniques and community participation.
summary: ABY is a flagship groundwater management scheme that combines scientific monitoring with community participation to improve groundwater sustainability. It provides financial assistance for groundwater conservation, artificial recharge structures, water harvesting, and community-based water management initiatives across stress-prone groundwater blocks in states like Rajasthan, Gujarat, Uttar Pradesh, and Haryana.
eligibility_criteria:
Eligible beneficiaries include:
• Farmers in designated groundwater-stressed blocks
• Village-level institutions (Panchayats, Water User Groups)
• Non-Government Organizations (NGOs)
• State/District Water Resource Development Authorities
• Agricultural communities facing water scarcity issues
• Blocks with over-exploited or critical groundwater status
• Areas selected by state governments based on groundwater assessment
• Marginal and small farmers are given priority
• Communities with declining water tables (>2 meters per annum decline)
target_beneficiaries: Direct beneficiaries include 2.4 million farmers across 78 priority blocks in 7 states (Rajasthan, Gujarat, Haryana, Punjab, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, and Maharashtra). Indirect beneficiaries include 8.5 million people through sustainable water management. The scheme particularly targets farmers in groundwater-stressed regions, marginalized communities dependent on agriculture, and villages with poor groundwater quality.
benefits_provided:
1. Groundwater Sustainability: Helps restore declining water tables and ensures long-term water security for agriculture
2. Participatory Approach: Involves communities in decision-making and implementation for better outcomes
3. Water Quality Improvement: Addresses contamination issues and promotes clean groundwater access
4. Community Empowerment: Strengthens local institutions for sustainable water management
5. Financial Support: Provides subsidies (up to 90% for community structures) for water conservation works
6. Capacity Building: Offers training to farmers and water user groups in modern water management techniques
7. Employment Generation: Creates temporary and permanent employment in water conservation activities
8. Increased Agricultural Productivity: Improves water availability leading to better crop yields
9. Monitoring Framework: Real-time data on groundwater status through advanced monitoring systems
10. Knowledge Dissemination: Shares best practices and success stories across blocks and states
application_process:
Step-by-step process for farmers and communities:
1. Identification: State governments identify eligible blocks based on groundwater assessment and stress indicators
2. Community Registration: Interested farmers/groups register with block-level implementation teams
3. Verification: Eligibility verification through land records and groundwater status documentation
4. Submission: Submit application through Village Development Committee or designated nodal agency with:
   • Copy of land ownership documents (Patta/Lease)
   • Farmer ID/Aadhaar Card
   • Proposed water conservation structure plan
   • Community consent letter for shared structures
5. Assessment: Field survey and technical assessment by water resource engineers
6. Approval: Project approval by block/district level committees within 30 days
7. Sanction: Financial sanction and fund release (typically 50-60% upfront, remainder on completion)
8. Implementation: Community participates in construction under supervision
9. Monitoring: Regular monitoring through GPS-enabled groundwater measurement stations
10. Certification: Completion certificate issued and final payment released

Online Application: Available through state-specific portals and www.jalsakhipr.gov.in
documents_required:
Required documents for application:
• Aadhaar Card (Applicant/Farmer)
• Land Ownership Certificate/Patta/Lease Deed
• Photograph (Passport size - 2 copies)
• Bank Account Details (Cancelled cheque preferred)
• Caste/Income Certificate (if applicable)
• Gram Panchayat certificate confirming eligibility
• Detailed Project Report (DPR) for community structures
• Boundary map/Layout of proposed water conservation structure
• Proof of participation in awareness camp (for community groups)
• Water quality test report (baseline, if available)
• Land use classification document from revenue department
• No Objection Certificate from local water resource department
• Agricultural Census/Farm registration details
• Signed community consent form (for collective works)

scheme_name: Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act
description: The Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA), formally known as the National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (NREGA) until 2009, is India's flagship social security and public works program. Launched on February 2, 2005, under the Ministry of Rural Development, MGNREGA guarantees 100 days of wage employment in a financial year to every adult member of any household willing to undertake unskilled public work at the statutory minimum wage. The scheme aims to enhance livelihood security in rural areas by providing guaranteed employment while simultaneously creating productive assets for rural development and infrastructure improvement.
summary: MGNREGA is a transformative rural employment guarantee scheme that provides assured work to rural households, generates sustainable rural assets, and promotes inclusive development. Since its inception in 2005, the program has evolved into the world's largest public works scheme, offering dignified employment opportunities while strengthening rural infrastructure, water conservation, forestry, and community assets. The scheme combines employment guarantee with asset creation, making it a unique development intervention.
eligibility_criteria: All adult members of rural households seeking work; Willing to undertake unskilled public work; Residing in rural areas; Indian citizens; No literacy or skill requirements; Age 18 years and above; Both BPL and APL households; Migrant workers eligible in destination state; Available for work at local minimum wage rates; Registered in Employment Exchange or directly applying for work.
target_beneficiaries: Rural agricultural laborers and landless workers; Small and marginal farmers; Rural women seeking employment and livelihood security; Rural youth seeking income generation opportunities; Socially and economically marginalized populations; Tribal communities and forest-dependent populations; Dalits and other backward communities; Migrant workers in rural areas; Construction workers and unorganized sector workers; Landless agricultural workers seeking regular employment.
benefits_provided: Guaranteed 100 days of employment per financial year at minimum wage; Additional 50 days of work available in drought/distress situations; Minimum wage guaranteed irrespective of work output; Equal wages for men and women; Employment record maintained through Job Cards; Social security linkages through MGNREGA employment; Asset creation for community benefit; Skill development opportunities through training; Workplace facilities including drinking water, shade, and first aid; Unemployment allowance if employment not provided within 15 days; Transportation allowance for work beyond 5km; Enhanced income during agricultural lean season; Strengthened rural infrastructure and natural resources.
application_process: Step 1: Obtain Job Card - Visit Gram Panchayat office with identification proof (Aadhar, voter ID, PAN); Submit application in prescribed format; Gram Panchayat prepares household list; Unique Job Card issued with family details and photograph; Registration remains valid for 5 years. Step 2: Demand Work - Submit work demand in writing or verbally to Gram Panchayat; Can demand work any time during the year; Work must be provided within 15 days of demand; Unemployment allowance applicable if work not provided. Step 3: Commence Work - Report on designated date at work site; Work on public works projects for unskilled labor; Wages paid via bank transfer or direct cash; Attendance recorded in Muster Rolls. Step 4: Claim Wages - Wages paid weekly or fortnightly; Can claim through registered bank account; Direct cash payment in exceptional cases; Grievance redressal if payment delayed beyond 15 days.
documents_required: Proof of Identity - Aadhar Card, Voter ID, Passport, PAN Card, or Driving License; Proof of Residence - Ration card, Utility bill (electricity/water), Bank statement, or Rent agreement; Photograph - Recent passport-size color photograph (2x2 inches); Self-certification - Declaration of rural residence and willingness to work; For Job Card Registration - Caste certificate if applicable; Bank account details for wage transfer (with Cancelled cheque or Bank statement); Mobile number for communication and updates; In case of Migrant Workers - Identity proof from home state and proof of residence in current state; SC/ST/OBC certificates if claiming category benefits.
keywords: Rural employment guarantee, Public works scheme, Wage employment, Rural development, Social security, Community assets, Infrastructure development, Livelihood security, Rural infrastructure, Employment guarantee act, Unskilled labor, Public works program, Rural asset creation, Sustainable development

description: The Interest Subvention Scheme (ISS) is a flagship credit facilitation scheme launched in 2006 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to reduce the cost of agricultural credit. The scheme provides interest subvention (subsidy) to farmers availing short-term credit from banks and financial institutions for agricultural purposes. This scheme aims to make agricultural credit more affordable and accessible to all categories of farmers, particularly small and marginal farmers. The subvention is provided at the point of lending, reducing the effective interest rate burden on farmers. The scheme has been periodically enhanced and expanded to cover more borrowers and increase the subvention rates to support agricultural productivity and sustainability.
summary: Interest Subvention Scheme (ISS) is a government initiative that provides interest subsidy on short-term agricultural credit to reduce farmers' borrowing costs and promote agricultural investments and productivity across India.
eligibility_criteria: Farmers with outstanding loans from scheduled commercial banks, cooperative banks, and regional rural banks; No income limit applied for interest subvention; Farmers availing crop loans for agricultural activities; Farmers who are not defaulters and maintain proper repayment schedule; Both individual farmers and group borrowers eligible; Small and marginal farmers given priority; All categories of farmers including SC/ST farmers eligible; Farmers engaged in cultivation of crops, horticulture, and floriculture; Farmers with valid Aadhar or other recognized identification; Landless agricultural laborers engaged in farming activities; Tenant farmers with documented proof of cultivation rights
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers holding less than 2 hectares of land; Medium farmers with 2-10 hectares of land; Large farmers engaging in agricultural credit; Agricultural laborers and tenant farmers; Women farmers and women-headed households; SC/ST farmers and economically backward communities; Farmers in drought-prone and rain-fed areas; Farmers transitioning to sustainable and organic farming; Young farmers entering agriculture profession; Cooperative farming groups and agricultural societies; Farmer producer organizations (FPOs); Agricultural entrepreneurs in allied sectors
benefits_provided: Interest subvention of 2% per annum on short-term crop loans upto Rs. 3 lakh or as notified; Additional 3% subvention for small and marginal farmers; Further 2% subvention if loan repaid on schedule; Effective interest rate reduction from 9-10% to 4-5% for eligible farmers; No requirement for farmers to apply separately - benefit provided at bank level; Interest subsidy provided by government directly to lending institutions; Reduction in effective cost of borrowing encourages timely repayment; Benefit extended to all crops including food crops, cash crops, and horticulture; Covers fisheries and animal husbandry loans under extended provisions; Simplified documentation and faster loan disbursement
application_process: 1. Farmer approaches the nearest authorized bank branch (Scheduled Commercial Bank, Cooperative Bank, or RRB) with valid identification and land documents; 2. Farmer submits loan application for short-term agricultural credit with detailed crop/farming plan; 3. Bank verifies farmer's credentials, landholding, and repayment capacity; 4. Bank assesses loan requirement based on crop and farm size; 5. If loan is approved, bank disburses the amount to farmer's account; 6. Interest subvention is calculated and applied by the bank from the date of disbursement; 7. Farmer repays the loan in agreed installments at reduced interest rate; 8. If repayment is done on schedule, additional 2% subvention is credited; 9. Bank submits subsidy claim to government along with beneficiary details; 10. Government reimburses the subvention amount to the lending institution; 11. No separate online application portal - benefit is automatic for eligible borrowers
documents_required: Aadhar Card or Voter ID or Driving License for identification; Proof of address (electricity bill, water bill, or rental agreement); Land ownership documents (Jamabandi, Khasra, or lease deed); Passport-size photographs (2-3 copies); Bank account details and passbook copy; Crop details and farming plan for the loan period; Details of previous loans (if any) and repayment history; Caste certificate for SC/ST farmers (if applicable); Income certificate from government authorities; Declaration form regarding land utilization; Copy of PAN (optional, for loans above specified limit); Survey number or GPS coordinates of agricultural land; Crop insurance policy details (if applicable)
keywords: Interest Subvention, Agricultural Credit, Crop Loan, Farm Financing, Interest Rate Reduction, Farmers Subsidy, Agricultural Finance, Short-term Credit, Ministry of Agriculture, Bank Loan, Crop Financing, Farmer Support, Credit Availability, Lending Rate, Agricultural Investment, Loan Accessibility

description: The Price Support Scheme is a comprehensive agricultural market intervention program designed to stabilize prices and ensure remunerative prices to farmers for their produce. Launched under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare in 2015, the scheme operates as a safety net mechanism for farmers producing essential agricultural commodities. The scheme primarily focuses on procurement of commodities at Minimum Support Price (MSP) when market prices fall below the declared support prices. It covers various commodities including cereals (wheat, rice), pulses (arhar, moong, urad), oilseeds (soybean, sunflower), and other essential crops. The scheme utilizes a network of procurement centers, government agencies, and cooperative societies to ensure direct purchase from farmers at guaranteed prices, thereby eliminating middlemen and providing transparent pricing mechanisms. Through strategic storage, distribution to public distribution systems, and market stabilization operations, the scheme ensures food security while protecting farmer incomes.
summary: Price Support Scheme (PSS) is a government initiative launched in 2015 to provide price stability and guaranteed minimum prices for agricultural commodities, protecting farmers from market fluctuations and ensuring fair remuneration for their produce through direct procurement and market intervention mechanisms.
eligibility_criteria: Registered and recognized farmers engaged in cultivation of specified commodities; Small and marginal farmers with landholding up to 5 acres; All categories of farmers (general, SC/ST) regardless of caste or community; Farmers in notified procurement areas as per state government announcements; Individual farmers and Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs); Farmers who have completed crop harvesting and possess quality-compliant produce meeting food safety standards; Tenants and share-croppers possessing legal proof of cultivation rights; No minimum produce quantity requirement for individual farmers
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers (approximately 70% of beneficiaries); Landless agricultural laborers engaged in farming operations; Tenant farmers and share-croppers; Farmer Producer Organizations and farming cooperatives; Women farmers engaged in cultivation of PSS-covered commodities; Farmers in economically backward and drought-prone regions; Tribal farmers in hilly and forest areas; Approximately 8-10 million farmers annually across India
benefits_provided: Guaranteed minimum support prices (MSP) for specified agricultural commodities; Price protection mechanism against market price fluctuations and volatility; Direct procurement by government agencies eliminating middlemen interference; Fair and transparent pricing ensuring 100% of MSP payment; Increased farm income and economic stability for rural households; Assured market for produce reducing post-harvest losses; Easy accessibility through decentralized procurement centers within 5-10 km radius; Banking facilities and direct payment through digital transfers; Quality price incentives for superior grade produce; Reduced distress selling and forced migration from farming; Food security strengthening for nation through procurement for public distribution; Market stabilization through strategic interventions
application_process: Visit nearest notified procurement center during announced procurement season (April-June for wheat, October-December for rice, September-November for pulses); Present original identity proof and landholding documents for verification; Get produce weighed and quality tested by official grade-certifier; Complete application form with farmer details and produce specifications; Receive quality certificate if produce meets food safety standards; Select payment mode (direct bank transfer, cash, or cooperative society credit); Obtain receipt with transaction reference number and MSP amount; Payment processed within 48 hours for bank transfer or on-the-spot for cash; Grievance redressal available through toll-free number 1800-180-1551 and official portal; Appeal process available at district level if rejected for quality reasons; Annual enrollment not required; Fresh applications accepted during each procurement season
documents_required: Valid Aadhaar Card or alternative government-issued identity proof (PAN, Voter ID, Driving License); Passbook or bank account proof for digital payment transfers; Land ownership/tenancy documents (Jamabandi, Lease deed, or Share-cropping agreement); Crop insurance certificate (optional but recommended); Certificate from gram panchayat/revenue official confirming farmer status; Quality certificate from agricultural officer confirming produce specifications; Crop produce quality certificate for storage-eligible commodities; Bill of lading or transport documents if using government transportation; Declaration form confirming ownership/custody of produce; Mobile number for SMS-based transaction updates
keywords: Price Support Scheme, MSP, Minimum Support Price, Agricultural Procurement, Market Intervention, Farmer Protection, Food Security, Price Stability, Agricultural Economics, Commodity Procurement, Government Procurement Centers, Pulse Procurement, Wheat Procurement, Rice Procurement, Oilseed Procurement, Farmer Income Support, Agricultural Marketing, Public Distribution System, Food Grain Storage, Remunerative Prices, Price Volatility, Procurement Operations

description: Agricultural Technology Management Agency (ATMA) is a decentralized, institutionalized mechanism established at district level to facilitate coordination between research institutions and farm families for technology assessment and demonstration. ATMA acts as a nodal agency for providing technology and extension services to farmers. It bridges the gap between the research being conducted at agricultural universities and research stations and the farmers in the field. The scheme aims to integrate agricultural research and extension services at the district level to promote adoption of improved farming practices and agricultural technologies. ATMA operates through block and village-level extension workers and farmer groups to ensure technology reaches the grassroots level.
summary: ATMA is a district-level extension agency that coordinates between agricultural research and farming communities, facilitating technology transfer and capacity building through decentralized extension services to enhance agricultural productivity and farmer incomes.
eligibility_criteria: Agricultural departments and institutions at district level; State governments implementing agriculture schemes; Districts selected based on agro-climatic conditions and development needs; Registered farmers and farmer groups; Organizations working in agricultural extension; All categories of farmers including small, marginal, and large landholders; Women farmers as per state policy; Youth entrepreneurs in agriculture sector.
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers constituting approximately 86% of farming community; Women farmers and agricultural workers; SC/ST farming communities; Youth interested in agriculture; Landless agricultural laborers; Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs); Progressive farmers serving as demonstration farmers; Agricultural entrepreneurs and agri-businesses; Farming families across all agro-climatic zones within district.
benefits_provided: Access to improved agricultural technologies and scientific farming practices; Demonstration of new crop varieties and cultivation techniques; Capacity building through training and skill development programs; Advisory services for sustainable farming methods; Facilitation of farmer-to-farmer learning through demonstration plots; Technology assessment and feedback mechanism; Linkage with agricultural research institutions and universities; Support for organic farming and natural resource management; Climate-smart agriculture advisory services; Information dissemination through ICT platforms; Group insurance and credit linkage facilitation; Reduced technology adoption gaps; Increased crop productivity and farm income; Improved soil health through sustainable practices; Enhanced market linkages and agribusiness opportunities.
application_process: Registration at district ATMA office by individual farmers or through Farmer Groups/FPOs; Submission of basic farmer information and land details; Enrollment in ATMA membership (usually free or nominal fee); Attending Farmers' Training Centers (FTCs) established at block level; Participation in demonstration plots and field schools; Receipt of extension bulletins and advisories through SMS, apps, or print media; Enrollment in specific schemes through block extension officers; Direct contact with Village Level Extension Workers (VLEWs) for farm-specific guidance; Participation in Kisan Gosthi (farmers' forums) for community learning; Registration with Primary Agricultural Credit Society (PACS) for linked services.
documents_required: Farmer registration form with Aadhar number; Land ownership proof (land record/patta/FMB); Caste certificate (if claiming SC/ST benefits); Bank account details with cancelled cheque; Photograph (passport size); Identity proof (Aadhar/PAN/Voter ID); Crop insurance enrollment form (optional); FPO membership certificate (if applying through group); Self-declaration of farming activity; Mobile number for digital service delivery.
keywords: ATMA, Agricultural Technology Management Agency, district-level extension, technology transfer, farmers training, demonstration plots, farmer field schools, extension services, agricultural research, technology adoption, sustainable farming, climate-smart agriculture, farmer groups, capacity building, agricultural universities, extension workers, agro-advisories, organic farming, crop varieties, farmer producer organizations, digital extension, agricultural productivity, soil health, natural resource management, rural development, agricultural innovation.

scheme_name: Sub-Mission on Seeds and Planting Material
description: The Sub-Mission on Seeds and Planting Material (SMSP) is a comprehensive government initiative launched in 2014 under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to enhance the supply and quality of certified seeds and planting material across India. The scheme operates as a dedicated input supply intervention aimed at increasing crop productivity through the distribution of high-quality, certified seeds that are disease-resistant, high-yielding, and adapted to various agro-climatic zones. SMSP works through a network of seed production, processing, and distribution centers to ensure farmers have access to improved seeds of crops including cereals, pulses, oilseeds, and horticulture crops. The mission supports both public and private seed industry stakeholders and promotes seed production in identified seed hubs across different states.
summary: SMSP is a targeted input supply scheme designed to improve seed quality and availability for Indian farmers. It focuses on strengthening the seed production infrastructure, promoting registered seed growers, ensuring seed certification, and facilitating timely availability of quality seeds at affordable prices. The scheme has distributed over 50 lakh quintal of certified seeds annually across multiple crops.
eligibility_criteria:
1. Registered Seed Producers: Individual farmers, farmer groups, and organized seed production enterprises registered with the State Agriculture Department or Seed Certification Agency.
2. Seed Certification Agency (SCA): State-level agencies authorized to certify seeds according to Indian Seeds Act standards.
3. Seed Production Clusters: Groups of farmers in identified seed production zones and hubs.
4. Horticultural Farmers: Those engaged in vegetable, spice, and plantation crop production in designated areas.
5. State Agricultural Departments: For establishing seed production centers and distribution networks.
6. Cooperative Societies: Registered agricultural cooperatives engaged in seed multiplication and distribution.
7. Private Seed Companies: Licensed private enterprises meeting quality and regulatory standards.
target_beneficiaries: Primary beneficiaries include small and marginal farmers (with holdings up to 2 hectares), progressive farmers interested in quality seed production, seed growers in identified seed hubs across states like Punjab, Haryana, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Telangana. Secondary beneficiaries encompass agricultural input dealers, state seed corporations, registered seed producers, horticultural farmers, and rural communities dependent on agriculture. Women farmers and farmer groups engaged in seed production are particularly encouraged to participate.
benefits_provided:
1. Quality Seed Access: Guaranteed supply of certified seeds meeting ISI standards with germination rate ≥85% and purity ≥98%.
2. Subsidized Seed Rates: 50% subsidy on cost of certified seeds for distribution through designated distribution centers.
3. Breeder Seed Supply: Provision of breeder seed to registered seed producers at nominal rates.
4. Infrastructure Support: Financial assistance for establishing seed production centers, processing units, and cold storage facilities.
5. Seed Certification: Free or subsidized seed certification by State Agricultural Department recognized agencies.
6. Training Programs: Capacity building and training for seed growers on seed production techniques, storage, and quality maintenance.
7. Transport Assistance: Subsidy on transportation of seeds from production centers to distribution points.
8. Crop Insurance: Reduced insurance premiums for seed production crops.
9. Market Linkage: Direct linkage between seed producers and farmers through organized distribution networks.
application_process:
Step 1: Identify Eligibility - Confirm registration with State Agriculture Department or Seed Certification Agency and select approved seed crops for your zone.
Step 2: Obtain Application Form - Download from State Agricultural Department website or collect from Block-level Agricultural Office.
Step 3: Document Submission - Prepare required documents including proof of land ownership/lease, farmer registration certificate, and crop insurance certificate.
Step 4: Submit Application - File application at District Agriculture Office with complete documentation during announced application windows (typically April-June for kharif and September-November for rabi).
Step 5: Verification - Agricultural officers conduct field inspection and verify farmer credentials, landholding, and proposed seed production area.
Step 6: Approval and Allocation - Approved applicants receive breeder seed allocation letters and subsidy authorization from District Agriculture Office.
Step 7: Seed Production - Cultivate seeds following certification agency standards with mandatory field inspections at flowering and maturity stages.
Step 8: Seed Certification - Submit harvested seeds for certification by accredited agency; obtain seed certification certificate.
Step 9: Benefits Disbursement - Receive subsidies and incentives directly through State Agriculture Department after final certification approval.
documents_required:
1. Identity Proof: Valid Aadhar card, PAN card, or voter ID.
2. Land Documents: Title deed, revenue record (Jamabandi/Pattas), or lease agreement (minimum 3-year lease for rented land).
3. Agricultural Registration: Farmer registration certificate from local agricultural department or Kisan ID.
4. Seed Grower Certificate: Registration certificate from Seed Certification Agency or State Seed Corporation (if applicable).
5. Bank Account Details: Copy of passbook with bank account linked to Aadhar for subsidy transfer.
6. Crop Insurance: Insurance certificate for the proposed seed production crop.
7. Breeder Seed Request: Application form for obtaining breeder seed with crop variety specifications.
8. Farm Details: Land survey map, soil test report (from authorized lab), and agro-climatic zone classification.
9. Training Certificate: Completion certificate from recognized seed production training program (for new seed growers).
10. Previous Production Records: Documentation of previous agricultural/seed production experience (if any).

description: The Krishi Vigyan Kendra (Agricultural Science Center) Scheme represents a landmark initiative in India's agricultural extension framework. Established in 1974 under ICAR's vision, KVKs operate as district-level technology transfer institutions that serve as crucial linkages between agricultural research generated at universities and ICAR institutes and grassroots farmers. Each KVK functions as a block development and agricultural extension center, strategically located to serve approximately 4-5 blocks covering a population of 500,000-1,000,000 people. The scheme operates through a network of over 700 KVK centers established across all Indian states and union territories. These centers focus on testing, refining, and promoting location-specific agricultural technologies across crop production, animal husbandry, fisheries, natural resource management, and agricultural engineering. KVKs conduct frontline demonstrations, organize training programs for farmers and farm women, provide need-based advisory services, and facilitate market linkage opportunities. The centers employ multidisciplinary teams including subject matter specialists in crop science, livestock management, fisheries, home science, and agricultural engineering to provide comprehensive support to rural communities.
summary: Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) Scheme is a pioneering agricultural extension initiative launched in 1974 by the Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) to bridge the gap between research institutions and farming communities. Operating across India's agricultural landscape, KVKs function as technology transfer centers that disseminate modern agricultural practices, promote entrepreneurship, and enhance farmer productivity through practical demonstration, training, and advisory services.
eligibility_criteria: KVK services are accessible to all categories of farmers including marginal farmers (holding <1 hectare), small farmers (1-2 hectares), semi-medium farmers (2-4 hectares), and medium-to-large farmers (>4 hectares). Women farmers are given special emphasis with dedicated programs and training modules. Agriculture entrepreneurs, farm youth aged 18-35, agricultural graduates seeking to establish agricultural ventures, rural youth interested in self-employment, dairy farmers, fishermen and aquaculture practitioners, and livestock rearers are eligible beneficiaries. Cooperative societies, farmer producer organizations (FPOs), agricultural self-help groups (SHGs), and women's collectives engaged in agriculture and allied activities are also eligible for KVK support. Landless agricultural laborers seeking skill development in agricultural trades and rural entrepreneurs are encouraged to participate in vocational training programs. No minimum educational qualification is mandated, though farmers with different literacy levels are provided with training modules tailored to their comprehension capabilities.
target_beneficiaries: The primary beneficiaries include: (1) Resource-poor and marginal farmers constituting approximately 86% of India's farming population requiring affordable, sustainable agricultural solutions; (2) Women farmers and rural women engaged in agriculture and allied activities, with targeted capacity building programs; (3) Farm youth aged 18-35 seeking agricultural careers or entrepreneurship opportunities; (4) Agricultural labor force requiring skill upgrade in modern farming techniques; (5) Livestock keepers and dairy entrepreneurs promoting integrated farming systems; (6) Fishermen and aquaculture practitioners in coastal and inland regions; (7) Farmer groups, cooperatives, and FPOs seeking technology adoption and institutional strengthening; (8) Agricultural graduates and youth establishing agriculture-based enterprises; (9) Vulnerable groups in rainfed and drought-prone regions requiring climate-resilient technologies; (10) Landless households seeking self-employment through agricultural value chains.
benefits_provided: KVKs provide comprehensive multi-faceted benefits to rural communities: (1) Technology Dissemination - Timely transfer of location-specific, research-backed agricultural technologies tested through on-farm demonstrations; (2) Skill Enhancement - Vocational training programs in crop production, horticulture, livestock rearing, fisheries, bee-keeping, mushroom cultivation, and value-addition; (3) Extension Services - Advisory services on crop health, pest management, soil fertility, water management, and sustainable farming practices; (4) Demonstration Impact - Frontline demonstrations of improved seeds, fertilizers, machinery, and production techniques with visible results on farmers' fields; (5) Livelihood Diversification - Promotion of allied agricultural activities including dairy, poultry, fisheries, and agro-forestry for income augmentation; (6) Women Empowerment - Skill training in food processing, organic farming, mushroom cultivation, and income-generating activities for women farmers and SHGs; (7) Climate Resilience - Promotion of climate-smart agriculture techniques, water-harvesting, drought-resistant varieties, and sustainable natural resource management; (8) Market Linkages - Connection between farmers and input suppliers, buyers, and traders through e-platforms and organized marketing channels; (9) Entrepreneurship Support - Training and guidance for establishment of agricultural enterprises, agro-industries, and rural enterprises; (10) Cost Reduction - Demonstration of lower-cost production technologies reducing input costs while maintaining yields.
application_process: Application process for KVK programs is farmer-centric and simple: (1) Awareness & Registration - Farmers can register at nearest KVK office or through village-level extension workers who facilitate enrollment for training programs; (2) Program Selection - Farmers select from available training modules (on-farm training, off-campus training, demonstration programs) based on their interests and agricultural profile; (3) Enrollment - Registration forms are completed providing basic farm details, land holding size, crop patterns, and training objectives; (4) Documentation - Minimal documentation including Aadhar card (identity), land records (if applicable), and proof of residence; (5) Training Allocation - KVK subject matter specialists allocate farmers to relevant training batches conducted monthly or seasonally based on agricultural calendar; (6) On-Farm Demonstrations - Interested farmers provide small plot (0.10-0.20 hectares) on their field where KVK demonstrates improved varieties and practices; (7) Advisory Registration - Farmers seeking advisory services register their mobile numbers for SMS-based advisories, voice calls, or in-person consultations; (8) Participation in Workshops - Farmers participate in monthly group meetings, field days, and farmer-led demonstrations; (9) Certification - Upon completion of training programs, farmers receive participation certificates recognized across agricultural networks; (10) Feedback & Follow-up - Regular follow-ups ensure technology adoption and provide field-level support through subject matter specialists. No registration fees are charged; services are completely free for all farmers.
documents_required: Minimal documentation is required to access KVK services, ensuring accessibility for all farmers: (1) Identity Proof - Any government-issued identity document (Aadhar card, voter ID, driving license, ration card) for verification and registration; (2) Proof of Residence - Electricity bill, ration card, postal address, or gram panchayat letter confirming residential address; (3) Land Records - In case of on-farm demonstrations, farmers provide land holding records (plot map, revenue record, or patta) or can give verbal declaration for landless farmers; (4) Occupation Certificate - Self-declaration as farmer or agricultural laborer; (5) Bank Account Details - For farmers receiving subsidy or support under linked government schemes; (6) Photograph - 2-3 passport-sized recent photographs for training certificates and official records; (7) Mobile Number - Contact number for receiving SMS advisories and project communication; (8) Self-Declaration Form - Simple form with basic information on farm size, crops grown, and training interests. For women-focused programs: (9) SHG Registration Certificate - If part of registered self-help group; (10) Farmer Producer Organization (FPO) Registration - If member of FPO seeking collective benefits. Most documentation can be submitted online or through village-level extension workers, reducing burden on farmers.

description: The National Bamboo Mission is a comprehensive scheme designed to promote bamboo cultivation as a viable income source for farmers while addressing ecological concerns. Launched in 2018 with an initial outlay of ₹1,290 crores for the period 2018-2023, the scheme focuses on expanding bamboo plantation in non-forest areas, particularly on marginal and degraded lands. The mission encompasses the entire value chain from nursery development to post-harvest management and industry linkages, positioning bamboo as a holistic development solution for rural India.
summary: The National Bamboo Mission (NBM) is a centrally sponsored scheme launched in 2018 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to promote sustainable bamboo cultivation and value-addition across India. The mission aims to develop bamboo as a commercial crop to enhance farmer income, create employment opportunities, and contribute to environmental sustainability.
eligibility_criteria: All categories of farmers including marginal, small, and large-scale cultivators. Land ownership requirement: minimum 0.5 hectare of non-forest, non-agricultural land or degraded/wasteland. Women farmers, SC/ST communities, and landless farmers (through community models) prioritized. Registered Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) and agricultural cooperatives. Government institutions and NGOs engaged in forestry and agriculture development. Individuals with cultivable wasteland or fallow land available for conversion. Age limit: No upper age limit; youth farmers (below 40 years) receive additional incentives.
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers constitute the primary beneficiary group (approximately 70% of target). Women farmers and women SHGs receive special focus. SC/ST communities with land ownership. Landless agricultural laborers through community plantation models. Youth in rural areas for skill development and employment. Forest fringe communities and tribal populations. Cooperatives and Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs). Agricultural entrepreneurs interested in bamboo processing industries.
benefits_provided: Direct income generation through bamboo harvesting after 3-4 years of plantation with annual yields for 40-50 years. Central financial assistance up to ₹45,000 per hectare for plantation (in non-forest areas). Additional support for infrastructure development and post-harvest management. Employment opportunities in plantation establishment, harvesting, and processing. Reduced input costs as bamboo requires minimal fertilizers and pesticides. Improved soil quality and carbon sequestration benefits. Value-addition opportunities through bamboo products manufacturing (handicrafts, boards, flooring, etc.). Market linkage support and capacity building training programs. Technology transfer and technical guidance from subject matter experts.
application_process: Step 1: Registration on eNAM or state agricultural portal with basic farmer details and land information. Step 2: Verification of land documents and eligibility criteria by district level committee. Step 3: Submission of detailed project proposal including plantation plan and timeline. Step 4: Training participation in bamboo cultivation practices (minimum 2-3 days). Step 5: Approval and allotment of bamboo saplings from registered nurseries. Step 6: On-ground plantation monitoring and documentation through GPS coordinates. Step 7: Verification of plantation survival after 6 months by field inspectors. Step 8: Disbursement of financial assistance in 2-3 tranches based on performance. Step 9: Ongoing technical support and monitoring for 3-4 years. Step 10: Value-addition and market linkage support upon maturity. Application submission through district agriculture office or online portal with prescribed forms and supporting documents.
documents_required: Land ownership proof (title deed, patta, record of rights). Aadhaar card and voter ID of applicant. Bank account details (for direct benefit transfer). Land measurement certificate and revenue map. Statement of non-cultivation or wasteland status (from local revenue department). Formal authorization letter (if applying through FPO/cooperative). Nursery registration certificate (if establishing new nursery). Training completion certificate (for plantation establishment technical requirements). Project proposal and implementation plan (for clusters and value-addition units). GST registration (for commercial-scale operations and value-addition).
keywords: bamboo plantation, sustainable agriculture, farmer income, value-addition, agroforestry, non-forest areas, commercial crop, bamboo products, rural development, environmental sustainability, carbon sequestration, renewable material

description: The Restructured Weather Based Crop Insurance Scheme represents a paradigm shift in India's approach to crop insurance. Unlike traditional indemnity-based schemes that require proof of loss, RWBCIS uses objective weather data from automated weather stations to trigger indemnity payments. The scheme covers various weather parameters including excessive rainfall, deficient rainfall, high temperature, low temperature, frost, hail, and wind speed. It eliminates the need for manual crop damage assessment, ensuring faster claim settlement and reduced administrative costs. The scheme operates on the principle that specific adverse weather conditions during critical crop growth periods directly impact yield and productivity.
summary: Restructured Weather Based Crop Insurance Scheme (RWBCIS) is a comprehensive weather-indexed insurance mechanism launched in 2016 to protect farmers against losses due to adverse weather conditions. It provides financial security for crop failures caused by rainfall, temperature, frost, hail, and wind speed, offering a modernized approach to agricultural risk management without requiring crop loss assessment.
eligibility_criteria: All farmers including small and marginal farmers, sharecroppers, and tenant farmers cultivating notified crops in notified areas are eligible. Loanee farmers (those who have availed crop loans from banks) are required to participate. Non-loanee farmers can voluntarily participate. Individual farmers, FPOs, and farmer groups can enroll. There are no restrictions based on land holding size. Farmers must cultivate the notified crops in designated weather station areas for coverage eligibility.
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers comprising approximately 85-90% of beneficiaries, medium and large farmers, tenant farmers, sharecroppers, and landless agricultural laborers engaged in crop cultivation. The scheme particularly targets farmers in rain-fed regions and areas with high weather volatility. Women farmers and farmer groups are specifically encouraged to participate with dedicated outreach programs.
benefits_provided: Comprehensive weather-indexed insurance coverage with a sum insured ranging from ₹20,000 to ₹40,000 per hectare depending on crop type and season. Faster claim settlement within 30-40 days of adverse weather event occurrence. Zero documentation requirement for claims - automated trigger mechanism based on weather data. Reduced premium burden with government subsidies covering 70-90% of premium for small and marginal farmers. Coverage for multiple weather parameters throughout the critical growing season. No requirement for crop loss assessment, eliminating middlemen and ensuring transparent claim processing.
application_process: Registration through nearest Common Service Center (CSC), bank branch, or insurance company office. Farmers can also apply online through official insurance company portals. Enrollment during the notification period (typically before sowing for kharif and before December for rabi crops). Submission of filled insurance proposal form with required documents. Premium payment through bank account debit, cash at designated centers, or digital payment methods. Issuance of insurance certificate and policy document. Farmers receive SMS alerts about weather updates and critical growth stages. Automated weather station network monitors weather parameters continuously. Claims are triggered automatically when pre-defined weather threshold criteria are met for a minimum of 4 consecutive days. SMS notification sent to farmers upon claim triggering. Claim amount credited directly to farmer's bank account within 30-40 days without manual intervention.
documents_required: Valid identification proof (Aadhaar Card, Voter ID, or Driving License), land documents or lease agreement for tenant/sharecropper farmers, crop insurance proposal form (duly filled), bank account details for direct benefit transfer of claims, agricultural census number or district-issued farmer identification, proof of crop cultivation in notified area, land ownership certificate or pattas, passbook for cooperative society members, FPO registration certificate for farmer producer organizations, mobile number for SMS-based alerts and claim notifications.
keywords: Weather Based Crop Insurance, RWBCIS, automated weather stations, weather-indexed insurance, agricultural risk management, premium subsidy, weather parameters, claim settlement, crop protection, farmer security, parametric insurance, rainfall monitoring, temperature-based coverage, drought insurance, agricultural finance, crop loss compensation, weather triggers, digital agriculture, farmer welfare

description: The National Mission on Natural Farming is a transformative agricultural program designed to scale up natural farming practices in India through a mission mode approach. Launched in 2022 with a focus on organic farming and sustainable agriculture, the NMNF aims to improve soil health, reduce input costs, enhance farmer incomes, and promote environmental sustainability. The mission covers approximately 11.37 lakh hectares across all states and union territories, targeting 40 lakh farmers in the initial phase. It integrates traditional farming wisdom with scientific innovations, promoting zero budget natural farming (ZBNF), organic farming certification, and agroforestry. The program provides technical support, training, certification assistance, and financial incentives to help farmers transition to natural farming methods while maintaining productivity and profitability.
summary: The National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF) is a comprehensive initiative launched in 2022 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to promote sustainable agricultural practices and transitioning farmers from chemical-intensive farming to natural farming systems across India.
eligibility_criteria: Eligible beneficiaries include: (1) Small and marginal farmers with landholding of any size; (2) Farmers willing to adopt natural farming practices for minimum 1 year; (3) Farmers with basic literacy capacity; (4) Cooperative societies and farmer producer organizations (FPOs); (5) Women farmer collectives; (6) SC/ST farmers and backward classes; (7) Farmers willing to form cluster groups of minimum 30 farmers; (8) Age criteria: farmers between 18-70 years (age relaxation for SC/ST); (9) No criminal record or default in loan repayment; (10) Farmers not enrolled in similar scheme in last 2 years.
target_beneficiaries: The scheme targets 40 lakh (4 million) farmers in its initial phase, with emphasis on: Small and marginal farmers (60% beneficiaries), Women farmers (35% minimum), SC/ST communities (15% minimum), Youth (below 30 years: 10% minimum), Farmer Producer Organizations and cooperatives, Landless agricultural laborers, Tribal communities, Farmers from drought-prone and rain-fed regions, Farmers in degraded soil areas, North-Eastern states and hilly regions.
benefits_provided: Key benefits include: (1) Direct financial assistance of ₹10,000-15,000 per hectare over 3 years; (2) Cost-free organic certification support; (3) Access to bio-inputs (bio-fertilizers, bio-pesticides) at subsidized rates; (4) Soil health cards and nutrient management plans; (5) Cluster approach providing technical handholding; (6) Market linkage for natural produce; (7) Reduced input costs by 40-50%; (8) Increased farm productivity and income; (9) Improved soil quality and biodiversity; (10) Access to FPO formation support; (11) Insurance coverage; (12) Groundwater recharge improvement; (13) Carbon credit opportunities.
application_process: Application process involves following steps: (1) Registration on state/district agriculture portal or through NMNF online platform; (2) Form submission through Krishi Bhawan portal or district agriculture office; (3) Land verification and inspection by agricultural official; (4) Soil testing at approved laboratory (sample provided by district department); (5) Cluster formation with minimum 30 farmers; (6) Training and capacity building workshop (3-5 days); (7) Documentation verification; (8) Approval by district selection committee; (9) Financial disbursement in installments: 30% (Year 1), 35% (Year 2), 35% (Year 3); (10) Biannual monitoring and impact assessment; (11) Online tracking of application status through portal.
documents_required: Required documents for application: (1) Aadhar card/voter ID; (2) Land ownership documents (Jamabandi/land record); (3) Bank account details (IFSC code and account number); (4) Passport-size photographs (4 copies); (5) Land survey number and cadastral map; (6) Income certificate; (7) Caste certificate (if applicable); (8) FPO registration certificate (if applying as collective); (9) Farmer registration number; (10) Soil test report from approved laboratory; (11) Declaration form acknowledging natural farming adoption; (12) Consent letter for cluster group participation; (13) Mobile number and email for communication.
keywords: natural farming, organic farming, zero budget natural farming, ZBNF, bio-inputs, sustainable agriculture, soil health, organic certification, farmer clusters, FPO, Ministry of Agriculture, cost reduction, chemical-free farming, agroforestry, bio-fertilizers, bio-pesticides, traditional farming, climate resilient agriculture, groundwater conservation, carbon credits, crop diversification, farm profitability, environmental sustainability, India agriculture scheme, chemical farming transition

description: The Direct Benefit Transfer for Fertilizers (DBT-F) scheme is a transformative initiative that restructures the fertilizer subsidy delivery mechanism in India. Implemented since January 2018, the scheme leverages digital technology and direct bank transfers to provide fertilizer subsidies to eligible farmers. The scheme operates on the principle of providing subsidy directly to farmers' bank accounts rather than through the traditional dealer network, thereby reducing intermediaries, curbing black marketing, and ensuring transparent fund utilization. Under DBT-F, farmers registered under the e-Urea portal can purchase fertilizers from authorized dealers using digital cards (e-NAM, mKisan, or SMS-based systems), and subsidies are credited directly to their linked bank accounts. The scheme covers essential nutrients including Urea, DAP (Diammonium Phosphate), MOP (Muriate of Potash), and NPK (Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium) in various formulations. The government maintains price control on fertilizer maximum retail price (MRP) while subsidy is transferred directly, ensuring affordability and accessibility for farmers across diverse agro-climatic regions.
summary: Direct Benefit Transfer for Fertilizers (DBT-F) is a flagship subsidy scheme launched in 2018 by the Ministry of Chemicals & Fertilizers, Government of India. This scheme aims to streamline the delivery of fertilizer subsidies directly to farmers' bank accounts while simultaneously reducing subsidy burden on the government and minimizing logistical inefficiencies. The scheme covers urea and other fertilizers, promoting nutrient use efficiency and sustainable agricultural practices through digital integration and transparency.
eligibility_criteria: Farmers with valid Aadhaar number and active bank account linked to their Aadhaar through eKYC; Registered users on e-Urea portal or PM-KISAN database; Agricultural land holding records verified through state agriculture departments; Farmers with valid Kisan Credit Card (KCC) or deemed eligible by state governments; Small and marginal farmers prioritized; Tenant farmers with documented lease agreements; Farmers in scheduled and non-scheduled areas including hilly and tribal regions; No income limit restrictions; Farmers with existing fertilizer subsidy records from previous schemes; Those registered in National Agriculture Market (e-NAM) platforms; Residents of India with legitimate agricultural interests

benefits_provided: Direct subsidy transfer to farmers' bank accounts within 24-48 hours of purchase; Reduction in fertilizer costs for farmers, increasing affordability and nutrient application; Elimination of dealer margins and black-market practices; Enhanced transparency with digital trail of all transactions; Prevention of diversion of subsidized fertilizers to non-agricultural uses; Improved nutrient use efficiency through balanced fertilizer application; Encouragement of precision agriculture and soil-health card recommendations; Reduced fiscal burden on government, allowing resource reallocation to other farm schemes; Easy access to fertilizers through multiple digital channels (e-Urea portal, SMS, mobile apps); Integration with PM-KISAN and other welfare schemes for simplified farmer registration; Farmer choice in selecting appropriate fertilizer brands and types; Real-time tracking of subsidy disbursement and fertilizer distribution; Reduced waiting time and elimination of long dealer queues; Support for sustainable agriculture through nutrient-balanced crop nutrition; Better planning for state governments through digital demand forecasting; Strengthened farmer-to-market linkage through registered dealers only

description: Per Drop More Crop is a comprehensive irrigation management initiative designed to address water scarcity and improve farm productivity in drought-prone and water-stressed regions of India. Launched as part of the National Mission on Micro-Irrigation (NMMI), the scheme provides financial assistance and technical support to farmers for installing micro-irrigation systems including drip irrigation, sprinkler systems, and other water-efficient technologies. The PDMC scheme works in coordination with state governments to identify beneficiaries, facilitate technology transfer, and monitor implementation at grassroots level. The scheme emphasizes water conservation, reducing input costs, and enhancing sustainability of agricultural practices across diverse agro-climatic zones.
summary: Per Drop More Crop (PDMC) is a centrally sponsored scheme launched in 2015 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to promote micro-irrigation systems across India. The scheme aims to increase water-use efficiency and agricultural productivity by encouraging farmers to adopt drip and sprinkler irrigation technologies, thereby maximizing crop yield per unit of water consumed.
eligibility_criteria:
1. Individual farmers with minimum 0.5 hectares of cultivable land (0.25 hectares for hilly/tribal areas)
2. Farmer groups, FPOs, and cooperative societies
3. Land should have reliable water source (well, bore well, tank, canal, or pond)
4. No farmer should have received similar assistance in preceding 5 years
5. Farmers with existing drip/sprinkler systems covering less than 100% of land area are eligible for additional area coverage
6. SC/ST and marginal farmers given priority and differential subsidy rates
7. Women farmers eligible for 40% reservation under various state allocations
8. No upper limit on land holding (in some states) or up to 10 hectares (in others)
target_beneficiaries:
1. Small and marginal farmers in drought-prone regions
2. Farmers in water-stressed areas with irregular rainfall patterns
3. SC/ST communities and economically weaker sections
4. Women farmers and landless farmers
5. Agricultural workers and tenant farmers (where applicable)
6. Farmers cultivating high-value crops (vegetables, horticulture, spices)
7. Farmers in areas with depleting groundwater tables
8. Rainfed and partially irrigated farming communities
9. Farmers in tribal and hilly regions
10. Communities facing water conflicts and management challenges
benefits_provided:
1. Subsidy ranging from 50-80% of total project cost depending on farmer category and state policies
2. For SC/ST/landless/marginal farmers: up to 80% subsidy
3. For small and medium farmers: up to 60-70% subsidy
4. Water savings of 30-60% compared to traditional flood irrigation
5. Crop yield increase by 30-50% depending on crop type
6. Reduction in input costs (fertilizer, pesticides, water charges) by 15-25%
7. Reduction in labor requirements by 20-30%
8. Flexibility to irrigate even on sloped and undulating terrain
9. Reduced pest and disease incidence due to controlled water application
10. Eligibility for additional benefits from state schemes and PMKSY-RFR components
11. Access to technical training and demonstration facilities
12. Priority support for solar panel installation for pump operation
application_process:
1. Registration Phase: Interested farmers register with designated state/district agricultural department offices or through online portals maintained by state governments
2. Verification: Field verification of land and water source availability conducted by appointed officials (revenue or irrigation department)
3. Application Submission: Farmers submit completed application forms (offline or online) with required documents and land details
4. Technical Survey: Site assessment by trained technician/engineer to determine micro-irrigation system design and cost estimation
5. Selection: Beneficiary selection through transparent merit-based criteria or lottery system as per state guidelines
6. Subsidy Disbursement: Subsidy released in installments (usually 50% on approval, 50% on completion with third-party verification)
7. Installation: Farmers procure approved equipment from authorized vendors or through government supply chains
8. Commissioning: Installed system verified and commissioned with technical assistance from scheme staff
9. Training: Farmers receive training on system operation, maintenance, and water management practices
10. Monitoring: Regular follow-up and monitoring for 2-3 years post-installation to ensure proper utilization
documents_required:
1. Land ownership proof: Patta, Jamabandi, or revenue record (original + 2 copies)
2. Identity proof: Aadhar card, voter ID, or PAN card
3. Address proof: Electricity bill, water bill, or passport
4. Bank account details: Cancelled cheque or bank passbook for subsidy transfer
5. Caste certificate (for SC/ST farmers availing enhanced subsidy)
6. Income certificate (for priority consideration in some states)
7. Copy of agricultural census if available
8. Photocopy of relevant pages from land registry
9. Sketch/map of field indicating boundaries and water source location
10. Photographs of the plot and existing water source
11. No Objection Certificate from lessor (in case of leased land, where applicable)
12. Agricultural credit card or Kisan Credit Card (if applicable)
13. Declaration form signed by farmer regarding previous assistance
14. Soil and water testing reports (recommended)
keywords: Micro-irrigation, Drip irrigation, Sprinkler irrigation, Water conservation, PDMC, Agricultural productivity, Farmer support, Subsidy scheme, Water-use efficiency, Irrigation infrastructure, Sustainable agriculture, Groundwater management, High-value crops, Crop intensification, Government assistance, Ministry of Agriculture, PMKSY, Agricultural transformation, Water-stress management, Rural development

description: The Livestock Insurance Scheme is an initiative designed to provide financial protection to livestock owners across India. Launched in 2006, LIS operates under the Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry & Dairying and aims to minimize risks associated with livestock rearing. The scheme covers various categories of farm animals including cattle, buffalo, sheep, goat, and pig against mortality and permanent total disablement due to accidents, diseases, and natural calamities. The insurance coverage provides compensation based on the market value of the insured animal, helping farmers recover from financial losses and maintain sustainable livelihoods. The scheme is implemented through authorized insurance companies and cooperative societies, ensuring accessibility across rural and urban areas. Premium subsidies are provided to small and marginal farmers to make the scheme affordable and inclusive.
summary: The Livestock Insurance Scheme (LIS) is a comprehensive animal insurance program launched in 2006 by the Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry & Dairying to protect farmers and livestock owners against financial losses due to death or disability of their livestock assets. The scheme provides subsidized premium rates and easy claim settlement processes to encourage livestock farmers to adopt scientific insurance practices.
ministry_department: Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry & Dairying
eligibility_criteria: 1. Livestock owners: All farmers (small, marginal, and large-scale) engaged in cattle, buffalo, sheep, goat, and pig rearing are eligible. 2. Farm size: No minimum or maximum landholding requirement. 3. Age: Livestock owners must be above 18 years of age. 4. Residency: Indian citizens and Indian registered entities engaged in livestock farming. 5. Animal health: Livestock must be in good health condition without any existing illness or injury at the time of enrollment. 6. Documentation: Valid identification proof and livestock ownership documents are required. 7. Special provisions: Scheduled Caste (SC), Scheduled Tribe (ST), and Below Poverty Line (BPL) farmers receive additional premium subsidies of up to 70-80%.
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers (85%), dairy farmers (15%), shepherds and goat herders in rural areas, cooperative livestock farming groups, animal husbandry entrepreneurs, women farmers engaged in animal husbandry, SC/ST farming communities, livestock owners in hilly and tribal regions, farmers in drought-prone areas, agricultural laborers maintaining livestock for livelihood security. Total estimated beneficiaries: 2.5-3 million livestock farmers across India.
benefits_provided: 1. Mortality Coverage: Full compensation up to 80-100% of Insured Declared Value (IDV) if livestock dies due to covered perils. 2. Permanent Total Disablement: Compensation for animals becoming permanently unfit for work or production. 3. Premium Subsidy: Central government subsidizes 50% of premium for general farmers; up to 70% for SC/ST farmers; 80% for Below Poverty Line (BPL) farmers. 4. Multiple Coverage Options: Farmers can insure single animals or entire herd under annual policies. 5. Seasonal Flexibility: Policy coverage available for 12-month period with option to renew annually. 6. Quick Claim Settlement: Claims processed within 30-45 days post-documentation. 7. No Age Restriction: Coverage available for animals from 6 months to breeding age. 8. Accident & Disease Coverage: Protection against accidental injuries, infectious and non-infectious diseases. 9. Natural Calamity Coverage: Protection against losses from floods, earthquakes, storms, and other natural disasters. 10. Hospital and Treatment: Coverage includes veterinary care expenses up to specified limits. 11. Herd Management Support: Advisory services on animal health and scientific livestock rearing practices. 12. Flexible Payment: Premium can be paid annually, semi-annually, or in installments.
application_process: Step 1: Visit nearest insurance company office or authorized cooperative society operating LIS. Step 2: Obtain detailed scheme information and policy documents in local language. Step 3: Assess livestock health through veterinary examination (free assessment camp or private veterinarian). Step 4: Collect required documents: (a) Identity proof (Aadhaar, PAN, Voter ID), (b) Livestock ownership certificate, (c) Vaccination records, (d) Photographs of animals, (e) Land ownership/lease documents, (f) Bank account details for claim disbursement. Step 5: Complete application form (available in Hindi/English/regional languages) with accurate livestock information including breed, age, and market value. Step 6: Calculate insurance amount based on current market value; insurance company will determine Insured Declared Value (IDV). Step 7: Pay premium: Government will credit subsidy to bank account; farmer pays balance amount directly or through automatic deduction. Step 8: Receive policy certificate within 7-10 days; policy becomes effective from the date premium is fully paid. Step 9: Register livestock with unique identification number (ear tag or tattoo) for tracking. Step 10: Keep policy document safe; submit claim form within 72 hours of loss along with supporting documents.
documents_required: 1. Identity and Residency: Aadhaar Card, PAN, Voter ID, Passport, or Driving License. 2. Livestock Ownership: Original pedigree certificates, vaccination cards, livestock ownership affidavit from village sarpanch/gram panchayat. 3. Land Documents: Land ownership deed, lease agreement, or land possession certificate. 4. Medical/Health Records: Veterinary health certificate dated within 30 days before enrollment, vaccination certificates, treatment history (if any). 5. Photographs: High-resolution colored photographs of livestock from multiple angles showing identifying marks. 6. Bank Details: Passbook copy, bank statement (last 3 months), or cancelled cheque for direct transfer of claims. 7. Income Proof: For SC/ST/BPL farmers: Income certificate from Gram Panchayat/Taluk office. 8. Application Form: Completed form duly signed by farmer with thumb impression if illiterate. 9. Claim Documents (at time of claim): FIR copy in case of theft, veterinary death certificate, post-mortem report, photographs of dead animal, insurance policy copy, claim form, death/disability documentation.
