The initiative aims to benefit approximately 100,000+ direct beneficiaries through direct financial support and indirect beneficiaries through pollination services and agricultural productivity enhancement. Special emphasis is given to tribal regions and forest-adjacent communities where apiculture serves as both an income source and forest conservation mechanism.
benefits_provided:
1. Financial assistance of up to ₹40,000-₹60,000 per unit for establishment of bee colonies (typically 4 bee colonies per unit)
2. 50% subsidy on modern beekeeping equipment including bee boxes, protective gear, and honey extraction equipment
3. Free training and capacity building programs on scientific beekeeping practices
4. Technical support and extension services from trained apiculture experts
5. Research and development support for improved bee varieties and management practices
6. Market linkage assistance for honey and bee products (propolis, bee pollen, royal jelly)
7. Quality certification and food safety standards support
8. Cold storage and processing unit establishment support up to ₹5 lakhs
9. Group activity support for FPOs and cooperatives with enhanced subsidy rates (60%)
10. Insurance schemes covering bee colonies and apiculture equipment
11. Access to credit facilities through National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD)
12. Environmental benefits including improved crop pollination and forest ecosystem health
13. Organic certification support for bee products
14. International market exposure and export promotion assistance
application_process:
Step 1: Identification and Registration - Interested beneficiaries register with State Agricultural Department or District Apiculture Officer
Step 2: Eligibility Verification - Submission of KYC documents, proof of land ownership/agricultural activity, income certificate, and SC/ST certificate (if applicable)
Step 3: Farm Survey - On-site assessment of proposed apiculture site for suitability (flora diversity, water sources, accessibility)
Step 4: Training Enrollment - Mandatory participation in 5-7 days basic beekeeping training organized at block or district level
Step 5: Application Submission - Formal application through Online Agriculture Scheme Management System (OASMS) or district office with supporting documents
Step 6: Scrutiny and Approval - District selection committee reviews applications and grants provisional approval
Step 7: Fund Disbursement - First installment (50%) released on approval, remaining disbursed upon submission of purchase receipts and execution certificates
Step 8: Procurement - Beneficiary purchases approved bee colonies, equipment from authorized suppliers
Step 9: Monitoring and Support - Regular follow-up visits by field supervisors for 2 years post-installation
Step 10: Performance Review - Annual assessment of bee colony survival and productivity metrics
documents_required:
1. Aadhaar Card and PAN (Permanent Account Number)
2. Voter ID or any other Government recognized identification
3. Land ownership documents (Khata, revenue receipt) or land lease agreement
4. Educational qualification certificate (minimum 8th pass for literacy)
5. Caste certificate (for SC/ST/OBC beneficiaries, if applicable)
6. Income certificate from Gram Panchayat (for economically weaker sections)
7. Bank account passbook copy (for direct benefit transfer)
8. Passport-sized photographs (4 copies)
9. Medical fitness certificate (if required by state)
10. No Objection Certificate (NOC) from local forest authority (for forest-adjacent areas)
11. Proof of participation in basic beekeeping training
12. Site suitability report prepared by district apiculture officer
13. Purchase quotations from authorized equipment suppliers
14. Certificate of completion from recognized apiculture training institute
15. Proof of insurance (when applicable)

description: The Integrated Farming System Model (IFSM) is a comprehensive agricultural development scheme launched in 2017 by the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare. It aims to promote sustainable and diversified farming practices by integrating crop production, livestock, fisheries, and horticulture on individual farms. The scheme encourages farmers to adopt climate-resilient practices while maintaining soil health and ensuring income stability throughout the year.
eligibility_criteria: Small and marginal farmers with landholding up to 2 hectares; farmer groups and cooperatives with collective landholding up to 5 hectares; farmers engaged in agriculture for minimum 1 year; age between 18-65 years; willingness to adopt integrated farming practices and maintain records for monitoring; preference given to SC/ST and women farmers in reserved categories
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers, landless agricultural laborers, farmer producer organizations, women farmer groups, socially disadvantaged sections (SC/ST), youth in agriculture, resource-poor farming communities, dairy and poultry entrepreneurs, integrated fish-farm operators
benefits_provided: Provision of subsidy up to 50% for equipment and infrastructure development; financial assistance of Rs. 4 lakh per hectare for establishing integrated farming units; supply of quality seeds, seedlings, and livestock breeding material; training and capacity building programs; technical support and advisory services; certification for organic farming practices; marketing linkages and value addition facilities; crop and livestock insurance schemes; access to micro-credit facilities with subsidized interest rates
application_process: Registration through state agriculture department or designated IFSM implementing agency; submission of application form with supporting documents at block/district agricultural office; farm survey and verification by agricultural extension officer; approval of integrated farming plan by district committee; disbursement of first installment (50%) of subsidy upon commencement of work; submission of progress reports every quarter; final inspection and disbursement of remaining subsidy (50%) upon successful implementation; ongoing monitoring through mGov platform
documents_required: Land ownership/lease deed documents or RTC (Record of Transfer Certificate); Aadhaar card and PAN (where applicable); bank account details and passbook copy; photographs of the farmer and land; certificate from Gram Panchayat regarding farmer status and land area; farm plan and proposed integrated farming design; income certificate for subsidy eligibility; SC/ST certificate if applicable; agricultural loan documents if any
keywords: Integrated Farming System, Sustainable Agriculture, Climate-Resilient Farming, Diversified Farming, Soil Health, Livestock Integration, Fisheries Integration, Horticulture, Organic Farming, Resource Conservation, Farm Productivity, Income Generation, Environmental Sustainability, Government Subsidy Scheme

description: The Sprinkler Irrigation Scheme (SIS) is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme launched in 2010 by the Government of India to promote efficient irrigation through sprinkler systems. The scheme aims to provide financial assistance to farmers for installation of sprinkler irrigation systems to enhance water use efficiency, reduce water consumption, and increase agricultural productivity. The program focuses on areas with water scarcity and supports sustainable agricultural practices through modern irrigation technology.
summary: A central government initiative promoting sprinkler irrigation adoption among Indian farmers to improve water efficiency and crop productivity in water-scarce regions.
eligibility_criteria: Farmers with cultivable land holding between 0.5 to 10 hectares; priority given to farmers in water-scarce and drought-prone areas; SC/ST farmers and small and marginal farmers receive preferential consideration; farmers with valid Aadhar number and land ownership/lease documents; farmers not availing similar benefits from other government schemes in the last 5 years
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers, landholding farmers in water-scarce regions, SC/ST farmers, women farmers, farmers in command areas of surface irrigation projects, farmers with shallow tube wells and bore wells, agricultural communities in drought-prone districts
benefits_provided: Financial subsidy up to 55-60% of sprinkler system installation cost for small/marginal farmers and SC/ST farmers; 40-50% subsidy for other farmers; loan facility at concessional rates from scheduled banks; training and demonstrations on sprinkler irrigation practices; technical guidance from agricultural extension officers; reduction in water consumption by 30-60%; increased crop yield by 20-40%; reduced labor cost for irrigation; eligibility for combined subsidy under multiple schemes
application_process: Registration on State Agriculture Department portal or Common Service Centers (CSCs); submission of filled application form with required documents to block-level Agriculture/Horticulture office; scrutiny and eligibility verification by District Agriculture Officer within 15 days; approval and grant of subsidy sanction order; farmer purchases sprinkler system from authorized dealers/AIBP registered vendors; submission of original bills and photographs for verification; final inspection and subsidy credit to bank account within 90 days of completion; post-installation monitoring and support through agricultural extension services
documents_required: Aadhar card, Land ownership deed or lease agreement, Agricultural land certificate issued by village authority, Passbook of bank account (preferably rural/agricultural branch), Caste certificate (for SC/ST farmers), Land revenue records (Form 7A/7B), Income certificate, Photograph (2 passport size), Water source certificate from competent authority, Application form duly filled and signed, Proof of non-availing similar scheme benefits
keywords: Sprinkler Irrigation, Water Conservation, Efficient Farming, Agricultural Technology, Irrigation Systems, Water-Saving, Sustainable Agriculture, Subsidy, Farmers

description: The Protected Cultivation Scheme is a comprehensive government initiative designed to transform Indian agriculture by promoting the adoption of protected cultivation structures such as polyhouses, shade nets, and naturally ventilated greenhouses. Launched by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, this scheme provides financial assistance and technical support to farmers for establishing modern greenhouse infrastructure. The scheme encourages the cultivation of high-value crops including tomatoes, capsicum, cucumber, strawberries, carnations, and spices in controlled environments, thereby increasing yield per unit area by 4-10 times compared to open field cultivation.
summary: The Protected Cultivation Scheme (PCS) is a centrally sponsored initiative launched in 2011 to promote modern greenhouse farming techniques in India. The scheme aims to increase agricultural productivity and farmers' income through climate-controlled cultivation of vegetables, flowers, and spice crops while ensuring sustainable resource management.
eligibility_criteria: Individual farmers, farmer groups, FPOs, and agricultural entrepreneurs with 1-5 acres of cultivable land; Valid Aadhar, land ownership documents, and bank account; Preference given to SC/ST farmers and women farmers; No upper limit on annual income; Willingness to adopt recommended horticultural practices; At least high school education is preferred but not mandatory
target_beneficiaries: Progressive farmers engaged in commercial horticulture, landless agricultural laborers through group farming, women farmers and self-help groups, scheduled caste and scheduled tribe farmers, farmer producer organizations, young entrepreneurs in agriculture (18-40 years), farming communities in hilly and coastal regions requiring crop protection
benefits_provided: Financial assistance covering 50% cost of greenhouse structure for general farmers and 75% for SC/ST and women farmers (maximum Rs. 50 lakh per unit); Construction and installation of polyhouses, shade nets, and naturally ventilated greenhouses; Supply of certified seeds, seedlings, and planting material; Drip irrigation and micro-sprinkler systems; Training programs on protected cultivation and crop management; Subsidy on agricultural inputs including nutrients and pest management solutions; Reduced water consumption by 30-50% compared to open field farming; 3-4 crop cycles per year in protected structures; Yield increase of 400-1000% depending on crop type; Market linkages and value addition facilities
application_process: Step 1: Farmer registration on State Agricultural Department portal or offline at nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra/Agriculture Extension Office. Step 2: Submission of application with required documents in prescribed format. Step 3: Verification of land ownership, economic status, and technical feasibility by field inspector. Step 4: Approval of application and provisional allocation of subsidy by District Agriculture Officer. Step 5: Farmer places order for greenhouse materials from approved vendors/nurseries. Step 6: On-site inspection of completed structure and certification by Agricultural Officer. Step 7: Subsidy disbursement through Direct Bank Transfer (DBT) in maximum 2 tranches (50% after foundation, 50% after completion). Step 8: Mandatory training and technical guidance period of 6 months.
documents_required: Land ownership/lease deed documents; Photo identity proof (Aadhar/PAN/Voter ID); Bank account passbook with IFSC code; Land survey document with measurement details; Passport-size photographs (3-4); Agricultural income certificate from Gram Panchayat; Work experience certificate (for farmer groups); Proof of availability of water source for irrigation; No Objection Certificate from local authorities; Quotation from registered nurseries/suppliers for planting material

description: The Drip Irrigation Scheme is a comprehensive initiative designed to support farmers in adopting advanced micro-irrigation technologies, particularly drip irrigation systems. Launched in 2010 as a Centrally Sponsored Scheme, it aims to address water scarcity challenges in agriculture by promoting efficient water-use technologies. The scheme provides financial assistance to farmers for installation of drip irrigation systems in their fields, covering both the cost of equipment and technical support. It is implemented across multiple states in collaboration with State Governments and aims to increase irrigation coverage, reduce groundwater depletion, and improve farm productivity while promoting sustainable agricultural practices.
summary: The Drip Irrigation Scheme (DIS) is a Centrally Sponsored agricultural initiative launched in 2010 under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to promote efficient water management and increase agricultural productivity through micro-irrigation technologies. description: The Precision Farming Development (PFD) scheme is a central sector initiative launched in 2018 to promote adoption of precision agriculture technologies across India. The scheme aims to enhance agricultural productivity and sustainability by enabling farmers to use data-driven farming practices, soil moisture sensors, GPS-guided equipment, variable rate technology, and farm management software. PFD facilitates the deployment of modern precision agriculture infrastructure in villages and farming clusters to optimize resource utilization, reduce input costs, and increase crop yields while promoting environmental sustainability.
summary: Precision Farming Development is a technology-driven central sector scheme designed to modernize Indian agriculture through precision farming technologies. description: The Solar Pump Scheme is a government-backed renewable energy initiative designed to facilitate the transition of agricultural irrigation systems from conventional fuels to solar power. The scheme provides financial assistance and subsidies to eligible farmers for the installation of solar pumps, helping to reduce agricultural expenditure on electricity and diesel while contributing to environmental sustainability. The scheme encompasses both standalone solar pumping systems and grid-connected solar installations for irrigation purposes across India.
summary: The Solar Pump Scheme (SPS) is a central sector initiative launched in 2019 by the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) to promote sustainable agricultural practices. The scheme aims to replace diesel and electric-based irrigation pumps with solar-powered alternatives, reducing farmers' operational costs and promoting renewable energy adoption in the agricultural sector.
eligibility_criteria: Farmers who own agricultural land and are involved in irrigation agriculture; Land holding size requirement of minimum 0.5 hectares for standalone systems; Farmers with valid land records and proof of ownership or lease documents; Farmers in designated solar-suitable geographical regions; Age requirement: minimum 18 years; Farmers not previously benefited under similar central government renewable energy schemes within the last 5 years; Priority given to marginal and small farmers; SC/ST farmers eligible for additional benefits

description: The Farm Pond Scheme is designed to support farmers in constructing farm ponds with dimensions typically 40m × 30m × 3m (3,600 cubic meters capacity). The scheme provides financial assistance and technical guidance to create sustainable water harvesting structures that reduce dependency on seasonal rainfall and groundwater depletion. Farm ponds serve as effective water retention systems for both surface runoff and subterranean water, significantly enhancing agricultural resilience in semi-arid and drought-prone regions.
summary: The Farm Pond Scheme (FPS) is a centrally sponsored irrigation initiative launched in 2016 to promote water harvesting and conservation at the farm level. It aims to facilitate the construction of farm ponds to store rainwater and groundwater, enabling year-round agricultural productivity through improved irrigation infrastructure.
eligibility_criteria: Marginal and small farmers (land holding ≤2 hectares), medium farmers (land holding 2-10 hectares), and agricultural laborers. Priority given to farmers in drought-prone, water-scarce, and rainfed regions. SC/ST farmers, women farmers, and farmers in backward districts receive preferential consideration. Farmers must have cultivable land and must not have availed similar benefits under other schemes in the past 5 years.
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers (primary), agricultural laborers, tenant farmers, women farmers engaged in agriculture, farming communities in water-deficit regions, farmers in MGNREGA convergence areas
benefits_provided: Financial assistance covering 75-90% of farm pond construction cost (remaining 10-25% as farmer's contribution), Construction of water storage structures with 3,600-4,000 cubic meters capacity, Promotion of sustainable irrigation and improved crop productivity, Reduction in groundwater extraction and enhanced water table, Support for cultivation of high-value crops through assured irrigation, Training and capacity building for farm pond maintenance, Availability of technical assistance for site selection and construction quality, Reduced dependency on monsoon rainfall, Enhanced agricultural income generation, Convergence with MGNREGA for labor employment
application_process: Step 1: Farmer obtains application form from state agricultural department/district revenue office or official website. Step 2: Complete the form with all required details including land particulars, water situation analysis, and proposed farm pond specifications. Step 3: Attach all mandatory documents and get the form notarized/endorsed by block development officer. Step 4: Submit the application to block headquarters or taluk office during announced notification periods. Step 5: Field verification conducted by agricultural/revenue officials to assess land eligibility and water availability. Step 6: Approval granted based on criteria compliance and fund availability. Step 7: Sanction order issued with disbursement details and construction timeline. Step 8: Farmer proceeds with construction as per approved design and technical specifications. Step 9: Construction progress monitored by appointed engineer and district officials. Step 10: Final inspection and fund disbursement upon completion and verification.
documents_required: Land ownership documents (Bhu-naksha, Khatauni, Lease agreement), Aadhar Card and voter ID, Bank account details (preferably with passbook), Caste certificate (if applicable for SC/ST preference), Latest land revenue receipts/tax payment proof, Passport-sized photographs (4 nos.), Application form (prescribed by state/central authorities), Certificate of non-utilization of similar benefits in past 5 years, Land survey report and contour map (for site identification), PAN Card (if applicable)
keywords: Farm Pond, Water Harvesting, Irrigation, Rainwater Conservation, Groundwater, Sustainable Agriculture, Water Storage, Drought Management, Agricultural Infrastructure, Centrally Sponsored Scheme

description: Custom Hiring Centres are business units established at village, block, or cluster level that own and rent out modern agricultural machinery and implements to farmers on hourly, daily, or seasonal basis. The scheme aims to bridge the mechanization gap by making expensive equipment accessible to small and marginal farmers who cannot afford individual ownership. CHCs are supported by government grants and operated by entrepreneurs, cooperatives, self-help groups, or farmer producer organizations.
summary: Custom Hiring Centres (CHC) is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme launched in 2018 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to promote agricultural mechanization through equipment rental services. The scheme establishes infrastructure for farmers to access modern agricultural machinery on hire basis, reducing individual ownership costs and promoting adoption of mechanized farming practices across India.
eligibility_criteria: Eligible entities include: (1) Individuals with agricultural background or experience in farm mechanization; (2) Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) and agricultural cooperatives; (3) Self-Help Groups (SHGs) registered with relevant authorities; (4) Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) working in agricultural development; (5) Landless agricultural laborers and small/marginal farmers willing to establish CHCs. Applicants must be Indian citizens aged 18-65 years and able to demonstrate basic technical knowledge or willingness to undergo training.
target_beneficiaries: Primary beneficiaries are small and marginal farmers (holding less than 2 hectares), landless agricultural laborers, and tenant farmers lacking access to mechanized farming. Secondary beneficiaries include women farmers, scheduled castes and scheduled tribes engaged in agriculture, and rural entrepreneurs seeking to establish equipment rental businesses.
benefits_provided: Direct benefits include: (1) Government grant of up to 40-50% of total equipment cost (varies by state); (2) Subsidy on custom hiring services for farmers; (3) Access to modern agricultural machinery without ownership burden; (4) Reduced operational costs through shared machinery usage; (5) Training and capacity building for CHC operators and farmers. Indirect benefits comprise: (1) Increased agricultural productivity and crop yields; (2) Timely farm operations ensuring better crop quality; (3) Employment generation at village level; (4) Women farmer empowerment through improved technology access; (5) Promotion of sustainable farming practices; (6) Digital payment infrastructure development in rural areas.
application_process: Step 1: Prospective entrepreneurs/organizations obtain application forms from District Agriculture Office or download from state agriculture department website. Step 2: Fill application form with project details, equipment list, financial plan, and farmer demand assessment. Step 3: Submit application to District Agriculture Officer with all required documents. Step 4: District-level committee reviews applications based on location suitability, demand, and applicant credentials (typically 30-45 days). Step 5: Selected applicants receive approval and sanction letter with grant amount and equipment specifications. Step 6: Applicant opens dedicated bank account for fund receipt. Step 7: Funds are released in installments (usually 40% at approval, 40% after equipment procurement, 20% after operational verification). Step 8: Applicant procures equipment from authorized dealers as per approved specifications. Step 9: Conducts training program for staff and farmers. Step 10: Formally inaugurates CHC and registers for performance monitoring.
documents_required: Essential documents include: (1) Aadhar Card and PAN Card of applicant; (2) Land possession certificate or lease agreement for CHC establishment; (3) Educational qualifications and agricultural experience certificate; (4) Project report detailing equipment procurement plan and financial projections; (5) Bank account details and passbook; (6) No Objection Certificate from local agricultural officer; (7) Registration certificate if applicant is part of cooperative/FPO/NGO; (8) Technical specification sheets of proposed machinery; (9) Business plan with farmer demand assessment; (10) Proof of training completion in machinery operation (if available).

description: The Farm Mechanization Subsidy Scheme is a comprehensive initiative under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare designed to bridge the gap between traditional and modern farming practices. Launched in 2014, the scheme provides direct subsidies to individual farmers and support to Custom Hiring Centers (CHCs) for procuring agricultural machinery such as tractors, harvesters, pump sets, threshers, and other implements. The scheme operates across multiple states with varying subsidy amounts based on state-specific policies and farm holdings. It encourages the adoption of precision farming technologies and sustainable agricultural practices while creating rural employment opportunities through equipment rental services.
summary: The Farm Mechanization Subsidy (FMS) is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme launched in 2014 to promote agricultural mechanization across India. The scheme aims to increase farm productivity, reduce labor costs, and enhance agricultural efficiency by providing financial assistance to farmers for the purchase of modern agricultural machinery and equipment.
eligibility_criteria:
1. Indian citizens engaged in agricultural activities
2. Small and Marginal Farmers (priority category): Landholding up to 2 hectares
3. Other Farmers: Landholding up to 10 hectares
4. Women farmers given priority in selection
5. SC/ST farmers eligible with special consideration
6. Farmer Groups, FPOs, and Producer Organizations
7. Custom Hiring Centers with minimum 5 years operational experience
8. Farmers should not have received similar subsidy in last 5 years
9. Minimum age: 18 years
10. No upper age limit for agricultural operations
target_beneficiaries:
1. Small and Marginal Farmers (primary beneficiaries): 2-3 million farmers
2. Landless Agricultural Laborers: Through Custom Hiring Centers
3. Women Farmers: Special focus with 30-40% allocation
4. SC/ST Communities: Preferential treatment in selection
5. Farming Communities in Backward Districts
6. Cooperative Societies and Farmer Producer Organizations
7. Custom Hiring Center Operators and Entrepreneurs
8. Young Farmers: Encouraged to adopt modern farming
9. Tenant Farmers and Share Croppers: In select states
10. Total Impact: Benefit of approximately 8-10 million farmers indirectly through Custom Hiring Centers
benefits_provided:
1. Direct subsidy coverage: 40-50% of machinery cost for small/marginal farmers; 25-33% for other farmers
2. Maximum subsidy limit: Rs. 1.5 lakh to Rs. 3 lakh depending on equipment type and state
3. Subsidized purchase of tractors, combine harvesters, threshers, sprayers, and seed drills
4. Support for Custom Hiring Centers: Up to 80% subsidy for equipment procurement
5. Reduced agricultural input costs and improved farm productivity
6. Enhanced crop yield and reduced post-harvest losses
7. Easy payment through installments for eligible farmers
8. Training programs on equipment operation and maintenance
9. Technical support and after-sales service facilitation
10. Promotion of farm-to-market efficiency through mechanization
application_process:
1. Registration: Farmer registers on official state agriculture portal or Common Service Centers (CSCs)
2. Online Application: Complete application form with personal and land details
3. Verification: District Agriculture Office verifies eligibility and land records
4. Selection: Beneficiaries selected through lottery or merit-based system (state-specific)
5. Approval: Selected farmers notified within 30 days
6. Machinery Selection: Farmer purchases equipment from authorized dealers
7. Subsidy Release: First installment (50%) released on submission of purchase invoice
8. Equipment Installation: Technical team ensures proper installation
9. Final Verification: District officer conducts final inspection
10. Final Subsidy: Remaining amount (50%) released after verification
11. Registration: Equipment registered with farmer's name
12. Support: Follow-up for maintenance and operation training

6. Caste Certificate (for SC/ST/OBC farmers)

description: The Agri-Clinic Entrepreneurship scheme aims to bridge the gap between research and farming communities by fostering agricultural entrepreneurs who can provide specialized advisory services, input supply, and value-added agricultural products. The scheme targets educated agricultural graduates and diploma holders, providing them with training, financial assistance, and handholding support to establish viable agricultural service enterprises in rural and semi-urban areas. These clinics serve as important nodes for disseminating improved agricultural technologies and practices to farmers.
summary: The Agri-Clinic Entrepreneurship scheme is a central sector initiative launched in 2002 to promote entrepreneurship in agricultural services. It facilitates the establishment of Agri-Clinics and Agri-Business Centers (ACBs) by trained agricultural professionals, enabling them to deliver need-based agricultural services at the grassroots level while creating self-employment opportunities.
eligibility_criteria: Indian citizens with agricultural education qualification (minimum B.Sc. in Agriculture, Horticulture, Agricultural Engineering, Veterinary Science, Dairy Science, Fisheries, or equivalent diploma from recognized institutions); Age between 18-60 years; Willing to settle in rural or semi-rural areas; Not employed in any government or semi-government organization; Possession of relevant agricultural knowledge and entrepreneurial aptitude

description: TSP-Agri is designed to address the unique agricultural challenges faced by tribal communities across India. The scheme emphasizes sustainable agriculture, soil conservation, water management, and promotion of traditional crops suited to tribal regions. It provides financial assistance, technical support, and capacity building to tribal farmers, with special focus on women farmers and marginalized sections within tribal communities. The scheme operates through state-level implementing agencies and coordination with district administrations.
summary: The Tribal Sub-Plan Agriculture (TSP-Agri) scheme is a centrally sponsored initiative launched in 2012 to promote sustainable agricultural development in tribal areas of India. The scheme focuses on improving agricultural productivity, ensuring food security, and enhancing the socio-economic status of tribal farmers through targeted interventions and modern farming practices.
eligibility_criteria: Tribal farmers and agricultural laborers in notified tribal areas; Farmers from Scheduled Tribe communities holding agricultural land; Small and marginal farmers with landholding up to 2 hectares; Women farmers from tribal communities; Agricultural cooperatives and farmer producer organizations in tribal areas; Individual farmers or joint farmer groups engaged in agriculture or animal husbandry
target_beneficiaries: Scheduled Tribe farmers in forest-fringe and hilly tribal areas; Small and marginal farmers representing 70-80% of tribal agricultural population; Women farmers and self-help groups in tribal communities; Landless agricultural laborers from tribal areas; Youth groups interested in agriculture and agri-entrepreneurship in tribal regions; Approximately 8-12 million tribal farming households across India
benefits_provided: Financial assistance up to ₹50,000 per beneficiary for agricultural inputs and equipment; Soil health cards and testing facilities for customized fertilizer recommendations; High-yielding crop varieties suited to tribal agro-climatic conditions; Drip irrigation and water harvesting infrastructure development; Training programs on modern farming, organic cultivation, and pest management; Marketing support through direct buyer linkages and cooperative networks; Insurance coverage for agricultural risks and crop losses; Access to agricultural credit at subsidized rates (4-6% interest subsidy); Supply of quality seeds and planting materials at 50% subsidized rates; Capacity building for agro-processing and value addition
application_process: Step 1: Identify eligible tribal farmers through convergence with Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) database. Step 2: Conduct awareness camps at block and village level through NGOs and agricultural extension workers. Step 3: Form farmer groups comprising 10-25 beneficiaries with women representation (33%). Step 4: Submit consolidated application with individual beneficiary details and land records. Step 5: Technical verification by district agricultural officer and revenue department. Step 6: Appraisal and approval by District Tribal Development Agency and State Level Committee. Step 7: Disburse funds via DBT within 7-10 days of approval. Step 8: Monitor implementation through quarterly field visits and performance reviews.
documents_required: Aadhaar card or voter ID for identification; Scheduled Tribe caste certificate issued by competent authority; Land ownership documents (pattas, revenue records, or tenancy certificates); Bank account details (passbook copy); Passport-sized photographs (2-3); Application form (prescribed format by state agricultural department); Soil test report (if available); For groups: list of members with individual documents and group registration certificate
keywords: tribal agriculture, sub-plan, TSP-Agri, tribal farmers, agricultural development, tribal areas, sustainable farming, crop productivity, food security, soil conservation, water management, tribal welfare, marginalized farmers, agro-techniques, indigenous crops, agricultural subsidies, tribal empowerment, rural development

description: The Women Farmer Empowerment scheme is designed as a comprehensive initiative addressing the multifaceted challenges faced by women engaged in agriculture and allied sectors. The scheme operates on the principle of inclusive development by integrating women farmers into mainstream agricultural systems. It provides financial assistance, technical support, and institutional capacity building to facilitate women's participation in agricultural decision-making and income generation. The scheme covers various agricultural activities including crop production, horticulture, animal husbandry, dairy farming, fisheries, and agro-processing, with special emphasis on sustainable and organic farming practices.
summary: Women Farmer Empowerment (WFE) is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme launched in 2016 to enhance the socio-economic status of women farmers in India through targeted interventions in agriculture, allied activities, and skill development. The scheme recognizes women's critical role in agricultural production and aims to provide them with equal access to resources, technology, and market opportunities.
eligibility_criteria: Women farmers aged 18-60 years with at least 1 hectare of agricultural land or actively engaged in agriculture/allied activities; Women Self-Help Groups (SHGs) and Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs); Widows and divorced women engaged in agricultural activities; Women from SC/ST/OBC communities receive priority; Beneficiaries must possess valid Aadhaar and bank account; Preference given to women farmers from drought-prone, hilly, and tribal regions
target_beneficiaries: Direct beneficiaries: 5 million individual women farmers; Indirect beneficiaries: Women in SHGs, FPOs, and cooperative societies; Priority groups: SC/ST women farmers (30%), OBC women farmers (27%), General category women farmers (43%); Geographic focus: All states with emphasis on agriculture-dependent rural areas
benefits_provided: Direct income support of Rs. 6,000-15,000 per acre per annum based on state variations; Subsidy coverage of 50-80% on agricultural machinery and equipment; Financial assistance for establishing agro-processing units (up to Rs. 5 lakhs); Capacity building and skill development training programs; Free certification for organic farming conversion (3-year support); Priority access to agricultural credit through bank linkage with interest subvention of 2-3%; Construction of water harvesting structures and irrigation facilities at 90% subsidy; Health and accident insurance coverage (premium contribution by government); Market linkage support and e-NAM registration assistance
application_process: Step 1: Registration through Common Service Centers (CSCs) or district agriculture office; Step 2: Submission of eligibility documents with filled application form (available online on district portal); Step 3: Verification by revenue official and agriculture department at village/block level; Step 4: Selection of beneficiaries through committee screening (prioritizes economically weaker sections); Step 5: Approval from District Level Nodal Agency; Step 6: Disbursal through Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) to registered bank account; Step 7: On-site verification before final fund release; Processing timeline: 45-60 days from application submission
documents_required: Valid identity proof (Aadhaar, Voter ID, Driving License, Passport); Agricultural land ownership documents or lease agreement; Bank account details with IFSC code; Caste certificate (for SC/ST/OBC applicants); Income certificate from Gram Panchayat or Taluka office; Photograph (4 copies, passport size); Declaration of crop pattern and farming practices; Mobile number for communications; Land measurement document (Pattas/Lease deed); Self-certification of agricultural engagement; Proof of membership in SHG/FPO (if applicable)
keywords: women empowerment, agriculture, skill development, financial inclusion, cooperative societies, gender equality, sustainable farming, livelihood enhancement, agricultural technology, socio-economic development

description: The Small and Marginal Farmers Scheme is a comprehensive government initiative designed to address the specific needs of small and marginal farmers who constitute the backbone of Indian agriculture. Launched under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, the scheme provides integrated support through multiple interventions including soil health management, improved seed distribution, crop insurance coverage, and capacity building programs. The scheme recognizes the unique challenges faced by small farmers including limited access to credit, lack of modern technology adoption, and vulnerability to market fluctuations. Through coordinated implementation with state governments and agricultural extension services, SMFS delivers evidence-based solutions that enhance farm productivity while promoting environmental sustainability.
summary: The Small and Marginal Farmers Scheme (SMFS) is a Centrally Sponsored initiative launched in 2015 to support and empower small and marginal farmers by providing access to modern agricultural technologies, improved credit facilities, and agricultural education. The scheme aims to increase agricultural productivity and ensure sustainable income growth for vulnerable farmer populations.
eligibility_criteria: Farmers with operational landholding up to 2 hectares (small farmers) and up to 1 hectare (marginal farmers) as per landholding records. Ownership proof of cultivated land (pattas/revenue records). Annual household income not exceeding ₹5 lakhs from all sources. Farmers not availing similar benefits under other central schemes simultaneously. Must be engaged in agricultural activities for minimum 6 months in the last financial year. SC/ST farmers and women farmers given preference in selection.
target_beneficiaries: Small and Marginal Farmers, Agricultural Labourers transitioning to farming, Women Farmers, SC/ST Farming Communities, Tenant Farmers with documented cultivation rights, Farmers in drought-prone and hilly regions
benefits_provided: Direct subsidy of 40-50% on improved agricultural tools and equipment. Certified quality seeds at 50% subsidized rates. Crop insurance premium subsidy covering up to 70% of premium cost. Soil health cards with customized nutrient management plans. Free capacity building and skill development training (minimum 3 days annually). Linkage to agricultural credit with 2% interest subvention for 3 years. Market intelligence and price information services via SMS/mobile app. Organic farming conversion support with ₹20,000-30,000 per hectare over 3 years. Drip irrigation system subsidies up to 60% of cost. Free veterinary services and livestock advisory for integrated farming.
application_process: 1) Registration: Visit https://pmksy.gov.in or contact State Agriculture Department. 2) Document Submission: Submit required documents to block-level agricultural office. 3) Verification: Revenue and agricultural staff verify landholding and eligibility (15-20 days). 4) Selection: Beneficiaries selected through transparent process and notified via SMS. 5) Bank Linkage: Farmer's bank account linked to PMKSY database. 6) Subsidy Disbursement: Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) to beneficiary accounts in installments. 7) Training: Enrolled in mandatory capacity building programs. 8) Monitoring: Annual review and performance tracking through agricultural extension.
documents_required: Aadhar Card and PAN Card (if available). Land ownership/cultivation documents (patta, lease agreement, revenue records). Bank account passbook (SECC registered preferred). Caste certificate (for SC/ST beneficiaries). Income certificate from revenue authority. Passport size photographs (2 copies). Self-declaration form confirming income and landholding details. Certificate of engagement in agriculture from Gram Panchayat/Revenue Official.
keywords: Small Farmers, Marginal Farmers, Agricultural Subsidy, Farmer Welfare, Crop Insurance, Soil Health, Agricultural Productivity, Centrally Sponsored Scheme, Ministry of Agriculture, PMKSY, Kisan, Farmer Income, Rural Development, Sustainable Agriculture, Agricultural Technology

description: The Young Farmer Scheme (YFS) is a central sector scheme launched by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare in 2020 to promote agricultural entrepreneurship and capacity building among youth in India. The scheme aims to encourage young individuals to adopt modern farming techniques, establish agricultural startups, and develop agribusiness ventures. It focuses on providing comprehensive training, financial support, and mentorship to create a sustainable agricultural ecosystem driven by youth participation and innovation.
summary: A capacity building initiative designed to empower youth between 18-35 years to establish themselves as progressive farmers and agribusiness entrepreneurs through integrated training, financial incentives, and market linkage support.
eligibility_criteria:
1. Indian citizens aged 18-35 years
2. Formal qualification in agriculture or related field (preferred but not mandatory)
3. Willingness to engage in farming or agribusiness for minimum 5 years
4. No existing agricultural business or substantial farming experience
5. Agricultural land availability (owned or leased) or space for agribusiness setup
6. Resident of India for minimum 3 years preceding application
7. Not currently employed in government/semi-government institutions
target_beneficiaries: Young entrepreneurs and aspirants aged 18-35 years seeking to establish careers in agriculture or agribusiness, including school/college graduates, skill development trainees, and individuals from farming communities interested in modernizing agricultural practices.

1. Comprehensive training programs (120-150 hours) covering modern farming techniques, crop management, soil health, and organic farming
2. Financial assistance up to ₹5 lakhs for establishing agricultural enterprises or purchasing farm equipment
3. Mentorship support from experienced farmers and agricultural experts for 2 years
4. Access to subsidized agricultural inputs and quality seeds
5. Market linkage and value chain integration support
6. Farm mechanization subsidies up to 40% of equipment cost
7. Exposure visits to successful farms and agribusiness centers
8. Business plan development assistance
9. Digital literacy and e-marketing training
10. Insurance coverage for crops and livestock at reduced premiums

2. Complete online application form with personal, educational, and land details
3. Upload required supporting documents in prescribed format

description: SC Sub-Plan Agriculture (SCSP-Agri) is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme launched by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare in 2012. The scheme aims to ensure socio-economic development of Scheduled Caste (SC) farmer communities through targeted agricultural interventions. It focuses on improving agricultural productivity, income generation, skill development, and access to modern farming technologies for SC farmers who constitute a significant portion of India's farming population. The scheme operates on the principle of inclusive growth and aims to bridge the development gap faced by SC agricultural communities.
summary: SCSP-Agri is a flagship centrally sponsored agricultural development scheme dedicated to uplifting Scheduled Caste farmers through enhanced agricultural productivity, technology transfer, skill development, and improved market access. Launched in 2012, it operates across multiple states with assistance from both central and state governments.
eligibility_criteria:
1. Farmers belonging to Scheduled Caste communities as per constitutional definitions
2. Agricultural landholders with minimum 0.5 hectares of cultivable land
3. Farmers engaged in crop production, horticulture, or allied agricultural activities
4. Annual household income not exceeding Rs. 3.5 lakh from all sources
5. Not beneficiary of similar schemes in the last 5 years
6. Permanent resident of the state/district for minimum 3 years
7. Age between 18-65 years for capacity building programs
8. Individual farmers and farmer groups/Self Help Groups (SHGs) are eligible
target_beneficiaries: Direct beneficiaries include SC farmers (estimated 15-20 million farmers) across agricultural zones. Indirect beneficiaries include SC agricultural laborers, landless SC families, rural SC youth seeking agricultural entrepreneurship, SC women farmers, and SC farming communities in economically backward regions. The scheme prioritizes women farmers (minimum 30% of beneficiaries), farmers in drought-prone areas, and marginal/small holding farmers.
benefits_provided:
1. Direct Income Support: Rs. 4,000-6,000 per hectare annually for crop production
2. Technology Transfer: Free supply of improved seeds, high-quality fertilizers, and modern farming equipment at subsidized rates (50-70% subsidy)
3. Irrigation Support: 80% financial assistance for drip irrigation, sprinkler systems, and water management infrastructure
4. Crop Insurance: 100% premium subsidy under Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) for SC farmers
5. Skill Development: Free training on organic farming, sustainable agriculture, water conservation, and soil health management
6. Market Linkage: 40% subsidy for establishing farm producer organizations and market access centers
7. Mechanization Support: 70% subsidy on agricultural machinery (tractors, threshers, harvesters) for individual farmers; up to 80% for SC farmer groups
8. Credit Facilities: Subsidized interest rates (2-3% per annum) for agricultural loans up to Rs. 1 lakh
9. Livestock Integration: 50% subsidy for dairy, poultry, and animal husbandry units
10. Infrastructure Development: Community wells, water tanks, and storage facilities construction with 90% grant support
application_process:
Step 1: Obtain application form from Block Agriculture Office or download from official State Agricultural Department website
Step 2: Provide SC community certificate (as per constitutional definitions)
Step 3: Submit land revenue record documents (Jamabandi/Patta/FMB extracts) or lease agreement
Step 4: Complete application with required personal, agricultural, and financial details
Step 5: Submit application to Block-level SCSP-Agri Coordinator before March 31st (annual deadline)
Step 6: Verification of SC status and land holdings by District Agriculture Officer
Step 7: Approval by District-level SCSP-Agri Monitoring Committee (typically 45-60 days)
Step 8: Beneficiary selection through transparent lottery/merit-based criteria
Step 9: Account opening assistance for direct benefit transfer (DBT)
Step 10: Fund transfer to beneficiary bank account; beneficiary must complete baseline survey and monitoring updates
documents_required:
1. SC Community Certificate (issued by District Magistrate/Tehsildar)
2. Aadhar Card or voter ID (photocopy)
3. Bank account passbook (first page with IFSC code)
4. Land ownership proof: Jamabandi, Patta, FMB, or mutation record
5. For leased land: Notarized lease agreement (minimum 5 years validity)
6. Income certificate (from last financial year)
7. Passport-size photographs (2 copies)
8. Ration card or residential proof (electricity bill, water bill, or rent agreement)
9. For farmer groups: Registration certificate and all member details with SC certificates
10. Agricultural crop details and previous crop production records (if available)
11. Signed declaration regarding scheme benefits received in previous 5 years
keywords: SC farmers, Scheduled Caste agriculture, social inclusion, agricultural development, income support, crop insurance, soil health, mechanization subsidy, farmer producer organizations, sustainable farming, skill training, water management, organic farming, precision agriculture, farmer welfare, centrally sponsored scheme, inclusive growth, rural development, agricultural productivity, technology transfer

description: The Landless Labourer Support scheme is a comprehensive social security program implemented across all states and union territories. It provides targeted support to agricultural laborers who lack land ownership and are engaged in seasonal or casual farming work. The scheme encompasses multiple components including direct income support, health insurance coverage, skill training programs, and livelihood diversification initiatives. The program is implemented through district-level administration with coordination from state agricultural departments and labor welfare boards.
summary: The Landless Labourer Support (LLS) scheme is a centrally sponsored social security initiative designed to provide financial assistance, skill development, and livelihood support to landless agricultural workers across India. Launched in 2018, the scheme aims to reduce poverty and improve the socioeconomic status of vulnerable agricultural laborers by providing direct cash transfers, health insurance, and occupational training.
eligibility_criteria:
1. Must be engaged in agricultural labor or farm-related activities
2. Household annual income should not exceed Rs. 1.5 lakhs
3. Should be registered with state labor departments or agricultural labor boards
4. Minimum 2 years of continuous agricultural labor engagement
5. Age group: 18-65 years for primary beneficiaries
6. No land ownership or possess less than 0.5 hectares of land
7. Not covered under any other central scheme like MGNREGA or PM-Kisan for land holdings
8. Must be Indian citizen residing in rural or semi-rural areas
9. Valid Aadhaar number and bank account mandatory
target_beneficiaries: Agricultural laborers, farm workers, seasonal farm workers, tenant farmers, agricultural wage earners, rural poor engaged in farming activities, SC/ST agricultural workers, women agricultural laborers, youth seeking agricultural employment, elderly farmers without land ownership. Primary focus on states with significant agricultural labor population including Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Odisha, West Bengal, and Tamil Nadu.
benefits_provided:
1. Direct Income Support: Rs. 6,000 per annum in two installments of Rs. 3,000 each
2. Crop Insurance Coverage: Premium subsidy for Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) - 75% premium covered by government
3. Health Insurance: Ayushman Bharat coverage for family members (up to 5 members per household)
4. Skill Development: Free training in improved agricultural practices, horticulture, dairy farming, and fisheries
5. Tool Kit Support: Rs. 5,000 one-time assistance for purchasing basic agricultural tools
6. Occupational Training: Vocational skill training with Rs. 2,000 monthly stipend during training period (3-6 months)
7. Pension Scheme: Rs. 200 per month for eligible beneficiaries above 60 years
8. Women Empowerment: Additional Rs. 1,000 annual benefit for women farmers
9. Accident Insurance: Coverage up to Rs. 2 lakhs for workplace accidents
10. Debt Relief: One-time write-off of agricultural loans up to Rs. 25,000 for eligible beneficiaries
application_process:
1. Registration: Applicants register with local Gram Panchayat or Block Development Office with required documents
2. Document Submission: Submit Aadhaar, bank account details, income certificate, and labor registration proof
3. Verification: Gram Panchayat verifies documents and beneficiary eligibility within 15 days
4. Online Portal: Enter details on state's agricultural labor portal (e-governance platform)
5. Approval: District Agriculture Officer and District Labor Commissioner cross-verify details
6. Bank Account Linking: Ensure beneficiary bank account is linked with Aadhaar
7. Benefit Disbursement: Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) to beneficiary's bank account
8. Timeline: Complete process from application to first disbursement takes 30-45 days
9. Renewal: Annual renewal required between April-June by submission of updated income certificate
10. Grievance Redressal: Complaints can be filed through toll-free helpline (1800-180-1551) or state portal
documents_required:
1. Aadhaar Card (original and photocopy)
2. Bank Account details (savings account passbook or bank statement)
3. Income Certificate issued by Gram Panchayat or Tahsildar
4. Agricultural labor registration certificate or labor card
5. Occupational Certificate from local authorities
6. Land ownership proof or no-land certificate
7. Crop Insurance eligibility certificate (if applicable)
8. Health insurance enrollment document
9. Voter ID or PAN Card (for identification)
10. Caste Certificate (if applying for SC/ST category benefits)
11. Widow/Divorcee certificate (if applicable)
12. Disability certificate (if applicable)
keywords: Landless Labourer Support, agricultural labor, social security, farm workers, income support, skill training, health insurance, direct benefit transfer, rural livelihood, farmer welfare, agricultural employment, poverty alleviation, crop insurance, Ayushman Bharat, occupational training, agricultural development, labor rights, rural development, government scheme, centrally sponsored, 2018, agricultural income, vulnerable workers, livelihood security

description: The Agricultural Education Scheme is a comprehensive initiative designed to modernize agricultural education institutions and improve the quality of teaching and research in agricultural sciences. The scheme focuses on strengthening State Agricultural Universities (SAUs), Central Agricultural Universities (CAUs), and Deemed Universities offering agricultural education. It provides financial assistance for infrastructure development, capacity building of faculty, introduction of modern curriculum, and promotion of research in emerging agricultural challenges such as climate change, soil health, water management, and organic farming. The scheme operates through a collaborative framework between the central government and state governments, ensuring equitable access to quality agricultural education across all regions.
summary: The Agricultural Education Scheme (AES) is a Central Sector scheme launched by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare in 2010 to strengthen and develop agricultural education infrastructure across India. The scheme aims to produce skilled agricultural professionals and promote quality research in agricultural sciences to enhance farm productivity and sustainability.
eligibility_criteria: Eligibility criteria include: (1) Agricultural Universities and institutions recognized by the Ministry of Education offering agriculture-related degree programs; (2) Faculty members pursuing higher agricultural studies and research; (3) State Agricultural Universities recognized under the Atmanirbhar Bharat initiative; (4) Institutions implementing curriculum aligned with National Curriculum Framework for Agricultural Education; (5) Students pursuing undergraduate and postgraduate courses in agricultural sciences; (6) Research institutions focused on solving agricultural problems of national and regional importance
target_beneficiaries: Primary beneficiaries include: (1) Faculty members in agricultural universities seeking professional development and research grants; (2) Students enrolled in bachelor's and master's degree programs in agricultural sciences; (3) Research scholars pursuing PhD in agriculture and allied sectors; (4) Agricultural universities and colleges seeking infrastructure upgradation; (5) Farming communities benefiting from research outputs; (6) Women pursuing agricultural education; (7) Backward and SC/ST students in agricultural higher education
benefits_provided: Key benefits of the scheme include: (1) Financial assistance for infrastructure development including laboratories, greenhouses, and teaching facilities; (2) Grants for faculty capacity building and international exposure through training programs; (3) Support for curriculum modernization incorporating latest agricultural techniques and sustainability practices; (4) Research grants for solving pressing agricultural challenges; (5) Scholarships and fellowship programs for meritorious students; (6) Equipment and technology upgradation for agricultural universities; (7) Support for collaborative research programs between Indian and international institutions; (8) Promotion of skill-based agricultural education aligned with market demands
application_process: The application process involves multiple stages: (1) Institutions identify project proposals aligned with scheme objectives; (2) Submit comprehensive project proposal to State Agricultural Department/Ministry of Agriculture; (3) Proposal evaluation by Technical Assessment Committee comprising experts; (4) Sanction of funds through administrative approval; (5) Fund release in tranches based on progress and milestone achievement; (6) Regular monitoring and compliance reporting by implementing institution; (7) Third-party evaluation and impact assessment; (8) Annual reviews and performance-based fund allocation. Proposals can be submitted during specified windows typically announced twice annually.
documents_required: Required documents for application and implementation include: (1) Institutional registration certificate and recognition document from Ministry of Education; (2) Detailed project proposal with objectives, methodology, and expected outcomes; (3) Budget estimate and financial justification; (4) Infrastructure assessment report and implementation plan; (5) Faculty qualification certificates and research credentials; (6) Details of existing facilities and resource availability; (7) Affiliation certificate from respective state/central university; (8) Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) for collaborative projects; (9) Environmental clearance for agricultural research facilities; (10) Audited financial statements of the institution
keywords: Agricultural Education, Central Sector Scheme, Agricultural Universities, Skill Development, Research Grants, Infrastructure Development, Faculty Training, Agricultural Sciences, Curriculum Modernization, Higher Education, Farmer Welfare, Agricultural Research, Innovation, Sustainable Agriculture, Agricultural Technology

description: The Cold Chain Development Scheme (CCDS) is a Centrally Sponsored infrastructure initiative designed to strengthen cold storage and cold chain facilities across India's agricultural sector. Launched in 2012, the scheme aims to reduce post-harvest losses of perishable agricultural commodities including fruits, vegetables, flowers, meat, poultry, fish, and dairy products. The scheme facilitates the development of critical cold chain infrastructure including cold storage facilities, ripening chambers, pre-cooling units, insulated transport containers, and temperature-controlled transportation vehicles. By establishing integrated cold chain networks from farm to consumer, CCDS enables farmers to access better markets, reduce product wastage, and improve the quality and shelf-life of perishable produce.
summary: Centrally Sponsored infrastructure scheme promoting cold storage and cold chain development to reduce post-harvest losses and improve agricultural commodity value chain efficiency across India.
eligibility_criteria: Eligible beneficiaries include Central/State Government agencies, Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs), Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs), Agricultural Cooperative Societies, Agricultural and Horticultural Development Boards, private sector companies, NGOs, and individual entrepreneurs engaged in cold chain infrastructure development. Applicants must demonstrate technical feasibility, financial viability, and commitment to operational sustainability of cold storage facilities for a minimum of 10 years.
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers, farmer cooperatives and FPOs, producers of perishable commodities (fruits, vegetables, flowers, meat, poultry, fish, dairy), agricultural traders, food processing units, exporters, and consumers through reduced post-harvest losses and improved product availability.
benefits_provided: Central financial assistance of up to 50% of project cost (maximum ₹2 crore for cold storage projects), subsidized loans with interest subvention of 2% for 10 years, capacity building and technical training programs, access to modern cold chain technology, improved product preservation and shelf-life, reduced post-harvest losses (target: 20-30% reduction), better price realization for farmers, market linkage opportunities, quality certification support, and employment generation in rural areas.
application_process: Step 1: Obtain application form from Department of Agriculture & Cooperation or download from official CCDS portal. Step 2: Prepare detailed DPR with cost estimates and technical specifications. Step 3: Submit completed application with supporting documents to State Agricultural Department nodal officer. Step 4: Preliminary scrutiny and eligibility verification by State authority. Step 5: Technical and financial appraisal by empowered committee. Step 6: In-principle approval and communication of subsidy terms. Step 7: Sanction order issuance with conditions. Step 8: Site verification and project implementation within stipulated timeline. Step 9: Progress monitoring through monthly reports and site visits. Step 10: Final inspection and subsidy release upon commissioning of facility.
documents_required: Project Detailed Report (DPR) with technical specifications, architectural drawings and land documents, financial projections and viability report, evidence of land ownership/lease agreement (minimum 20 years), NOC from environmental/local authorities, registration certificate and incorporation documents, qualification certificates of technical staff, bankability report from financial institution, bank account details and authorization letters, proof of financial capacity, feasibility study for handling capacity and commodity throughput, operation and maintenance plan, and compliance certificates.
keywords: cold chain, cold storage, post-harvest losses, perishable commodities, agricultural infrastructure, government subsidy, cold storage facility, farmer producer organization, temperature control, agricultural development, food security, rural infrastructure, centrally sponsored scheme, farmers welfare

description: The Farmer Training Programme (FTP) is a comprehensive skill development initiative launched by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare in 2008. It is designed to enhance the technical knowledge and practical skills of farmers through structured training in modern agricultural practices, crop management, soil conservation, water management, and allied agricultural activities. The programme aims to bridge the gap between traditional farming methods and contemporary agricultural techniques, thereby improving farm productivity and sustainability.
summary: The Farmer Training Programme is a pioneering centrally-sponsored scheme that transforms agricultural practices through systematic skill development. Since 2008, it has trained hundreds of thousands of farmers across India in modern, sustainable, and science-based agriculture. The program leverages Krishi Vigyan Kendras, agricultural universities, and government extension infrastructure to deliver contextualized training. By combining theoretical knowledge with practical demonstrations and exposure visits, FTP enables farmers to adopt improved crop varieties, scientific soil management, efficient water use, and integrated pest management. The programme particularly benefits small-holder farmers and women, contributing to increased productivity, income security, and agricultural sustainability across India's diverse agro-climatic zones.
eligibility_criteria:
1. Indian citizens engaged in agricultural activities
2. Active farmers with minimum 0.5 hectare of agricultural land or engaged in allied agriculture
3. Age group: 18-65 years preferred
4. No formal educational qualification required; basic literacy preferred
5. Members of farmer groups, cooperatives, or self-help groups given priority
6. Young farmers (18-35 years) especially encouraged
7. Women farmers and marginalized farming communities eligible for targeted schemes
8. Tenant farmers and agricultural laborers seeking skill enhancement
target_beneficiaries: Primary: Small and marginal farmers, tenant farmers, and landless agricultural laborers. Secondary: Young entrepreneurs interested in agriculture, women farmers, farmers from SC/ST communities, farming communities in rainfed and tribal areas, cooperative members, and members of farmer producer organizations (FPOs)
benefits_provided:
1. Free technical training in improved farming techniques and modern agricultural practices
2. Certification upon successful completion of training modules
3. Exposure visits to agricultural research stations and demonstration farms
4. Hands-on experience with mechanized equipment and tools
5. Knowledge on sustainable agriculture and organic farming methods
6. Access to training materials and extension literature at no cost
7. Networking opportunities with other farmers and agricultural experts
8. Enhanced market linkage information and value addition techniques
9. Training in soil testing, water management, and pest management
10. Stipend/support provided during residential training programs (when applicable)
11. Links to government subsidies and credit schemes
12. Improved income generation capacity through productivity enhancement
application_process:
Step 1: Identify the relevant training module offered by the Department of Agriculture & Cooperation in your state/district
Step 2: Register through the state agricultural department website or visit the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or State Agricultural University extension center
Step 3: Fill the application form with required personal and agricultural information
Step 4: Submit documents (as listed above) along with completed application
Step 5: Verification of eligibility by district agriculture office within 7-10 days
Step 6: Selection and notification of selected candidates
Step 7: Attend orientation/induction session
Step 8: Participate in structured training programs (duration varies from 3 days to 6 months depending on module)
Step 9: Pass assessment/evaluation tests
Step 10: Receive certificate and establish links with extension services for post-training support
documents_required:
1. Proof of Indian citizenship (Aadhaar, Voter ID, PAN, or Passport)
2. Land ownership certificate or lease deed (for land-based eligibility)
3. Recent identity proof with photograph
4. Proof of agricultural engagement (land records, cooperative membership certificate, or self-declaration)
5. Bank account details for scholarship/stipend disbursement (if applicable)
6. Mobile number and email address for communication
7. Age proof document
8. Income certificate from relevant authority (for BPL/priority group classification)
9. Caste certificate (if SC/ST/OBC for priority benefits)
10. Self-declaration form signed by applicant
keywords: skill development, farmer training, agricultural practices, capacity building, crop management, extension services, technical knowledge, farm productivity

description: The Warehouse Development Scheme is implemented by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare in conjunction with the National Cooperative Union of India (NCUI) and Warehousing Development and Regulatory Authority (WDRA). The scheme focuses on developing modern warehousing infrastructure compliant with international standards to facilitate better preservation of agricultural produce. It covers construction of warehouses with scientific storage facilities, climate-controlled chambers, and modern material handling equipment. The scheme encourages participation from cooperative societies, farmer organizations, FPOs, and private sector entities to create a robust storage network.
summary: The Warehouse Development Scheme (WDS) is a Central Sector scheme launched in 2007 to promote scientific storage infrastructure development in India. It aims to reduce post-harvest losses of agricultural commodities through establishment of modern warehousing facilities and standardized storage infrastructure across agricultural regions.
eligibility_criteria: Eligible applicants include: (1) Central/State Government agencies; (2) Registered cooperative societies (agricultural, dairy, fisheries); (3) Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) registered under NCUI or Farmers' Producer Company Act; (4) Private corporate entities with minimum net worth of Rs. 10 crores; (5) Self Help Groups (SHGs); (6) Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) engaged in agricultural sector. Applicants must have minimum land of 2 acres for warehouse construction and must follow WDRA guidelines and standards.
target_beneficiaries: Primary beneficiaries: Farmers, farmer cooperatives, agricultural produce marketing committees (APMCs), FPOs, and agricultural storage operators. Secondary beneficiaries: Rural communities, agro-processors, food manufacturers, exporters, and traders. The scheme particularly benefits small and marginal farmers in regions with significant post-harvest losses.
benefits_provided: 1. Capital subsidy: 25% of eligible project cost (up to Rs. 1 crore) for cooperative societies and government agencies; 20% for private sector entities. 2. Reduced post-harvest losses: Modern storage reduces commodity losses from 15-20% to 5-10%. 3. Better price realization: Improved storage enables farmers to store and sell produce during peak prices. 4. Employment generation: Direct and indirect employment through warehouse operations and maintenance. 5. Standardized grading and sorting facilities: Infrastructure for quality improvement and value addition. 6. Concessional credit: Financial institutions provide loans at preferential rates to WDRA-registered warehouses. 7. Technical support: Government provides training and capacity building for warehouse management and scientific storage practices.
application_process: Step 1: Submit complete application with DPR to designated State Agricultural Department/NCUI office. Step 2: Department reviews eligibility and completeness of documentation within 30 days. Step 3: Technical committee evaluates DPR and site verification (15-20 days). Step 4: Approval by State/Central authority (15 days). Step 5: Applicant completes warehouse construction as per approved plans (typically 12-24 months). Step 6: Submission of completion certificate and third-party inspection. Step 7: Verification of subsidy eligibility criteria. Step 8: Subsidy disbursement in installments: 40% on completion of foundation, 35% on structural completion, 25% on final commissioning. Entire process typically takes 18-30 months from application to subsidy disbursement.
documents_required: 1. Application form (prescribed format); 2. Detailed project report (DPR) with design and cost estimation; 3. Land ownership/lease documents (minimum 5-year lease); 4. Architectural drawings and building plans approved by competent authority; 5. Environmental clearance certificate (where applicable); 6. Registration certificate of applicant organization; 7. Bank account details and authorization; 8. Proof of financial capacity/balance sheet; 9. Detailed feasibility report; 10. WDRA compliance certificate; 11. Warehouse design specifications as per BIS standards; 12. Safety and security plan; 13. Operations and maintenance manual; 14. Board resolution (for cooperative/corporate entities).
keywords: Warehouse Development Scheme, WDS, agricultural storage, warehousing infrastructure, post-harvest management, commodity storage, cooperative societies, FPO, capital subsidy, WDRA, scientific storage, agricultural infrastructure, storage capacity, farmer benefit, government scheme

description: The Pack House Scheme provides financial assistance to entrepreneurs, farmer groups, cooperatives, and agribusiness companies to construct and establish modern pack houses equipped with grading, washing, sorting, and packaging machinery. Pack houses serve as critical infrastructure nodes in the agricultural value chain, enabling farmers to process and package produce according to quality standards and market requirements. The scheme covers states across India with focus on major horticultural production regions.
summary: The Pack House Scheme (PHS) is a Centrally Sponsored infrastructure development initiative launched in 2015 to establish modern post-harvest handling and packaging infrastructure for horticultural produce across India. The scheme aims to reduce post-harvest losses, enhance product quality, and facilitate better market linkages for farmers through professional packaging infrastructure.
eligibility_criteria: Eligible applicants include registered farmer producer organizations, agricultural cooperatives, self-help groups with minimum 25 members, agribusiness entrepreneurs, private companies involved in agricultural produce handling, and state/central agricultural development corporations. Applicants must have minimum land/infrastructure space of 500 sq.m., technical competency in post-harvest management, financial capacity to contribute matching funds (20-30% depending on beneficiary category), and commitment to ensure farmer participation and benefit sharing.
target_beneficiaries: Primary beneficiaries are farmers engaged in horticulture (fruits, vegetables, flowers), farmer producer organizations (FPOs), agricultural cooperatives, self-help groups in rural areas, and small-to-medium agricultural entrepreneurs. Secondary beneficiaries include allied workers in packaging and processing sectors, transporters, and retail market intermediaries. Geographic focus includes major horticultural zones in states like Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Jammu & Kashmir, Maharashtra, Gujarat, Karnataka, and Northeastern states.
benefits_provided: Financial assistance of up to Rs. 50-60 lakhs per pack house unit (depending on capacity and location), covering 60-70% of eligible project cost with farmers/groups contributing 30-40% match. Additional benefits include subsidy on machinery installation, technical support for capacity building, certification assistance, access to national-level market linkages, reduced post-harvest losses (from average 20% to below 5%), improved product quality and shelf-life, premium pricing opportunities, and income enhancement for participating farmers.
application_process: Application process involves: (1) Obtaining notification from state agricultural department/implementing agency, (2) Preparing detailed project report with technical specifications, (3) Submitting application through online portal or directly to district/state level implementing agency with all required documents, (4) Technical evaluation by expert committee assessing project viability and farmer benefit potential, (5) Site inspection and recommendation by state officials, (6) Approval by State Level Sanctioning Committee, (7) Release of first installment (70-80%) post foundation stone and project commencement, (8) Final inspection and release of remaining funds after project completion and commissioning.
documents_required: Land ownership/lease documents (minimum 500 sq.m.), registration certificates of farmer groups/cooperatives, technical feasibility report prepared by accredited consultants, detailed project report (DPR) with cost breakup, Aadhar and bank account details of applicants, partnership agreements (if applicable), proof of technical competency or training, environmental clearance certificates (where applicable), state government recommendation letter, and audited financial statements (for organizations with annual turnover above Rs. 10 lakhs).
keywords: Pack House Scheme, post-harvest management, horticultural infrastructure, packaging facility, farmer producer organization, agricultural value chain, quality standardization, market linkage, post-harvest loss reduction, agribusiness, grading and sorting, subsidy scheme, cooperative society, rural infrastructure development

description: The Mobile Processing Units scheme aims to facilitate decentralized food processing by providing mobile equipment and infrastructure that can be transported to agricultural production areas. MPUs are equipped with modern machinery for processing various agricultural commodities including fruits, vegetables, spices, grains, and meat products.
