The units operate as per food safety standards and enable small farmer groups and FPOs (Farmer Producer Organizations) to establish processing operations without significant capital investment in permanent infrastructure.
summary: The Mobile Processing Units (MPU) scheme is a Central Sector initiative launched in 2019 to establish mobile food processing facilities across rural India. These units operate on wheels and can be deployed to agricultural clusters, farmer groups, and APMC markets to process agricultural commodities at source, reducing post-harvest losses and adding value to farmers' produce.
eligibility_criteria: Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs), Agricultural Cooperative Societies, Self Help Groups (SHGs) engaged in agricultural production, NGOs working with farming communities, Individual farmers having minimum 2 hectares of cultivable land, APMC market committees, State/Central agricultural departments, Food processing entrepreneurs with prior experience.
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers, Farmer Producer Organizations, Agricultural cooperatives, Women self-help groups involved in agriculture, Agricultural entrepreneurs, Rural communities in agricultural clusters, APMC market associations, Primary processing centers in rural areas.
benefits_provided: Mobile Processing Units reduce post-harvest losses by 20-25%, Enable value addition at farm gate, Reduce transportation costs for farmers, Provide employment generation in rural areas (8-10 jobs per unit), Improve quality and shelf life of agricultural products, Facilitate compliance with food safety standards (FSSAI), Support export-quality production, Increase farmer income by 30-40%, Enable collective processing through farmer groups, Reduce supply chain intermediaries.
application_process: Step 1: Farmer groups or FPOs prepare a detailed project proposal with technical specifications and business plan. Step 2: Beneficiary organization registers with concerned State Agricultural Department or Ministry of Food Processing Industries portal. Step 3: Online application submission through official portal with all required documents and DPR. Step 4: Technical evaluation by State/Central agency team regarding feasibility and conformity. Step 5: Site verification and inspection of proposed processing location by government officials. Step 6: Approval of project and sanction letter issued by Ministry/State authority. Step 7: Financial assistance disbursed in installments upon milestones. Step 8: Procurement of MPU equipment as per approved specifications. Step 9: Installation, testing, and FSSAI compliance certification. Step 10: Training and capacity building for operators and entrepreneurs. Step 11: Commencement of operations and monitoring.
documents_required: Registration certificate of FPO/Cooperative Society/SHG, Certificate of Incorporation or registration proof, Bank account details of the beneficiary organization, Detailed Project Report (DPR) with processing plan, FSSAI Food License application or provisional approval, Land ownership/lease agreement for processing area, List of farmer members and their landholdings, Letter of intent from beneficiary organization, Audited financial statements (for cooperatives/FPOs), Technical specifications of MPU equipment, Training completion certificate for operators, Environmental clearance certificate (if applicable), Insurance policy documents, GST registration certificate.
keywords: Mobile Processing Units, MPU scheme, decentralized processing, rural agro-processing, farmer producer organizations, post-harvest management, value addition, food processing, FSSAI compliance, agro-entrepreneurship, farmer income enhancement.

description: The Export Oriented Agriculture (EOA) scheme is a Central Sector initiative launched in 2015 to promote and strengthen India's agricultural export sector. The scheme focuses on enhancing the competitiveness of Indian agricultural products in global markets by providing financial and technical support to farmers, farmer groups, and agro-enterprises. Through this scheme, the government aims to increase agricultural exports, generate employment, and ensure sustainable agricultural practices. The EOA scheme covers the entire value chain from production to export, including processing, packaging, certification, and market linkages.
summary: Central Sector scheme launched in 2015 to boost India's agri-export sector through financial assistance, capacity building, and market linkages for farmers and agro-enterprises engaged in export-oriented production.
eligibility_criteria: Farmers, Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs), cooperative societies, agro-enterprises, exporters, and other stakeholders involved in export-oriented agriculture. Minimum land holding of 0.5 hectares for individual farmers. FPOs should have minimum 50 members. Enterprises should be registered and compliant with export standards and food safety regulations.
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers, farmer groups, women farmers, Scheduled Caste/Scheduled Tribe farmers, agro-enterprises, agricultural exporters, agricultural processors, cold chain operators, and horticulture producers engaged in export-oriented production.
benefits_provided: Subsidy for setting up export-oriented production infrastructure (25-50% of project cost). Financial assistance for certification (ISO, GlobalGAP, Organic) up to Rs. 1 lakh per beneficiary. Support for cold chain and storage facilities (40% of project cost). Training and capacity building programs for producers and exporters. Market linkage and trade promotion support. Technology adoption incentives. Export promotion grants for participation in international trade fairs.
application_process: 1. Obtain application form from district agriculture department or download from official website. 2. Fill form with required details and supporting documents. 3. Submit to district-level committee with project report and cost estimates. 4. Verification and assessment by technical committee. 5. Approval by district/state authority. 6. Disbursement of subsidy in installments upon project completion. 7. Post-project monitoring and evaluation.
documents_required: Aadhaar card, land ownership/lease documents, bank account details, project report with cost estimates, revenue records, photographs of existing farm/enterprise, export permission from APEDA if applicable, ISO/certification documents (if already obtained), FPO registration certificate (for farmer groups), business registration documents (for enterprises), bank statement for last 2 years, technical specifications of proposed infrastructure.
keywords: export oriented agriculture, agri-export, agricultural exports, farmer support, agricultural subsidy, export promotion, farmer producer organization, certification support, cold chain, market linkage, agricultural infrastructure, global competitiveness, sustainable agriculture, crop production

description: The Contract Farming Scheme is a state sector agricultural initiative that promotes structured agreements between farmers and agricultural corporations, exporters, and other agribusiness entities. Under this scheme, farmers engage in cultivating specific crops as per predetermined contracts, which include agreed-upon prices, purchase guarantees, and technical support. The scheme operates across multiple states and is designed to bridge the gap between traditional farming practices and modern agricultural commerce.
summary: The Contract Farming Scheme (CFS) is a government initiative launched in 2018 to facilitate agreements between farmers and agricultural companies for pre-agreed prices and quality standards. This scheme aims to modernize agricultural practices, reduce market risk, and ensure guaranteed income for farmers while providing assured supply to agribusiness companies.
eligibility_criteria:
1. Small and marginal farmers with minimum 0.5 hectares of cultivable land
2. Indian citizens above 18 years of age
3. Agricultural laborers and landless farmers (in some states)
4. Farmers with valid land ownership documents or lease agreements
5. No restriction on gender participation; women farmers encouraged
6. Farmers not defaulting on previous loans
7. Registered agricultural society members eligible for collective farming
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers, agricultural laborers, women farmers, landless cultivators, cooperative societies, farmer producer organizations (FPOs), and agribusiness-engaged youth. Primary focus on farmers in rural areas with limited access to modern markets and technology.
benefits_provided:
1. Guaranteed market access and fixed price contracts
2. Reduced marketing risks and price volatility
3. Technical guidance and quality inputs provided by contracting company
4. Access to modern farming technology and improved seeds
5. Reduced dependence on intermediaries and local traders
6. Direct income assurance before harvest
7. Government support in resolving contract disputes
8. Improved farm productivity and crop quality
9. Enhanced export opportunities for quality products
10. Insurance coverage for contract farming crops
application_process:
1. Registration through state agricultural department website or common service centers
2. Identification of registered contracting companies/buyers
3. Negotiation and signing of contract agreement specifying crop, quality, price, and delivery terms
4. Submission of required documents to agricultural department
5. Verification of documents and farmer eligibility by district authorities
6. Approval and issuance of contract farming certificate
7. Orientation program on contract terms and responsibilities
8. Technical support setup with assigned extension officer
9. Monitoring and regular compliance check-ups
10. Dispute resolution through agriculture department if contract issues arise
documents_required:
1. Land ownership documents or lease agreement
2. Aadhaar Card or voter identity proof
3. Bank account details for direct benefit transfer
4. Agricultural land certificate from revenue department
5. Signed contract agreement between farmer and company
6. KYC documents (PAN optional)
7. Photographs (2x2 size)
8. Declaration of landholding area and cultivation details
9. No Objection Certificate from local panchayat (in some states)
10. Previous year harvest records (if available)
keywords: Contract Farming, Agriculture Scheme, Farm-to-Market, Price Guarantee, Agricultural Contracts, Farmer Income, Market Linkage, Agribusiness, Cooperative Farming, Government Agriculture, Rural Development, Sustainable Farming

description: The Primary Processing Centres (PPC) scheme is a Centrally Sponsored initiative designed to establish integrated primary processing facilities across agricultural regions in India. Launched in 2016, the scheme aims to reduce post-harvest losses, add value to agricultural produce, and create employment opportunities in rural areas. PPCs serve as intermediate processing units that bridge the gap between farmers and large-scale food processing industries, enabling small and marginal farmers to collectively process their produce at the primary level.
summary: A centrally sponsored food processing scheme providing financial assistance to establish primary processing centres for agricultural value addition and post-harvest management.
eligibility_criteria:
1. Non-profit organizations, NGOs, cooperatives, and farmer producer organizations registered under relevant statutes
2. Government institutions and agricultural bodies
3. Private entrepreneurs and companies with minimum 51% participation of primary producers/farmers
4. Entities with proven experience in food processing or agriculture
5. Beneficiaries must have access to adequate raw material supply
6. Minimum annual processing capacity of 500 metric tonnes
7. Land and water resources availability as per scheme norms
8. No upper limit on income for institutional entities
target_beneficiaries:
1. Small and marginal farmers (majority beneficiaries)
2. Farmer producer organizations (FPOs) and farmer collectives
3. Women farmer groups and self-help groups (SHGs)
4. Scheduled Caste and Scheduled Tribe communities engaged in agriculture
5. Landless agricultural laborers through employment opportunities
6. Youth entrepreneurs in rural areas
7. Agricultural cooperatives
8. Primary producers and agricultural entrepreneurs
benefits_provided:
1. Capital subsidy up to 40% of total project cost for general beneficiaries
2. Capital subsidy up to 50% for SC/ST and women-led organizations
3. Financial assistance covers land, building, plant & machinery, and equipment
4. Soft loans and credit facility linkage through partner banks
5. Technical assistance and training programs for operators and staff
6. Technology transfer and knowledge sharing
7. Market linkage support and branding assistance
8. Employment generation in rural areas
9. Reduction in post-harvest losses (typically 20-35% reduction)
10. Value addition and increased farmer income
11. Quality improvement and food safety compliance
12. Modern processing techniques and mechanization
application_process:
1. Preliminary Screening: Submit application through state implementing agency or centralized portal during notification period
2. Document Verification: Submission and validation of required documents
3. DPR Preparation: Detailed technical and financial project report with assistance from state coordinator if needed
4. Committee Review: Assessment by state-level screening committee based on eligibility and viability criteria
5. Site Inspection: Physical verification of location, raw material availability, and infrastructure
6. Approval: State approval followed by central ministry approval for fund allocation
7. Fund Release: Phase-wise subsidy and loan disbursement (typically 40% at sanction, 40% on construction, 20% on completion)
8. Implementation: Beneficiary executes project with technical oversight and monitoring
9. Monitoring and Evaluation: Quarterly progress reports and compliance verification
10. Post-commissioning Support: Operational assistance for first 2-3 years
documents_required:
1. Detailed Project Report (DPR) with techno-economic viability
2. Registration certificate and bylaws of the organization
3. Board resolution/management approval
4. Proof of land ownership or lease agreement (minimum 5-10 years)
5. Water availability certificate from appropriate authority
6. Agricultural production data from the region
7. Farmer linkage agreement (for farmer producer organizations)
8. Environmental clearance as per applicable rules
9. Financial statements and audited accounts (last 3 years)
10. Bank account details and PAN/GST registration
11. Farmer participation certificate and consent
12. Proof of technical expertise/project management capacity
13. Quotations from equipment suppliers
14. Site plan and architectural drawings
15. No Objection Certificate from local authorities
keywords: Primary Processing, Food Processing, Agricultural Value Addition, Post-Harvest Management, Farmer Producer Organizations, Centrally Sponsored Scheme, Rural Employment, Food Safety, Processing Infrastructure, Agricultural Cooperatives, Subsidy, Credit Linkage, Value Chain, Quality Improvement, Market Linkage

description: The Farmer Producer Company Scheme (FPCS) is a centrally sponsored institutional initiative designed to facilitate the formation and development of Farmer Producer Companies (FPCs) as viable business entities. Launched in 2014, this scheme aims to enable small and marginal farmers to collectively access modern agricultural technologies, quality inputs, and better market linkages. FPCs operate on a cooperative business model where farmers are shareholders and stakeholders, allowing them to achieve economies of scale in production, processing, and marketing of agricultural produce.
summary: FPCS is a centrally sponsored scheme that promotes the formation of Farmer Producer Companies to strengthen collective farming, enhance value addition, and improve market access for smallholder farmers across India. The scheme provides financial and technical support to establish legally registered FPCs with farmer members, enabling them to function as organized business entities for agricultural production and marketing.
eligibility_criteria: Eligible applicants include: (1) Groups of minimum 10-11 farmers (for general categories) to form a FPC; (2) Farmer groups, Self Help Groups (SHGs), and existing cooperative societies that wish to convert into FPCs; (3) Non-Government Organizations (NGOs) and agricultural societies with direct farming interest as facilitating organizations; (4) Individual farmers with farming as primary occupation; (5) Marginalized and vulnerable farmer groups are encouraged; (6) States with agriculture sector having significant potential for value addition and market integration.
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers, landless agricultural workers, women farmers, Scheduled Caste (SC) and Scheduled Tribe (ST) farmers, young farmers, agricultural laborers, farming communities in rural areas, women self-help groups engaged in agriculture, and producer groups seeking to strengthen their collective bargaining power and market presence.
benefits_provided: 1. Subsidy support of up to Rs. 25 lakh per FPC for establishing infrastructure and working capital; 2. Technical assistance for business planning, registration, governance, and operational management; 3. Training programs for farmer members and FPC management committees; 4. Direct market linkages and value chain development opportunities; 5. Access to credit facilities at reduced interest rates; 6. Collective procurement of quality seeds, fertilizers, and farm inputs at discounted rates; 7. Post-harvest management and processing facilities; 8. Quality certification and branding support; 9. Risk mitigation through collective approach; 10. Sustainable agricultural practices promotion.
application_process: 1. Farmer groups or facilitating organizations identify and mobilize eligible farmers in the target area; 2. Conduct awareness and capacity building workshops for prospective members; 3. Prepare Memorandum and Articles of Association as per Companies Act, 2013; 4. Apply for registration with the Registrar of Companies (RoC); 5. Receive registration certificate from RoC; 6. Prepare detailed business plan and project proposal with technical and financial components; 7. Submit application to District Agriculture Office or nodal agency with all required documents; 8. Application is evaluated by State-level Steering Committee for technical and financial feasibility; 9. Approved FPCs receive subsidy disbursement in phases (typically 40% on approval, 40% on infrastructure setup, 20% on completion); 10. Monitor and support implementation of the project plan; 11. Conduct performance reviews and impact assessments.
documents_required: 1. Memorandum of Association (MOA) and Articles of Association (AOA); 2. Registration certificate from the Registrar of Companies (RoC); 3. Pan card and TAN number of FPC; 4. Bank account statements and passbooks; 5. List of member farmers with land holding certificates; 6. Aadhar cards and bank account details of all members; 7. Business plan and viability report; 8. Detailed project proposal with budget estimates; 9. Supporting documents for FPC formation (minutes of formation meeting); 10. Compliance certificates from State Agriculture Department; 11. Land documents for proposed infrastructure sites; 12. NOC from local authorities if applicable.
keywords: Farmer Producer Company, FPC, institutional scheme, collective farming, farmer organization, value chain development, agricultural marketing, subsidy scheme, centrally sponsored, cooperative business model, farmer empowerment, agricultural entrepreneurship, market linkage, value addition, rural development

description: The Direct Marketing Scheme aims to strengthen the agricultural marketing infrastructure and promote direct sale of farmers' produce. The scheme encourages farmers to adopt direct marketing channels through market yards, farmer-to-consumer markets, and online platforms. It provides financial assistance to State/Union Territories for development of necessary infrastructure and capacity building at the primary level (farm gates and farmer collection centers) and secondary level (market yards and aggregation centers). The scheme focuses on reducing transaction costs, increasing farmer income, improving market efficiency, and enhancing consumer access to fresh agricultural produce.
summary: The Direct Marketing Scheme (DMS) is a Central Sector scheme launched in 2017 to promote direct marketing of agricultural produce by farmers, thereby eliminating middlemen and ensuring better prices for farmers while providing quality produce to consumers at reasonable rates.
eligibility_criteria:
1. State and Union Territory governments
2. Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) with minimum 100 members or as per state guidelines
3. Cooperative societies and agricultural marketing federations
4. Individual farmers' groups (minimum 10 farmers)
5. Non-government organizations (NGOs) working in agricultural marketing
6. Panchayat-level institutions engaged in agricultural marketing
target_beneficiaries:
1. Small and marginal farmers (primary focus)
2. Farmer Producer Organizations and their member farmers
3. Agricultural cooperative societies
4. Consumers seeking fresh and quality agricultural produce at reasonable prices
5. Rural and semi-urban populations
6. Women farmers and farming communities
benefits_provided:
1. Financial assistance for infrastructure development (up to 90% of project cost for priority sectors)
2. Support for agricultural market yards, collection centers, and storage facilities
3. Funding for quality assurance mechanisms and packaging infrastructure
4. Capacity building and training programs for farmers in modern marketing practices
5. Digital platform support for direct online marketing and e-commerce
6. Improved farm-gate prices by eliminating middlemen (typically 20-40% price increase)
7. Access to market information and price intelligence systems
8. Cold chain infrastructure development
9. Transportation and logistics support
10. Post-harvest management infrastructure assistance
application_process:
1. Notification and Awareness: State/UT announces the scheme and invites applications from eligible entities
2. Online Registration: Applicants register on the MMIS (Meri Fasal Mera Byora) or state-designated portal
3. Application Submission: Complete application with required documents, project proposal, and cost estimates submitted online/offline
4. Verification: District-level committees verify applications and conduct field inspections
5. Project Appraisal: Technical committee appraises feasibility and alignment with scheme objectives
6. Approval: State government issues approval and sanction letter
7. Fund Disbursement: First installment (50-60%) released upon approval and project mobilization
8. Implementation: Project execution as per approved plan with monitoring
9. Final Inspection: Completion verification and final fund release (40-50%) upon achieving milestones
10. Compliance and Reporting: Regular performance monitoring and submission of periodic reports
documents_required:
1. Registration certificate of FPO/cooperative/organization
2. Proof of membership (for FPOs and farmer groups)
3. Land ownership documents or lease deeds
4. Project proposal with detailed cost estimation and technical specifications
5. Audio-visual documents of the proposed project site
6. Farmers' identity proof (Aadhaar/PAN) and bank account details
7. No-objection certificate from local municipal/revenue authorities
8. Environmental clearance (if applicable for facilities above certain capacity)
9. Audited financial statements (for organizations with previous experience)
10. Detailed project report including market survey and demand assessment
keywords: Direct Marketing Scheme, DMS, agricultural marketing, farmers' direct sales, farm-to-consumer, elimination of middlemen, farmer producer organizations, market infrastructure, agricultural value chain, rural economic development, farmer income, direct sale channels, e-commerce for agriculture, cold chain, post-harvest management

description: The Climate Resilient Agriculture scheme aims to support farmers in adopting climate-adaptive agricultural practices to mitigate the adverse impacts of climate change on agricultural productivity. Through a combination of research, technology dissemination, and farmer capacity building, the scheme promotes sustainable land management, water conservation, crop diversification, and resilience-building measures. The scheme operates through Krishi Vigyan Kendras (KVKs), State Agricultural Departments, and NGOs to implement field demonstrations, farmer training programs, and technology adoption at grassroots level.
summary: Climate Resilient Agriculture (CRA) is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme launched in 2011 to promote sustainable agricultural practices that enhance farmers' resilience to climate variability and change. The scheme focuses on building climate-smart farming systems across diverse agro-ecological zones in India.
eligibility_criteria: Small and marginal farmers, landless agricultural laborers, and farming communities in climate-vulnerable regions; Farmers willing to adopt climate-resilient practices; Communities in drought-prone, flood-prone, and hill regions; Farmers engaged in agriculture, horticulture, and allied activities; Preference given to SC/ST communities and women farmers; Minimum land holding varies by state (generally 0.5 hectares or more for farmers); Farmers not availing benefits under other conflicting government schemes
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers (primary focus); Women farmers and SHGs; Landless agricultural laborers; Farming communities in climate-vulnerable zones; SC/ST communities; Youth in agriculture; Approximately 5 million farmers targeted across climate-sensitive regions of India
benefits_provided: Free/subsidized climate-resilient seed varieties and planting materials; Drip and sprinkler irrigation systems with 50-80% subsidy; Training on climate-smart farming practices; Demonstration of water conservation and soil management techniques; Support for crop diversification and alternate livelihood options; Agroforestry and wasteland development support; Weather-based crop insurance premium support; Access to climate information services; Subsidy for soil and water conservation structures; Support for renewable energy adoption (solar pumps, biogas); Farm ponds and check dams construction; Capacity building and farmer field schools; Certification support for organic farming
application_process: Step 1: Registration at nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or block agriculture office; Step 2: Verification of farmer eligibility and land holdings; Step 3: Submission of application with required documents; Step 4: Assessment of farm condition and climate vulnerability; Step 5: Enrollment in beneficiary database; Step 6: Selection for demonstration activities or training programs; Step 7: Disbursement of assistance through direct bank transfer; Step 8: Monitoring and technical support during implementation; Step 9: Post-adoption follow-up for sustainability; Grievance redressal available at block and district level
documents_required: Aadhaar card and address proof; Agricultural certificate/tenant certificate; Khasra/Khatauni (land ownership documents); Bank account details (for direct benefit transfer); SC/ST certificate (if applicable); Woman farmer certificate (if applicable); Photo identity proof (Voter ID/Driving License/PAN); Agriculture Income Certificate; Latest tax paid receipt; No Objection Certificate from landowner (for tenant farmers); Land survey map/diagram; Soil health card (where available)
keywords: Climate change adaptation, Sustainable agriculture, Water conservation, Crop resilience, Soil health, Farmer training, Climate-smart farming, Irrigation efficiency, Agroforestry, Agricultural sustainability, Weather-based services, Drought management, Climate vulnerability, Agro-ecological zones, Farmer empowerment

description: The Drought Mitigation Programme (DMP) is a comprehensive Centrally Sponsored Scheme launched in 2009 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to address the recurring challenges of drought across India. The scheme focuses on building resilience in drought-prone areas through integrated water resource management, soil conservation, afforestation, and livelihood diversification. It aims to reduce the vulnerability of farming communities through structural and non-structural interventions that enhance ground water availability and promote sustainable agricultural practices. The programme operates through participatory approaches involving farmers, local communities, and state governments to ensure sustainable drought management and improved food security in vulnerable regions.
summary: Drought Mitigation Programme is a centrally sponsored risk management scheme addressing drought vulnerability in India through integrated watershed management, water harvesting, soil conservation, and livelihood support, benefiting over 2.5 million farming families across 15 drought-prone states.
eligibility_criteria: States/districts identified as drought-prone or experiencing recurring water scarcity; Farming communities with landholdings affected by irregular rainfall and drought conditions; Communities practicing rainfed agriculture; Vulnerable groups including marginal farmers, small and landless laborers in identified drought zones; Gram Panchayats and community-based organizations willing to participate in collaborative resource management.
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers (primarily); Agricultural laborers; Women farmers and self-help groups; Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in drought-affected areas; Rural households dependent on agriculture; Pastoral and livestock-dependent communities; Approximately 2.5 million farming families across 15 major drought-prone states including Rajasthan, Gujarat, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, and Madhya Pradesh.
benefits_provided: Subsidized or free construction of check dams, percolation tanks, and johads for water harvesting; Farm ponds and ground water recharge structures; Soil conservation measures including terracing and contour farming; Tree plantation and afforestation support; Improved agricultural tools and drought-resistant seeds; Livelihood diversification training and input support for animal husbandry, dairy, and horticulture; Direct income support during severe drought periods; Subsidized installation of drip irrigation systems; Capacity building and agricultural extension services; Reduction in groundwater depletion; Development of community assets; Employment generation through watershed development activities.
application_process: Online registration through state agricultural department portals or common service centers; Village-level awareness camps conducted by state agriculture officials; Application submission through Gram Panchayats with required documentation; Verification of eligibility by block-level officials; Stakeholder meetings for participatory planning of watershed and water conservation projects; Approval by District Committee; Implementation through designated contractors and community organizations; Progress monitoring through regular field visits and documentation; Final certification and release of funds to beneficiary accounts.
documents_required: Land ownership documents (Khasra, Khatauni); Identity proof (Aadhar Card, Voter ID, PAN); Residence certificate; Bank account details (for direct benefit transfer); Photograph (passport size); Land use certificate; Proof of enrollment in farmer database; Certificate from Gram Panchayat confirming drought-affected status; Caste certificate (if applicable); Antecedent certificate of the applicant.
keywords: drought mitigation, water harvesting, watershed management, soil conservation, groundwater recharge, rainfed agriculture, risk management, drought-prone areas, water resources, livelihood support, irrigation, agro-forestry, climate resilience, community participation, sustainable agriculture, India drought scheme

description: Flood Affected Area Support (FAAS) is a centrally sponsored scheme launched in 2010 to provide immediate and comprehensive relief to farmers and agricultural communities affected by flooding. The scheme focuses on restoring agricultural productivity, providing financial assistance for crop loss, livestock protection, and rehabilitation of flood-damaged infrastructure in vulnerable flood-prone regions across India.
summary: FAAS is a risk management initiative designed to mitigate losses and support recovery of flood-affected farming communities through timely financial aid, input subsidies, and infrastructure restoration measures.
eligibility_criteria: Farmers in notified flood-affected districts and villages with documented crop loss of at least 50% or more; landholding size unrestricted; both small and marginal farmers to large farmers eligible; areas declared as 'calamity-hit' by respective state governments; farmers with valid Aadhar or voter ID; no income ceiling restrictions; SC/ST farmers given priority for relief disbursement.
target_beneficiaries: Marginal and small farmers in flood-affected regions; landless agricultural laborers; tenant farmers; livestock owners in flood-prone areas; agricultural communities in disaster-declared zones; vulnerable farming populations in states like Bihar, Assam, Odisha, West Bengal, Uttar Pradesh, and other flood-prone regions; approximately 8-12 million farmers per flood season.
benefits_provided: Direct cash compensation for crop loss (up to 90% of actual loss); subsidized agricultural inputs (seeds, fertilizers) at 50-75% reduced cost; livestock insurance premium support (40-50% subsidy); emergency veterinary care for affected animals; interest-free crop loans up to Rs. 50,000; waiver of land revenue for 1 year in severely affected areas; free provision of replacement seeds for Kharif crops; subsidized water pumps and irrigation equipment; repair grants for damaged farm structures; training programs for climate-resilient farming practices; emergency food grain assistance to landless laborers.
application_process: Applications submitted at designated Gram Panchayat offices or designated agricultural extension centers; farmers required to report loss within 15 days of flood event to village officials; Revenue Inspector conducts field assessment and prepares damage report; block-level verification committee reviews applications; approval by District Disaster Management Authority; beneficiary list published for public scrutiny (7-day objection period); payments credited directly to farmer bank accounts via NEFT/RTGS; second installment released after monsoon season ends; grievance redressal through dedicated helplines and district-level committees.
documents_required: Aadhar card or voter ID; Land ownership proof (Jamabandi, Mutation record, or lease agreement); Bank passbook or account details; FIR or official calamity declaration notice from Patwari; Crop insurance policy details (if applicable); Agricultural census data or landholder identity; Revenue inspection report documenting crop loss; Livestock ownership documentation (for animal assistance); Income certificate for priority categorization; Photos of damaged crops/fields with date and location.
keywords: Flood relief, Risk management, Crop insurance, Disaster assistance, Agricultural support, Calamity relief, Crop loss compensation, Flood-prone areas, Farmer welfare, Input subsidies, Livestock protection, Climate resilience, Agricultural loans, Centrally sponsored scheme, Disaster management, Agricultural rehabilitation

description: Agri Export Zones scheme represents a comprehensive approach to strengthening India's agricultural export competitiveness. Launched by the Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, the scheme establishes designated zones with world-class infrastructure facilities including pack houses, cold storage, processing units, testing laboratories, and logistical support. These zones facilitate compliance with international quality standards (SPS/TBT), enable economies of scale, and promote value addition through processing and packaging. The scheme covers a wide range of agricultural commodities including horticultural products, spices, cereals, pulses, cotton, and processed foods. It leverages public-private partnership models to ensure sustainable infrastructure development and operational efficiency.
summary: The Agri Export Zones (AEZ) scheme is a central sector initiative launched in 2001 to promote and facilitate agricultural exports from India. It aims to develop integrated infrastructure and support systems for export-oriented agricultural production and value addition. The scheme focuses on creating competitive export clusters by bringing together farmers, exporters, processors, and service providers in identified geographical areas.
eligibility_criteria: Eligibility criteria include: (1) Cluster of agricultural producers/exporters in a defined geographical area demonstrating significant production potential; (2) State government commitment to provide necessary support and facilitate land availability; (3) Presence of basic transport and communication infrastructure; (4) Minimum 50 hectares of cultivable land in horticultural zones or equivalent production capacity for other commodities; (5) Producer groups, farmer producer organizations (FPOs), or private exporters with track record of agricultural exports; (6) Commitment to adopt good agricultural practices (GAP) and meet international quality standards; (7) Willingness to participate in cluster-based approach and coordinate with other stakeholders.
target_beneficiaries: Primary beneficiaries include: small and marginal farmers organized in clusters or through FPOs; agricultural exporters and trading companies; agro-processors and food manufacturers; cold storage and logistics operators; agricultural input suppliers; agricultural extension service providers; rural entrepreneurs engaged in value addition; and rural communities in identified geographical clusters. Secondary beneficiaries include state agricultural departments, research institutions, and quality certification bodies.
benefits_provided: (1) Infrastructure Development: Capital investment support for establishing pack houses, cold storage facilities, processing units, testing laboratories, and logistics infrastructure within designated zones; (2) Certification Support: Assistance in obtaining international quality certifications (ISO, GlobalGAP, organic certifications, SPS compliance) and phytosanitary compliance; (3) Technology and Knowledge Transfer: Access to best practices, modern farming techniques, and value addition technologies through training and demonstrations; (4) Export Promotion: Facilitation of direct market linkages with international buyers, participation in global trade fairs, and export promotion activities; (5) Quality Assurance: Establishment of testing laboratories and quality monitoring systems to ensure compliance with international standards; (6) Financial Support: Concessional credit, subsidized interest rates, and investment support through partnering financial institutions; (7) Cluster Development: Support for cluster-level coordination, market intelligence, and collective bargaining mechanisms; (8) Employment Generation: Creation of direct and indirect employment opportunities in production, processing, storage, and export logistics.
application_process: (1) Expression of Interest (EoI): Stakeholders submit preliminary interest through state agricultural department with basic zone details; (2) Cluster Identification: Identification of suitable geographical areas with production potential and infrastructure capacity; (3) Stakeholder Consultation: Engagement with farmers, exporters, state government, and other stakeholders for consensus building; (4) Master Plan Preparation: Development of comprehensive infrastructure and development plan with cost estimates and timelines; (5) Feasibility Assessment: Third-party evaluation of technical, economic, and environmental feasibility; (6) State-level Approval: Approval by state steering committee followed by central approval from Department of Agriculture; (7) MoU Signing: Formalization of agreement between state government, zone management entity, and participating stakeholders; (8) Zone Notification: Official notification establishing the Agri Export Zone with defined boundaries; (9) Infrastructure Development: Commencement of infrastructure projects through hybrid funding model; (10) Operational Launch: Activation of zone facilities and enrollment of participants; (11) Monitoring & Evaluation: Regular assessment of performance against agreed targets.
documents_required: (1) Proposal document outlining zone boundaries, production potential, and infrastructure requirements; (2) State government support letter confirming commitment and land availability; (3) Master plan prepared by state agricultural department or recognized agency detailing infrastructure layout and development timeline; (4) Feasibility study demonstrating economic viability and export potential; (5) Land availability certificates or lease agreements for proposed infrastructure sites; (6) Organizational structure and governance framework for zone management; (7) Environmental clearance and land use certification from appropriate authorities; (8) Letters of intent from exporters/producers confirming participation and investment commitment; (9) Financial projections and bankability assessment; (10) Project implementation schedule with timeline and cost estimates; (11) Certificates of agricultural production potential from district agricultural authorities; (12) Environmental impact assessment report.
keywords: agricultural exports, export clusters, agricultural infrastructure, cold chain, value addition, food processing, international quality standards, farmer producer organizations, agri-export zones, horticultural exports, spices export, commodity trading, pack houses, certification, SPS compliance, rural development, export promotion, agricultural competitiveness, market linkages, sustainable farming

description: The Crop Diversification Programme is designed to encourage farmers to transition from traditional monoculture practices to diversified farming systems. The scheme focuses on regions with high groundwater extraction, low rainfall, or environmental stress. It promotes cultivation of pulses, oilseeds, spices, vegetables, fruits, and other high-value crops while maintaining soil health and ensuring better economic returns. The programme integrates climate-smart agriculture with resource conservation techniques.
summary: The Crop Diversification Programme (CDP) is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme launched in 2013 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to promote sustainable agricultural practices through crop diversification. The scheme aims to reduce farmer dependence on water-intensive crops and shift towards high-value, nutritious, and sustainable alternatives in water-stressed regions.
eligibility_criteria: Farmers in identified water-stressed and groundwater-depleted districts; Land holding size: minimum 0.5 hectares preferred; Farmers engaged in cereal crop cultivation (wheat, rice, sugarcane); Age: 18 years and above; Priority given to small and marginal farmers; SC/ST farmers receive additional consideration; Farmers should be willing to adopt crop diversification for minimum 3 years
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers (primary focus); Agricultural laborers transitioning to farming; Women farmers groups; Farmers in water-scarce regions (Eastern Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana, UP, MP); Approximately 5 lakh farmers targeted annually across diversification zones
benefits_provided: Crop diversification assistance: 50% subsidy on certified seeds of pulses, oilseeds, and vegetables; Input subsidies for soil amendment and organic fertilizers (40% support); Farm mechanization support: 40-50% subsidy on equipment; Irrigation infrastructure development: 80% grant for drip/sprinkler systems; Income support: Guaranteed price support through NITI Aayog; Training and capacity building programs; Market linkage facilitation; Risk coverage through Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) integration
application_process: Online registration through rkvy.nic.in portal; District Agriculture Office verification; Farm inspection and baseline survey; Selection and enrollment; Subsidy disbursement in phases (40% on enrollment, 40% on crop establishment, 20% on completion); Monitoring through agro-forestry camps and farmer field schools
documents_required: Valid Aadhaar Card; Land ownership certificate (Jamabandi/Patta/Lease deed); Passbook (first and last page) or cancelled cheque for bank account; Census OBC/SC/ST certificate (if applicable); Farmer ID or SRADHAN registration; Photograph (2 recent passport-size); Loan eligibility certificate from bank (if availing credit); Residential proof
keywords: Crop diversification, sustainable agriculture, pulses cultivation, high-value crops, water conservation, soil health, farmer income, climate-smart agriculture, groundwater management, market linkage, agri-entrepreneurship, nutritional security, RKVY-RAFTAAR

description: The Drone Technology for Agriculture (DTA) scheme is a Central Sector initiative launched in 2022 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to promote the use of drone technology in agricultural operations. The scheme aims to enhance agricultural productivity, reduce operational costs, and encourage farmers to adopt modern precision farming techniques through subsidized drone services and training programs.
summary: A government-backed technology scheme providing financial support for drone adoption in agriculture, covering spraying, mapping, and monitoring operations with subsidies up to 40% for farmers and 25% for custom hiring service providers.
eligibility_criteria: Farmer groups, agricultural cooperatives, FPOs (Farmer Producer Organizations), custom hiring service providers, and agri-entrepreneurs with minimum 5 acres of operational land. Individual farmers, registered organizations with agricultural focus, entrepreneurs with relevant qualifications, and self-help groups engaged in agricultural activities. State agricultural departments and recognized agricultural institutions also eligible for participation.
target_beneficiaries: Marginal and small farmers, agricultural laborers, farmer producer organizations, women farmer collectives, custom hiring service providers, agricultural startups, rural youth, cooperative societies, and agricultural input dealers. Priority given to SC/ST communities, women entrepreneurs, and farmers from rainfed and hilly regions.
benefits_provided: Subsidy of 40% for individual farmers and 25% for custom hiring service providers on drone purchase. Free training and certification in drone operation and maintenance. Insurance coverage for drone equipment. Technical support and after-sales service from authorized dealers. Access to digital platform for drone hiring and service booking. Reduced pesticide usage by 30-40%. Operational cost reduction of 20-25%. Coverage of 40-50 acres per day per drone. Real-time crop monitoring and data analytics. Soil health and moisture mapping capabilities.
application_process: Step 1: Register on the official DTA portal or contact the district agriculture office. Step 2: Fill the application form with required personal and land details. Step 3: Submit supporting documents and verification by agriculture officer. Step 4: Bank verification and financial assessment. Step 5: Approval from state nodal agency. Step 6: Selection of authorized drone dealer. Step 7: Subsidy amount released post-purchase as per installments. Step 8: Drone delivery and on-site training completion. Step 9: Registration on digital platform and issuance of operational certificate.
documents_required: Aadhar Card and PAN, Land ownership or lease documents with proof of operational area, Bank account details (IFSC code), Passport-size photographs, Mobile number and email ID, Caste certificate (if applicable for SC/ST category), Income certificate or proof of farmer status, Cooperative/FPO registration certificate (if applying as organization), Educational qualifications and experience certificate for entrepreneurs, Declaration form signed by applicant, No Objection Certificate from concerned gram panchayat.
keywords: Drone technology, Precision agriculture, Agricultural drones, Drone subsidy, Spray drones, Aerial mapping, Crop monitoring, Agriculture 4.0, Custom hiring, Agri-tech, Remote sensing, Digital farming, Unmanned aerial vehicles, Drone certification, Smart farming

description: The Digital Crop Survey scheme leverages cutting-edge technologies including satellite imagery, drone surveys, artificial intelligence, and mobile applications to conduct real-time crop assessments across all agricultural regions in India. This scheme aims to replace traditional manual surveys with automated digital solutions, enabling accurate crop estimation, yield prediction, and health monitoring. The system provides farmers and government agencies with timely data on crop conditions, pest infestations, and resource requirements. The platform integrates with existing agricultural databases and provides decision-support systems for crop management and policy formulation.
summary: Digital Crop Survey (DCS) is a comprehensive central sector scheme launched in 2023 by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare to revolutionize crop assessment and monitoring in India through advanced digital technologies and remote sensing capabilities.
eligibility_criteria: All states and union territories can participate; Farmers with land records in government databases; Minimum farm size: 0.5 hectare; No upper limit on holding size; Agro-climatic zones: All zones covered; Tech-enabled blocks as priority in Phase 1; No income restrictions for eligibility
target_beneficiaries: Small and marginal farmers; Medium and large-scale farmers; State agricultural departments; District administration; Agricultural extension officers; Agribusiness enterprises; Agricultural research institutions; Insurance companies for crop insurance assessment
benefits_provided: Real-time crop health monitoring and early pest/disease detection; Accurate crop yield estimation and forecasting; Reduced assessment time from months to weeks; Digital records for crop insurance and subsidy claims; Improved water and fertilizer management through precision agriculture recommendations; Risk mitigation through early warning systems; Enhanced agricultural policy-making based on accurate data; Increased productivity through data-driven farming practices; Integration with PM Fasal Bima Yojana for quick claim settlement
application_process: Register on DCS web portal (dcs.agriculture.gov.in); Download mobile app 'Krishi Sukhiyan'; Upload land documents and farm details; Complete GPS-based farm mapping; Enroll in survey notification system; Receive SMS/app alerts for survey schedules; Cooperate with digital survey teams during designated survey period; Verify and download digital crop assessment reports; File claims and avail benefits through integrated portal
documents_required: Land ownership proof or registered lease documents; Unique ID (Aadhar/Voter ID); Mobile number for digital access; Email ID for portal registration; GPS coordinates of farm location; Crop details from previous season; Bank account details for digital payments; In case of tenant farmers: written consent from landowner
